The two rezidentury in any given capital sometimes competed for the same sources, sometimes shared information when operational coordination was more efficient, and consistently maintained institutional rivalry that reflected the broader KGB-GRU relationship at Centre. In the photographs from Soviet military attaché offices and receptions — officers in Red Army uniform at diplomatic functions, military attachés in uniform at host-country defense ministry briefings — the GRU's collection function is not visible. The uniform was the cover.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
KGB Active Measures Against SDI — The Star Wars Campaign
KGB Service A / First Chief Directorate · worldwide / Western Europe / United States · 1983–1991

Reagan's March 1983 announcement of the Strategic Defense Initiative — a program to develop technology capable of intercepting Soviet ballistic missiles — was the single most alarming development in Soviet strategic planning of the 1980s. If SDI achieved its stated goal, it would negate the Soviet Union's most expensive and politically central strategic capability: the intercontinental ballistic missile force that was the basis of the entire Soviet deterrent posture and the rationale for the RVSN. The KGB's response, managed through Service A (active measures), was among the most sustained and resource-intensive dezinformatsiya campaigns of the late Cold War. The campaign's objectives were: to persuade Western European publics and legislatures that SDI was destabilizing; to support the scientific community's objections to SDI on technical grounds; to amplify American congressional opposition; to promote the argument that SDI was a violation of the Anti-Ballistic Missile Treaty; and to sustain the SDI debate within the American scientific community by supporting research critical of the program through cutout channels. The specific operations included: placement of critical technical analyses in Soviet-influenced academic publications; contacts with American and European scientists opposed to SDI research; support for organizations like the Union of Concerned Scientists whose published critiques of SDI paralleled Soviet strategic interests; and propaganda material produced by Service A and distributed through front organizations in Western Europe. Gorbachev's insistence at Reykjavik in October 1986 that any nuclear deal would require confining SDI to laboratory research — the condition that caused the summit to collapse — was the diplomatic expression of the same strategic concern the KGB active measures campaign had been pursuing through covert means. The campaign did not stop SDI, which was congressionally funded throughout the 1980s. It contributed to the political environment in which SDI remained controversial in Western Europe and in American academic and arms control circles, complicating NATO's management of alliance politics around the program.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Gen. Valentin Varennikov — The Only One Who Demanded Trial
Commander of Soviet Ground Forces / GKCHP · Moscow; Foros, Crimea · 1989–1994

Valentin Varennikov was Commander in Chief of Soviet Ground Forces from 1989, one of the most senior military officers in the Soviet Union — a career soldier who had served since the Great Patriotic War and who occupied a position in the hard-liner camp that believed Gorbachev's reforms were dismantling everything the Soviet military had bled to build and defend. On the evening of August 18, 1991, he was part of the delegation that flew to Gorbachev's dacha at Foros in Crimea to present the General Secretary with an ultimatum: either endorse the State of Emergency or resign. Gorbachev refused both. The coup proceeded without him; Varennikov returned to Moscow as one of the senior military figures associated with the GKCHP. The coup lasted seventy-two hours. Soviet military units declined to assault the Russian White House. Yanayev appeared on television with shaking hands. Kryuchkov and the others were arrested. In 1992, all of the GKCHP defendants were offered prosecution under the same legal framework. In 1994, the Russian State Duma passed a general amnesty covering the coup participants, and all of the defendants accepted it — the amnesty was granted without any admission of guilt and without trial, and accepting it was the straightforward thing to do. Varennikov refused. He was the only GKCHP defendant who rejected the amnesty and demanded that his case proceed to trial, because he was prepared to stand before a Russian court and defend the proposition that what he had done in August 1991 had been right. The Russian Military College of the Supreme Court agreed with him: in August 1994, he was acquitted, the court finding that his actions had been motivated by genuine concern for the state and its citizens and could not constitute criminal conduct in the circumstances of 1991. The acquittal was received with satisfaction by Russian nationalist opinion. Varennikov served as a Duma deputy, remained a vocal advocate of the position that the coup attempt had been a patriotic and legitimate act, and died in 2009. He was the only person legally exonerated for the GKCHP, and he was exonerated because he insisted on the opportunity.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Marshal Sergei Akhromeyev — The Last Soviet Military
Chief of the Soviet General Staff; Personal Military Advisor to Gorbachev · Moscow · 1984–1991

Sergei Akhromeyev became Chief of the Soviet General Staff in September 1984, succeeding Nikolai Ogarkov, and served until 1988 — a four-year tenure that spanned the most significant arms control negotiations of the Cold War. He accompanied Gorbachev to Geneva in 1985, to Reykjavik in 1986, and to Washington for the INF Treaty signing in 1987, serving as the senior military representative in negotiations that required a general officer who understood both the strategic balance and the technical specifications of what was being traded away. His role at Reykjavik was critical: when Gorbachev and Reagan came within reach of a comprehensive nuclear arms reduction agreement, Akhromeyev was the military voice in the Soviet delegation who could assess whether the proposed elimination schedules and verification arrangements were operationally acceptable to the Soviet military. He signed the INF Treaty in his official capacity, watched Soviet SS-20s being destroyed under American observation, and continued as Gorbachev's personal military advisor after leaving the General Staff. He was the last generation of Soviet military leadership formed by World War II — the Great Patriotic War remained his operational and moral reference point throughout his career, shaping his understanding of what the Soviet military existed to prevent. He opposed German reunification and the dissolution of the Warsaw Pact, which he considered the dismantling of what the Soviet Union had bled to create. On August 19, 1991, he went to the Kremlin to support the coup. On August 24, 1991 — two days after the coup collapsed — he was found dead in his Kremlin office. He had hanged himself, leaving several notes. One read: "Everything I have worked for is being destroyed." In his official military photographs — a marshal in dress uniform with the medals of a lifetime's Soviet service, composed and precise in the manner of his generation's senior officers — he looks exactly like what he had been: the Soviet Union's military, embodied.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
The Reykjavik Summit — October 1986
Reagan Administration / Gorbachev Soviet government · Höfði House, Reykjavik, Iceland · October 11–12, 1986

Reagan and Gorbachev met in Reykjavik on October 11–12, 1986, nominally for a preparatory session intended to set the agenda for a later formal summit. Over two days, the conversation moved beyond what either delegation had been briefed to expect. By Sunday afternoon they had discussed the elimination of all intermediate-range missiles, the elimination of all ballistic missiles within ten years, and — in some versions of the record — the elimination of all nuclear weapons. The CIA's pre-summit assessment of Soviet negotiating intentions had not predicted this range of movement. The talks collapsed Sunday evening when Reagan refused to accept Gorbachev's condition that the Strategic Defense Initiative be confined to laboratory research. Both leaders left Iceland through the same door in front of waiting cameras, and both appeared to have failed. The allied reaction was disarray: the British and German governments had not been consulted about the sweeping proposals their ally had nearly agreed to. Arms control experts argued about what had almost happened and what it meant. Fourteen months later, the Intermediate-Range Nuclear Forces Treaty — the first agreement to eliminate an entire class of nuclear weapons, not merely to cap them — was signed in Washington. Reagan's willingness to consider the scope of what Gorbachev proposed had proven, in retrospect, to have been the catalyst rather than the collapse. The intelligence agencies that briefed both leaders in advance had provided detailed analyses of their adversary's likely positions and constraints. The briefings were well-researched and substantially wrong. What the two leaders were willing to consider in a room together turned out to be outside the envelope that professional assessments had predicted. In the Reykjavik photographs — two men in overcoats at the door of a white house on a gray Icelandic October afternoon, shaking hands before going inside — they look like what they were: heads of state conducting the familiar rituals of diplomacy. The conversation inside was not familiar. They almost ended the nuclear era in an afternoon in Iceland. Then they didn't. Then, in the years that followed, they mostly did.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Andrei Sakharov — The Nuclear Conscience in Exile
Soviet Academy of Sciences / KGB surveillance · Gorky (now Nizhny Novgorod) · exiled January 22, 1980; released December 22, 1986

Andrei Dmitrievich Sakharov designed the Soviet hydrogen bomb. As the chief theoretical physicist of Arzamas-16 in the early 1950s, he developed the core concepts of the Soviet thermonuclear weapon design — the multilayer fusion-fission geometry that produced the Soviet Union's first true thermonuclear test in 1955, with a yield of 1.6 megatons. He became a full academician at thirty-two, the youngest in Soviet history, and received the Order of Lenin three times. He spent the 1960s becoming, with increasing public visibility, a critic of Soviet nuclear testing, nuclear proliferation, and the political system that had built the weapons he designed. His 1968 essay "Reflections on Progress, Peaceful Coexistence, and Intellectual Freedom," circulated as samizdat and published in the West, called for democratic reforms and convergence of Western and Soviet societies. The KGB removed him from weapons work. In 1975 he was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize; the Soviet government refused to let him travel to Oslo. His wife Elena Bonner accepted in his place. In January 1980, after Sakharov publicly condemned the Soviet invasion of Afghanistan, he was arrested — not through a formal criminal process, which would have required evidence and proceedings — but by administrative decree: stripped of his Soviet honors and exiled to Gorky, a closed city where no foreigners were permitted, where he lived under continuous KGB surveillance in a two-room apartment on Gagarin Avenue. He conducted three hunger strikes in protest of specific injustices; each time, Soviet authorities force-fed him in a hospital and returned him to his apartment. In December 1986, Mikhail Gorbachev telephoned his Gorky apartment personally. The call lasted a few minutes. Gorbachev said he could return to Moscow. Sakharov returned, resumed his academic and political work, was elected to the Congress of People's Deputies in 1989, and used the platform to continue his advocacy for the democratic reforms that Gorbachev's own party was resisting. He died of a heart attack in his Moscow apartment on December 14, 1989 — six months before the formal dissolution of the system he had spent two decades opposing. In the photographs from his Gorky exile — a heavyset man in his sixties in a modest Soviet apartment, reading, writing, surviving the surveillance environment — he looks like a retired scientist living quietly in a provincial city. He was. He was also the most inconvenient person in the Soviet Union, which is why the KGB had put him there.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Mathias Rust — Red Square, May 28, 1987
Soviet Air Defense Forces (PVO Strany) · Vantaa, Finland → Moscow · May 28, 1987

Mathias Rust was a nineteen-year-old West German amateur pilot with approximately 50 hours of flight time. On May 28, 1987 — Soviet Border Guard Day — he filed a flight plan for Helsinki to Stockholm, flew instead southeast from Vantaa Airport across the Gulf of Finland, entered Soviet airspace, and flew 900 kilometers through the most comprehensively defended airspace in the world to land a rented Cessna 172 on the Bolshoy Moskvoretsky Bridge next to Red Square, 500 meters from the Kremlin wall. Soviet air defense forces detected him at multiple points during the flight. On each occasion the engagement authorization failed: his aircraft's radar signature was too small for automatic engagement protocols; at one point it had been misclassified in the same zone as an arriving IL-62 commercial airliner; the chain of command at each level had declined to authorize an engagement against what appeared to be an unarmed civil aircraft without clear hostile intent. The result was that a West German teenager with a rental aircraft and a navigation device had just demonstrated that a 900-kilometer flight from Finnish territory to the center of Moscow was possible without being interdicted. He taxied to a stop at 7:10 p.m., climbed out, and was surrounded by astonished bystanders who took photographs and asked for autographs before the KGB arrived. Gorbachev's political use of the incident was immediate and decisive. He convened a Politburo session within 48 hours and used the air defense failure to dismiss Defense Minister Sergei Sokolov, Commander-in-Chief of Air Defense Alexander Koldunov, and over 150 senior military officers — the largest purge of Soviet military leadership since Stalin, and one that removed the primary institutional conservative opposition within the military to the INF Treaty negotiations then at their critical stage. Rust was sentenced to four years in a Soviet corrective labor camp; he was released after fourteen months. His aircraft is in a German museum. The gap in Soviet air defense through which he flew was not a gap in sensors — Soviet radar had tracked him — but a gap in command authority: the institutional failure to act on what the surveillance apparatus had detected. The problem was not what the system could see. The problem was what the system was permitted to do about it.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
CIA Directorate of Operations — The Casey Expansion
CIA Directorate of Operations · Langley, Virginia; worldwide · 1981–1987

When William Casey arrived at CIA in January 1981, he inherited an organization that Stansfield Turner had restructured around technical collection and from which Turner had removed approximately 820 clandestine service positions in 1977. The Directorate of Operations — the clandestine service — was demoralized, understaffed, and operating in a post-Church Committee legal environment that case officers described as producing institutional reluctance to take operational risks. Casey's seven years as DCI reversed this in every respect. The DO expanded significantly in personnel; the number of case officers and paramilitary officers grew substantially through the early 1980s. Covert action funding increased dramatically: the Afghan covert support program (Operation CYCLONE) grew from approximately $30 million per year in 1981 to over $500 million per year by 1986-1987; the Angola program resumed after the Clark Amendment's 1985 repeal; the Nicaragua program expanded before the Boland Amendment forced its formal suspension; the anti-communist support programs in Cambodia, Ethiopia, and elsewhere received new or expanded funding. Casey managed the DO with a personal involvement in operations that went beyond previous DCIs: he traveled to meet station chiefs, visited operational theaters, and drove specific recruitment operations and technical collection programs with energy that subordinates found both inspiring and concerning. His relationship with the DO's institutional culture was occasionally difficult — career professionals who had spent twenty years learning tradecraft were sometimes unsettled by a DCI who wanted results at a pace that didn't always allow proper tradecraft — but the institutional expansion he drove was real. The assessment of his DO expansion is mixed: the growth in paramilitary operations produced the Afghan program's military success and the Iran-Contra program's institutional damage; the human intelligence recruitment results were less uniformly positive, with both significant successes (Gordievsky, Tolkachev) and failures (Howard, whose access was lost before Casey's tenure ended). In the DO's offices and training programs of the mid-1980s — the expanded classes at the Farm, the new case officers being deployed worldwide — the expansion is visible as a deliberate attempt to rebuild what Turner had dismantled. Whether the rebuilding matched the strategic requirements of the period was an assessment that the subsequent intelligence failures of the late 1980s and early 1990s would measure.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Soviet Military's Afghanistan Assessment — Strategic Learning
Soviet General Staff / Soviet Army / Military Academy · Moscow; Kabul; 40th Army headquarters · 1980–1991

The Soviet military's institutional assessment of the Afghanistan campaign — what it taught, what it cost, what it proved about Soviet doctrine and equipment — was a classified process that Western intelligence agencies monitored closely, not because the tactical lessons were directly transferable but because the political implications of the Soviets' own assessment would shape future Soviet military behavior. The General Staff's after-action analysis of the Afghanistan conflict was partially visible through defector testimony, through open Soviet military publications that discussed lessons in unusually candid terms given the political sensitivity of the campaign, and through the observable organizational changes the Soviet military made in response to its Afghanistan experience. The broad assessment that emerged from these sources: the 40th Army had faced a counterinsurgency environment for which Soviet doctrine was almost entirely unprepared, and it had adapted, imperfectly but substantially, over the nine years of the campaign. Soviet armor was vulnerable in mountain terrain; the BMP and BTR infantry fighting vehicles that were the backbone of Soviet mechanized forces were effective in the plains battles they had been designed for and disastrous in the mountain ambushes the Mujahideen preferred. Helicopter operations were the central tactical adaptation: the Mi-8 and Mi-24 Hind provided mobility and firepower that compensated for the terrain disadvantages of mechanized forces. The Stinger's introduction in 1986 was the single most significant intelligence and military development of the campaign — not because it prevented Soviet operations but because it forced tactical adaptation (higher altitude operations, reduced loiter time, changed approach profiles) that reduced the effectiveness of the helicopter operations that had been the primary Soviet tactical advantage. The political assessment — what the campaign demonstrated about the limits of Soviet military power as an instrument of policy — was more contested within the General Staff. Advocates of the engagement argued it had succeeded in preventing Afghanistan from becoming a U.S. strategic base and had demonstrated Soviet resolve; opponents, led by figures like Ogarkov, had questioned the strategic rationale from the beginning and saw the outcome as confirmation that military power could not resolve political problems in societies it didn't understand. In the General Staff analyses, the lessons are visible as the kind of institutional self-examination that any military does after a difficult campaign: what we learned, what we got wrong, what we would do differently. The political question — whether the campaign should have been fought at all — appears between the lines.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
The Polygraph in American Intelligence
CIA / NSA / DIA Security Offices · Langley; Fort Meade; Bolling AFB · Cold War institutional policy

The polygraph — the device that measures respiration, blood pressure, and galvanic skin response during questioning, on the theory that deceptive responses produce detectable physiological indicators — became the primary security screening and counterintelligence tool of the American intelligence community in the 1950s and never left. By the Cold War's peak it was used routinely for initial clearance screening of all CIA, NSA, and DIA personnel, for periodic reinvestigation, and as an investigative tool in specific counterintelligence cases. Its institutional status rested on a belief in its reliability that the scientific literature did not consistently support: studies on polygraph accuracy showed rates ranging from 60 to 90 percent, depending on methodology and subject population, and the device consistently produced both false positives — innocent subjects assessed as deceptive — and false negatives — guilty subjects assessed as truthful. The false negative rate was the national security problem. Aldrich Ames passed a polygraph examination in 1986, a year after he had begun betraying CIA's Soviet sources to the KGB, and passed another in 1991. The 1986 examination was conducted during his counterintelligence center assignment, a period in which he had already committed multiple acts of espionage and received substantial KGB payments. He passed. He later described preparing for the examination by following KGB advice: get a good night's sleep, be cooperative, be calm. The CIA's Inspector General's investigation of the Ames case found that the polygraph examination had contributed to management's failure to pursue Ames as a suspect — cleared by the box, cleared in the office's institutional assessment. Robert Hanssen at the FBI was not polygraphed for the first two decades of his espionage career. The post-Ames reform debates produced the National Counterintelligence Center and enhanced financial disclosure requirements; they did not produce the replacement of the polygraph with a more reliable security screening method, because no such method existed. The device remains in use. It remains unreliable in precisely the cases that matter most: the experienced, deliberate spy who has been trained or coached to pass it. The KGB training materials on polygraph countermeasures, which became available after 1991, showed a service that understood the device's limitations better than the service that relied on it — and had been systematically exploiting those limitations for decades.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
William Webster — Director of Central Intelligence
Director of Central Intelligence · CIA Headquarters, Langley · 1987–1991

William Webster succeeded William Casey as Director of Central Intelligence in May 1987 — arriving at Langley in the aftermath of the Iran-Contra revelations, the Year of the Spy, and a period of Congressional and public skepticism about the CIA that had not been this intense since the Church Committee investigations twelve years earlier. He was a former federal judge and former FBI Director, a man whose explicit mandate was institutional restoration after the operational excesses and legal irregularities of the Casey years. Where Casey had expanded the covert action program to a scale that had outrun Congressional notification requirements and violated statutes against aiding foreign governments, Webster was deliberate, lawyerly, and procedural — qualities that made him the right DCI for the moment and a less comfortable fit for the operational culture that Casey had cultivated. He served through the entire period of Soviet disintegration: the withdrawal from Afghanistan, the collapse of Communist governments across Eastern Europe in 1989, German reunification, and the August 1991 coup attempt against Gorbachev. The intelligence community's assessment of the Soviet Union in his tenure — that the system was stable enough that fundamental change was unlikely in the near term — was consistently more conservative than the actual pace of events. Webster was not a producer of that assessment error; he inherited an analytical culture that had developed strong institutional biases against predicting regime collapse and had been rewarded for not predicting it. He left CIA in August 1991, days before the coup. The system he had been told was stable fell within four months of his departure. In photographs from his CIA tenure — at Congressional testimony, at the Langley headquarters, at interagency meetings — he looks like a federal judge who has taken a government position and is being careful about it. He was. The caution served the institution's legal interests and did not serve the analytical requirement for honest assessment of what was happening in Moscow.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
The INF Treaty — December 8, 1987
State Department / Arms Control and Disarmament Agency · Washington, DC; Geneva · signed December 8, 1987

The Intermediate-Range Nuclear Forces Treaty, signed December 8, 1987 by Reagan and Gorbachev at the White House, was the first arms control agreement in history to actually eliminate an entire class of nuclear weapons rather than cap numbers or constrain deployments. Both sides agreed to destroy all ground-launched ballistic and cruise missiles with ranges between 500 and 5,500 kilometers: the American Pershing II and BGM-109G Ground Launched Cruise Missiles deployed in West Germany and across Europe since 1983, and the Soviet SS-20 Pioneers, SS-23 Spiders, SS-12 Scaleboard and associated systems that had generated a decade of NATO-Warsaw Pact confrontation. The agreement was also the first arms control treaty to include systematic on-site verification: American inspectors at Soviet missile production and storage facilities observing the controlled destruction of SS-20s under treaty protocols; Soviet inspectors at American facilities watching Pershing IIs be dismantled. The verification regime required significant intelligence community support on both sides — NSA and NRO assets for overhead monitoring of Soviet compliance, careful management of what inspection protocols disclosed about American collection capabilities, and the technical training of inspectors who needed to recognize compliant versus non-compliant destruction. The Senate ratified the treaty 93-5 in May 1988. The deadline for complete elimination was June 1, 1991; both sides met it. 2,692 missiles were destroyed in total. Reagan's formula — "trust but verify" — became the public shorthand for a verification architecture that was, in practice, the most intrusive mutual inspection agreement ever concluded between the two powers. In the signing photographs, Reagan and Gorbachev lean forward over the East Room table in identical postures. They are the same age — Gorbachev 56, Reagan 76 — and they look like two men who have arrived at the same conclusion by entirely different reasoning. The United States withdrew from the INF Treaty in August 2019. Russia suspended participation in February 2023. The missiles that the treaty destroyed are being rebuilt under different designations, on both sides, in both directions.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Lt. Col. James "Nick" Rowe — Five Years to Freedom
U.S. Army Special Forces · South Vietnam (POW 1963–1968); Fort Bragg, NC; Manila, Philippines · killed April 21, 1989

Rowe was captured by the Viet Cong in October 1963 as a Special Forces lieutenant advising South Vietnamese forces in the Mekong Delta. He spent the next five years in prison camps in conditions that killed most of his fellow prisoners: severe malnutrition, tropical disease, continuous psychological pressure, and a slowly developing plan for his execution once his captors concluded he had no more intelligence value. He survived by making himself useful — fixing equipment, practicing basic medicine on other prisoners — and by concealing the fact that he was a Special Forces officer rather than a construction engineer, which his cover claimed. On December 31, 1968, during a U.S. air strike that scattered his guards, he escaped. He was the first U.S. Army officer to escape from Viet Cong captivity. His book, Five Years to Freedom, published in 1971, became required reading in Army survival and resistance training — not because it was instructional but because it documented in granular and honest detail what actual captivity did to a person over five years, and what it actually took to survive it. He wrote the modern SERE curriculum. In 1989, now a lieutenant colonel, he was assigned to the Philippines as a counterinsurgency and SERE advisor to the Philippine Army. The New People's Army — the Communist insurgency then active across Luzon — identified him. On April 21, 1989, he was shot and killed by NPA gunmen as his car stopped in Manila traffic. His driver survived. Intelligence assessments concluded that the NPA had received operational information about Rowe's movements, identity, and vehicle through channels that included Soviet-connected support networks in the Philippines. He had not varied his route. He had survived five years of deliberate captivity in the Vietnamese jungle. He was killed in Manila traffic by an organization that knew who he was and where he would be. His Army photographs from across his career — the slight, composed man who spent five years in a cage and then spent twenty more years teaching others not to die in one — look like the photographs of a professional soldier. The face shows nothing about the five years. Neither did the road where he died.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Andrei Bezrukov ("Don Heathfield") & Elena Vavilova ("Ann Foley")
KGB Directorate S · Toronto, then Cambridge, MA · c. 1985–2010

The Bezrukovs were KGB illegals embedded in Canada in the early 1980s, living as "Don Heathfield" and "Ann Foley." They relocated to the Boston area in the 1990s. Andrei—as Heathfield—obtained a Harvard Kennedy School master's degree and worked as an international business consultant. Elena ran their household and taught their two sons, who grew up as native English speakers and had no idea their parents were KGB officers. The family photographs from this period show exactly what you would expect: a professional couple in their late thirties or early forties, two boys, a reasonable house in a reasonable suburb of Boston, a sensible family car. In June 2010, they were among ten Russian illegals arrested in an FBI sweep. Their sons—who were American in every way that mattered—were left in the United States when their parents were exchanged.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Jack Barsky — The Illegal Who Stayed
KGB Directorate S · New York; New Jersey · 1978–1997

Albrecht Dittrich was born in East Germany in 1949, recruited by the KGB in his twenties, and entered the United States in 1978 under the identity of Jack Barsky—a Brooklyn child who had died in infancy. He settled in New York, worked in IT, and built a life. By the mid-1980s he had an American daughter he adored. In 1988, the KGB—having flagged him as potentially compromised—sent a message ordering him to return. He declined. He sent back word that he had AIDS, knowing the KGB would not pursue a diseased officer. There was no AIDS. He was not recalled. He continued working in IT in New Jersey, obtained accounting credentials, raised his daughter. He became, in all the ways that accumulate into a person, American. When the FBI identified him through a technical operation in the mid-1990s, they watched for years before making contact in 1997. He cooperated fully and received probation. He was not deported. His daughter stayed with him. He is the entry that explains what the entire KGB illegals program was designed to produce—and the case where the design worked on the designer. The legend was intended as a cover. For Barsky, it became the thing itself. In his New Jersey photographs, he looks exactly like an IT consultant with a daughter, a car, and a normal life. He was. He also was not. He has since written a memoir and gives talks at corporate events about his former career.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Lt. Col. Vladimir Putin — KGB Residency, Dresden
KGB First Chief Directorate · Dresden, East Germany · 1985–1990

Putin arrived in Dresden in 1985, age 33, assigned to liaise with the Stasi and recruit informants among foreign students and political contacts. His cover was a position at the Soviet-German House of Friendship. His East German personal identification photographs from this period show a slight man in a brown suit with close-set eyes who could be a regional procurement official, which was plausible. He jogged in the mornings. He played judo. He gained weight. He drove a Lada. When the protests that ended East Germany began in October 1989, his building—the KGB residency at 4 Angelikastraße—was near the route of the demonstrators. A crowd surrounded it. Putin walked outside and told them that Soviet troops inside would defend the building. There were no troops. He called Moscow for guidance. The duty officer said Moscow could not respond. He and his colleagues burned documents all night. He drove to Leningrad in January 1990, with what he later described as a sense that the country he was returning to no longer existed. He was an officer in his late thirties, slightly overweight, in a brown suit, carrying boxes of files that had not been burned in time. The East German photographs show nothing that suggests anything about the next thirty-five years.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Operation CYCLONE — CIA Afghan Program
CIA Near East Division / Islamabad Station · Langley; Islamabad; Peshawar · 1979–1992

The CIA program to arm and finance the Afghan resistance to Soviet occupation — authorized in the final weeks of the Carter administration and systematically escalated under Reagan — became the largest covert action program CIA had managed since Vietnam, and by some measures the most consequential: it contributed materially to the Soviet military withdrawal of February 1989 and, in combination with the economic costs of the Afghan War, to the pressures that accelerated the Soviet Union's dissolution. The program began with modest authorization of approximately $20-30 million in December 1979, intended primarily to signal American opposition to the invasion rather than to produce significant military effect. Reagan's CIA Director William Casey escalated it dramatically: $100 million by 1983, $200 million by 1984, $630 million in 1987, matched dollar-for-dollar by Saudi Arabia under a bilateral funding arrangement that CIA brokered through the Saudi intelligence service, approximately doubling the effective program budget. The program was channeled exclusively through Pakistan's Inter-Services Intelligence (ISI), whose director General Akhtar Abdur Rahman controlled the distribution architecture on the Pakistani side — which commanders received weapons, which factions were built up, which tribal and political groupings were prioritized or marginalized. This gave ISI influence over the internal politics of the Afghan resistance that would outlast the Soviet withdrawal, outlast the Cold War, and shape the political conditions that produced the Taliban government and the September 11 attacks. The material flowing through the pipeline changed character as the program scaled: Chinese-manufactured AK variants and RPGs in the early period, graduated through heavy machine guns, DShK anti-aircraft guns, 82mm mortars, and anti-tank weapons to the Stinger FIM-92A shoulder-fired surface-to-air missile in 1986 — the system whose introduction changed the threat environment for Soviet aviation, forced Soviet helicopters to higher altitudes, degraded the close air support doctrine that had been the 40th Army's most effective counterinsurgency tool, and gave the mujahideen their first reliable counter to the Mi-24 gunship. The program achieved its strategic objective. The secondary effects — the weapons, the fighters, the Saudi-funded infrastructure, the ISI relationships with factions that the United States would later designate as terrorist organizations — became the inheritance problem of the decade that followed.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Milton Bearden — CIA Islamabad Station
CIA Chief of Station, Islamabad, Pakistan · 1986–1989 · Operation Cyclone

By the time Bearden arrived in Islamabad in 1986, the CIA's covert Afghan program was already the largest in the agency's history. His job was to make it larger. CIA Director William Casey had given him $1 billion and an instruction: drive the Soviets out. Bearden oversaw the delivery of FIM-92 Stinger surface-to-air missiles to the mujahideen beginning in September 1986—the introduction that neutralized Soviet air superiority and changed the calculus of the war. He grew a beard for the assignment. His photographs from the period show a large, genial man in civilian clothes who could be an agricultural development consultant from Montana, which was roughly the cover. He managed approximately 250,000 fighters across nine provinces and helped end the Soviet-Afghan War. The beard was considerable.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
The U.S. Navy Maritime Strategy
United States Navy / Office of the Chief of Naval Operations · Washington, DC; fleet-wide · 1982–1990

The Maritime Strategy, developed under Chief of Naval Operations Admiral James Watkins and published in its unclassified summary form in 1986, represented the Navy's most aggressive Cold War operational concept: rather than defending Atlantic sea lanes in a reactive counter-Soviet posture, the Navy would strike forward — into the Norwegian Sea, into the Barents, toward the Kola Peninsula — threatening the Soviet ballistic missile submarine bastions that Moscow's nuclear deterrence depended on. The theory was escalation dominance: if the Navy could credibly threaten the Soviet SSBN fleet's sanctuary areas, Moscow's leadership would face a choice between abandoning their second-strike capability or escalating in ways that created their own problems. NATO conventional defense would be strengthened by the threat to Soviet nuclear command-and-control elements whose loss would undercut Soviet willingness to sustain conventional operations. The classified version went further than the published summary. The carrier battle groups that exercised in the Norwegian Sea in 1983, 1985, and 1987 — the Ocean Venture exercises — were demonstrating the concept under Soviet observation, with Soviet reconnaissance aircraft and submarines tracking the exercise as an intelligence problem in real time. Soviet naval planners who reviewed the exercise reports understood what the Americans were practicing. The debate inside the U.S. Navy about whether the Maritime Strategy was credible or counterproductive — whether the aggressive forward posture strengthened deterrence or created escalation risks — was unresolved when the Cold War ended. What is documented is that Soviet naval planning in the late 1980s shifted to place greater priority on SSBN protection, and that the SOSUS hydrophone arrays and P-3 Orion maritime patrol aircraft that supported forward naval operations were the most consistently productive anti-submarine intelligence collection systems the United States operated. In the exercise photographs from the Norwegian Sea — carrier flight decks in gray Atlantic weather, F-14s in the break, escort ships in formation — it looks like the Navy practicing something. It was.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
K-278 Komsomolets — The Last Dive
Soviet Navy · Norwegian Sea · April 7, 1989

The K-278 Komsomolets was the most advanced submarine the Soviet Navy had ever built: a titanium-hulled attack submarine with a crush depth of 1,000 meters — deeper than any Western submarine could follow — designed to reach operational depth that made it essentially invulnerable to anti-submarine warfare of the period. On April 7, 1989, at a depth of 380 meters in the Norwegian Sea, a fire started in the aft compartment. The crew fought it for five hours before the order to surface was given. When the submarine surfaced, the fire was consuming the aft sections; the escape capsule mechanism failed; and the submarine sank at 17:08 Moscow time. Of the 69 crew members, 42 died — most in the freezing Norwegian Sea water while waiting for rescue ships that arrived too slowly. The K-278 sank to the bottom of the Norwegian Sea at a depth of 1,685 meters with two nuclear warheads in its torpedo tubes. Norwegian and Soviet (later Russian) scientific expeditions documented leakage from the weapons casing in the years following the sinking. The wreck remains at the bottom of the Norwegian Sea. The fire's cause was attributed to equipment failure in the compartment design; the specific failure mechanisms were classified by the Soviet Navy. NATO anti-submarine warfare intelligence — which tracked Soviet submarine deployments throughout the Cold War through SOSUS hydrophone arrays, P-3 Orion maritime patrol aircraft, and surface ship sensor data — monitored the accident from its early stages through emergency radio traffic. The Komsomolets had been the Soviet Navy's answer to the depth limitation that made Western submarines vulnerable to detection at known operational depths; its loss removed an entire asymmetric advantage from the Soviet undersea order of battle. In the photographs taken from rescue vessels as the K-278 was sinking — showing the long, dark hull at an angle, the crew on deck in survival suits waiting for rescue in the cold sea — it looks like what it was: a very expensive piece of military technology on its way to the bottom of the ocean, taking 42 men with it.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
KGB Afghanistan Operations — KHAD and the Intelligence War
KGB / KHAD (Afghan State Security) · Kabul; Kandahar; Jalalabad · 1979–1989

The KGB's role in the Soviet-Afghan war extended well beyond the 40th Army's military intelligence function: KGB advisers created, trained, and effectively ran KHAD — the Khadamat-e Aetela'at-e Dawlati, the Afghan state security service — from its founding in 1980 through the full decade of the Soviet occupation. KHAD was organized along KGB departmental lines, trained in KGB doctrine, and its senior officers received training in the Soviet Union at the same institutions that trained KGB officers. Mohammad Najibullah, who led KHAD from 1980 to 1986 before becoming General Secretary, was the KGB's most successful Afghan institutional creation: an operator who combined political intelligence, counterinsurgency, and human network development in the pattern his Soviet advisers had taught him. KGB advisory teams at the Kabul rezidentura and in the provincial capitals ran parallel intelligence networks independent of and sometimes overlapping with the 40th Army's GRU intelligence sections — a jurisdictional division that produced coordination problems familiar from every theater where the two Soviet services operated simultaneously. The KGB's primary counterintelligence mission in Afghanistan was agent penetration of the mujahedeen factions: identifying commanders, understanding inter-faction relationships, and if possible inserting sources into CIA-ISI supply chains. The introduction of Stinger surface-to-air missiles in September 1986 — which immediately began destroying Soviet helicopter gunships and was CIA's most consequential material contribution to the war — represented a KGB intelligence failure: the Stingers had transited through Pakistan's ISI delivery network, the program's full scale had not been detected in advance, and the impact on Soviet air operations was far greater than the 40th Army's intelligence assessments had projected. KGB counterintelligence operations to locate and recover unexpended Stingers after the war ended — through third-country cutouts, financial incentives, and direct covert operations — continued for years after the Soviet withdrawal, generating some of the most active CIA-KGB operational competition of the post-war period. In the photographs of KGB advisers in Kabul from the 1980s — men in civilian clothes in the environment of a Soviet-allied security service in a country at war — they look indistinguishable from diplomatic personnel. In function they were the institution that built and ran Afghan state security. The institution they built survived them by longer than the Soviet Union did.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
40th Army — The Afgantsy
Soviet Army, Limited Contingent in Afghanistan · Kabul; Kandahar; Jalalabad · December 1979–February 1989

The Soviet 40th Army entered Afghanistan in December 1979 and left in February 1989. In between, approximately 620,000 Soviet soldiers rotated through a war their government described publicly as a limited assistance mission and privately as a strategic campaign the GRU's prewar assessment had warned would likely fail within its first two years. The 40th Army lost 15,051 dead, 35,478 wounded, and 311 missing; the Soviet government released none of these figures during the war. The zinc coffins came back on An-12 transport aircraft — soldiers called them "Black Tulips" for the cargo planes, "Cargo 200" for the military classification on the paperwork. Families were sometimes told their sons had died in training accidents. Coffins were sometimes welded shut with instructions not to open them. The men who came back alive — the Afgantsy — returned to a country that had been told almost nothing about what the war was and received them accordingly: neither vilified nor celebrated, simply not acknowledged. They formed their own veterans' associations and maintained their own networks. The war ended when the mujahideen — supplied by CIA through Pakistani ISI, with Stinger missiles that neutralized Soviet air superiority — had inflicted enough casualties, enough materiel loss, and enough political cost that Gorbachev concluded the war could not be won and would not be continued. The withdrawal was organized by Lt. Gen. Boris Gromov, who crossed the Friendship Bridge from Afghanistan to Soviet territory in February 1989 as the last Soviet soldier to leave. He had timed it that way. The photographs of the 40th Army from the Afghan war — young Soviet soldiers in their summer and winter gear in the same mountains where, on the other side of the ranges, CIA-supplied fighters were being trained by ISI under the supervision of the officers described in the Islamabad Station entries earlier in this compendium — look like what they were: young men in a war that was being lost. On the other side of the mountains, the men in those entries looked like what they were too. Neither side looked like what they were doing together had a clear end.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
SOSUS — Sound Surveillance System
U.S. Navy / NSA · North Atlantic; Pacific Ocean; Greenland-Iceland-UK Gap · 1954–present (still classified portions)

The Sound Surveillance System — SOSUS — was the United States Navy's fixed, bottom-mounted hydrophone network that monitored Soviet submarine movements across the world's oceans through acoustic detection. Deployed beginning in the 1950s across the Atlantic floor, then extended across the Pacific and into other ocean basins, SOSUS consisted of arrays of hydrophones anchored to the ocean bottom and connected by undersea cables to land-based processing stations where the acoustic signatures of passing submarines were analyzed, identified, and tracked. The system's fundamental physics were elegant: submarines radiated acoustic signatures — propeller cavitation noise, machinery vibration, flow noise around their hulls — that propagated through the ocean's sound channel at distances of hundreds or thousands of miles. Hydrophone arrays that spanned the geographic choke points through which Soviet submarines had to pass to reach the open Atlantic — the Greenland-Iceland-UK Gap in particular — could detect and track Soviet submarines transiting from their Kola Peninsula bases into the North Atlantic. By the 1960s and 1970s, SOSUS coverage was comprehensive enough that the U.S. Navy maintained awareness of the approximate location of most Soviet submarines at sea, most of the time. This strategic awareness was the foundation of American ASW confidence and was the primary intelligence driver of the Soviet submarine quieting program: the Soviets knew from their intelligence operations (Walker ring, Ronald Pelton's disclosure of IVY BELLS, technical analysis of SOSUS capabilities) that the Americans were listening, and invested heavily through the 1970s and 1980s in reducing their submarines' acoustic signatures to below the detection threshold. The culmination of this effort — the Yankee-class and then Delta-class submarines, and eventually the Akula, whose acoustic signature approached American submarine noise levels — was driven entirely by the intelligence awareness that SOSUS existed and was effective. The system's existence was publicly acknowledged by the Navy in 1991, though specific capabilities, coverage patterns, and current deployments remained classified. In the SOSUS processing stations — the racks of recording equipment, the acoustic displays where analysts identified submarine signatures by their characteristic noise profiles, the communication links to ASW patrol aircraft directed to locations SOSUS had identified — the listening infrastructure looks like what it is: a continental-scale surveillance system that had been tracking Soviet submarines since before most of its operators were born.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Operation TECHSNATCH — The Cuckoo's Egg
KGB First Chief Directorate · West Germany; worldwide computer networks · 1986–1987

In 1986, Cliff Stoll, an astronomer serving as a computer systems manager at Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory in California, noticed a 75-cent accounting discrepancy in his lab's computer network billing. Tracing the discrepancy, he found an unauthorized user accessing the laboratory's VAX computers and using them as a gateway to other research and military computer networks. Over the following months, Stoll — working with FBI, NSA, and CIA representatives who were initially skeptical that a single hacker was worth their attention — documented an intrusion network centered on a group of hackers based in West Germany operating under the leadership of Markus Hess of Hanover. The hackers had sold their services to the KGB's First Chief Directorate, conducting systematic intrusions into American military and research computer networks and exfiltrating user files, system configuration data, and any sensitive materials they could access. The KGB contact was a Hanover-based intermediary; the payment was in cash and cocaine. The operation, which the KGB designated TECHSNATCH internally, demonstrated that computer intrusion had become a viable KGB collection methodology years before the American intelligence community had developed systematic defenses against it. The hackers accessed military systems at Anniston Army Depot, at Strategic Defense Initiative-related research computers, and at numerous research university networks. The material they stole was assessed as of limited strategic value — the military network security of the period was poor enough that truly classified material was rarely stored on networks with external connectivity — but the operation's significance was the methodology: the use of civilian hackers as a deniable collection capability, paid in criminal currency, targeting military networks through the research network connections that linked university computing to military R&D. Stoll's documentation of the intrusion, compiled over ten months of patient monitoring while NSA and FBI struggled to get authorization to trace the communications, eventually led to the West German BKA arresting Hess and his associates. Three of the five hackers arrested subsequently died in circumstances that were ruled suicide or accident; the case's violent resolution added to its Cold War atmosphere. Stoll published his account as "The Cuckoo's Egg" in 1989 — the first widely-read account of computer network intrusion that reached a non-technical audience. In the printouts Stoll preserved from his monitoring sessions — the logs of the hacker's movements through computer networks, the commands typed at a terminal in Hanover connecting to systems in California — the KGB's technical collection capability looks exactly like what it was: a creative adaptation of criminal computer expertise to intelligence collection requirements, fifteen years before the practice was institutionalized.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
The NIE on Soviet Power — The Analytical Failure
CIA Directorate of Intelligence / National Intelligence Council · Langley, Virginia · 1975–1991

The Central Intelligence Agency's National Intelligence Estimates on Soviet economic and military power, produced throughout the 1970s and 1980s, consistently overestimated Soviet economic strength and consistently underestimated the degree to which the Soviet economy was structurally failing. This was not a failure of access — CIA had extensive signals intelligence, satellite imagery, and defector and emigré reporting on Soviet economic conditions. It was a failure of analysis, driven by a combination of methodological conservatism (CIA's analysts applied Western economic modeling to a command economy that behaved differently), institutional incentives (assessments that showed the Soviet threat growing supported budget requests, while assessments that showed it declining were politically uncomfortable), and the extraordinary difficulty of analyzing an economy systematically designed to be opaque. The 1970s NIEs estimated Soviet GNP at approximately 60 percent of American GNP; retrospective analysis after the Soviet collapse concluded the actual figure was closer to 25 percent. The CIA's assessments of Soviet defense spending — which showed it growing at a rate that implied sustained military modernization — were contested by academic economists who argued the Soviet Union simply could not afford what CIA said it was spending, and that CIA's methodology was double-counting. The political consequences of the overestimate were direct: the Reagan defense buildup, justified in part by CIA's assessments of Soviet capability, was a response to a threat that was simultaneously real in its military dimensions and fictitious in its economic ones. The Soviet Union that the NIEs described — a state capable of sustained competition across all domains — was already collapsing economically by the mid-1980s in ways that CIA's Directorate of Intelligence did not describe clearly in its assessments until Gorbachev's reforms made the failures visible. Robert Gates, as DDI and then DCI, was accused by career analysts of pressuring assessments to conform to policy preferences; the parallel failure was more institutional: an entire analytical framework optimized for measuring a threat that was changing form in ways the framework was not designed to detect. In the photographs from CIA's Directorate of Intelligence conferences in the 1980s — the analysts in their conference rooms, their graphs of Soviet military spending, their order-of-battle charts — they look like serious people doing serious work. They were. The subject they were analyzing was lying to them, and they were partly lying to themselves, and the two things reinforced each other for fifteen years.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Afghanistan 1989 — After the War
CIA Near East Division / JCAG · Pakistan / Afghanistan · 1989–1994

In February 1989, the last Soviet soldier crossed the Friendship Bridge from Afghanistan into the Soviet Union. The CIA's Afghan covert action program — which had distributed more than $3 billion in weapons, ammunition, and materiel to the mujahideen over nine years — was drawing down. The weapons were in place: an estimated 300 to 400 FIM-92 Stinger missiles remained in the field unaccounted for, alongside Chinese-supplied mortars, Egyptian-supplied assault rifles, and American communications equipment distributed across factions that CIA had funded but whose long-term allegiances it had never controlled. ISI's distribution priorities, which had consistently favored Gulbuddin Hekmatyar's Hezb-i-Islami, had placed the largest share of CIA's weapons in the hands of the faction most hostile to any post-war political settlement and most consistently described in CIA's own reporting as the least stable of the major mujahideen organizations. CIA mounted a Stinger buy-back program, offering $100,000 per missile against a black market price that was rising. The recovery rate was poor. The program that had achieved its stated objective — compelling Soviet withdrawal — left a weapons-saturated country with a shattered government, competing warlord factions, an ISI with its own political agenda, and a collection of men who had spent a decade learning that foreign powers would fund them, arm them, and then stop calling. The CIA officers who had built the program were rotated out or retired. The Islamabad station, which had been one of the largest CIA operations in the world, was reduced to a routine diplomatic footprint. In the operational photographs from the program's peak years — the weapons deliveries, the ISI coordination meetings, the training sessions — everyone looks like government officials engaged in a foreign policy program with a clear strategic rationale. They were. The rationale ended when the Soviets left. What the program had built did not.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Col. Gen. Boris Gromov — The Bridge at Termez
40th Army, Limited Contingent of Soviet Forces in Afghanistan · Termez, USSR / Afghan border · February 15, 1989

At 10:00 a.m. on February 15, 1989, Colonel General Boris Gromov walked alone across the Friendship Bridge at Termez, on the Amu Darya River between Afghanistan and Soviet Uzbekistan. He was the last Soviet soldier to leave Afghanistan. He carried his young son's textbook. He stopped in the middle of the bridge to face the cameras — Soviet, Western, Afghan — and then walked to the northern bank. The nine-year Soviet presence in Afghanistan, which had begun on December 25, 1979 when the first airborne units landed at Kabul Airport and special forces units killed President Hafizullah Amin in the Tajbeg Palace, was formally over. The 40th Army's withdrawal, which Gromov had commanded since 1987, had proceeded in two phases under the terms of the Geneva Accords signed in April 1988: the first withdrawal of forces completed by August 15, 1988; the second from August 1988 to February 15, 1989. The 40th Army had fought a counterinsurgency it was not designed for — a Soviet military force built around armored breakthrough and deep operations, deployed against a decentralized rural resistance that used terrain, tribal structure, and the Pakistani border as its operational environment. The afgantsy — the Soviet veterans who came home — came back to a Soviet Union that was embarrassed by the war, uncertain how to honor them, and unprepared for what they brought with them: PTSD in a country with no clinical framework for the diagnosis, drug dependency introduced by the Afghan heroin trade, and a generation of young NCOs and officers who had learned that the Soviet military system did not function as its doctrine described. Gromov wrote a memoir called "Limited Contingent" (Ogranichenny Kontingent). He went on to serve as Governor of Moscow Oblast from 2000 to 2012. In the famous bridge photograph — a uniformed general in his late forties walking alone across a bridge, carrying a schoolbook — he looks like a man at the end of something. He was. So was the country behind him.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Dr. Mohammad Najibullah — The Last Afghan President
PDPA General Secretary; President of Afghanistan · Kabul · 1986–1992; died 1996

Mohammad Najibullah had been a KGB-trained intelligence officer before he became Afghanistan's last Communist president. As KHAD's founding director from 1980 to 1986, he built the Afghan secret police under direct KGB supervision and ran the systematic repression of the PDPA's domestic opposition. He was moved from KHAD to lead the PDPA itself in 1986 — replacing Babrak Karmal at Soviet insistence — and became President of Afghanistan under a new constitution in 1987, presenting himself with Soviet encouragement as a reformer under a policy of "national reconciliation" that attempted to broaden the regime's base as Soviet withdrawal approached. The reconciliation failed. The mujahideen resistance did not reconcile. Gorbachev's decision to withdraw Soviet forces, announced in February 1988 and completed in February 1989, ended the military support that had been keeping the regime alive. Najibullah's government survived Soviet withdrawal by three years — longer than most Western assessments predicted, sustained in part by Soviet arms deliveries that continued until the USSR itself collapsed in December 1991. With the end of Soviet aid, the PDPA government fell in April 1992. Najibullah attempted to leave Afghanistan but was prevented by Uzbek warlord Abdul Rashid Dostum, whose militia controlled the airport. He took refuge in the UN compound in Kabul. He lived there for four years. When the Taliban entered Kabul on September 27, 1996, they entered the UN compound, dragged him out, tortured him, and hanged his body from a traffic post near the presidential palace, leaving it for public display. The man the KGB had built as its instrument of Afghan state power died at the hands of a movement whose growth CIA had underwritten, in a country destroyed by the competition between them. In the photographs from his presidency — in Western suits, at international forums, attempting to project the legitimacy that Soviet departure had removed — he looks like a man who understood, precisely, what was coming.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Manuel Noriega — The Asset Who Became the Target
CIA / DIA asset; Panamanian Defense Forces commander · Panama City · 1967–1989

Manuel Noriega had been a paid CIA asset since at least 1967 — his name appeared in CIA files as an intelligence source during his time as a military intelligence officer in Panama, and the relationship continued and deepened through the 1970s and 1980s as he rose to commander of the Panamanian Defense Forces and effective head of state. His intelligence value was considerable: he provided information on Cuban intelligence activities in Latin America, on Nicaraguan FSLN operations, and on drug trafficking networks that his services was simultaneously protecting. The CIA's willingness to work with him, and the continuation of the relationship by successive station chiefs and DCIs including William Casey, was based on the standard intelligence calculus: his information was useful; his position in a strategically important transit country was valuable; and the alternatives were less controllable. Noriega's relationship with the Medellín cartel — he had been on their payroll since the late 1970s, providing money-laundering services, airstrip access, and protection for cocaine shipments transiting Panama — was documented in DEA files by the mid-1980s. By 1986 the collision between his intelligence value and his cartel relationships had created an interagency problem that went unresolved for three years. The Reagan administration indicted him in federal court in 1988 — while he was still, formally, an intelligence asset — and began the political and military pressure that culminated in Operation Just Cause in December 1989. The invasion force of 24,000 US troops was the largest American military operation since Vietnam. Noriega eluded capture for four days, eventually taking refuge in the Papal Nuncio's residence in Panama City; US forces deployed loudspeakers outside the building and played rock music around the clock until he surrendered. He was flown to Miami, tried in federal court on drug trafficking charges, convicted, and sentenced to forty years — later reduced on cooperation. He served twenty-two years in American custody, followed by extradition to France and then Panama for further charges. He died in Panama in 2017. CIA's files on his three decades of association with American intelligence remain classified. The indictment, conviction, and imprisonment of a former CIA asset on drug charges by the United States government is not, in the historical record, a unique occurrence. It is, however, among the better-documented ones.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Felix Rodriguez — "Max Gomez"
CIA Paramilitary Operations Officer · Bay of Pigs; Bolivia; Vietnam; El Salvador · 1961–1986

Rodriguez was born in Cuba in 1941, escaped after the Castro revolution, was recruited by the CIA, and participated in the Bay of Pigs invasion at nineteen. He spent the following twenty-five years in the CIA's paramilitary service and developed the defining visual vocabulary of the CIA field officer in hot climates: guayabera shirt, slacks, no tie, the bearing of a competent small businessman from Miami who has found himself in difficult situations before and dealt with them efficiently. In 1967, the CIA tracked Che Guevara's guerrilla band to Bolivia; Rodriguez advised the Bolivian Army unit that captured him. On October 9, 1967, he stood next to Guevara in a schoolhouse in La Higuera before the execution and photographed himself there. The photograph was published around the world. He also flew CIA helicopter missions in Vietnam and was reportedly involved in Phoenix Program operations. In 1985 he was operating out of Ilopango Air Base in El Salvador under the name "Max Gomez," coordinating CIA-connected supply flights to the Nicaraguan Contras under arrangements linked to NSC staff — specifically Oliver North — and introduced to the operation through Donald Gregg, National Security Advisor to Vice President George H.W. Bush. Whether Bush knew the extent of Rodriguez's activities was a central question during the Iran-Contra investigation. Rodriguez testified cooperatively before Congress. He wore a guayabera shirt to some of the hearings. In his photographs from across forty years of operations — Bolivian schoolhouses, Vietnamese helicopters, Salvadoran airfields, congressional hearing rooms — the terrain and the decade change but the expression does not: a compact man who appears entirely untroubled by his current location, because he has been in worse ones.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Gen. Richard Secord — "The Enterprise"
USAF (Ret.) / NSC contractor · Ilopango; Tehran; Geneva · 1984–1986

Secord was a retired Air Force major general who, with Iranian-American arms dealer Albert Hakim, built what Oliver North called "The Enterprise" — a private, self-financing covert operation infrastructure that existed outside normal government authorization channels. The Enterprise owned aircraft registered through shell companies in the Cayman Islands, maintained Swiss bank accounts funded by Iranian arms proceeds, employed former CIA and special operations personnel, and conducted supply operations to the Nicaraguan Contras that direct U.S. government funding could not legally conduct under the Boland Amendment. It also ran the arms transfers to Iran — weapons for hostages — that generated the money. The operation was not hidden so much as not where the oversight mechanisms were looking: North managed it from his NSC basement; Secord managed the logistics from a network of shell companies; the aircraft flew from Ilopango in El Salvador with manifests that described civilian cargo. The apparatus became visible in October 1986 when one of the supply aircraft was shot down over Nicaragua and the American crew member — Eugene Hasenfus — was captured alive by Sandinista forces. The manifests, crew contact cards, and phone numbers found on the plane were the thread congressional investigators pulled. Secord testified before Congress in closed and open sessions, wearing the suits of a defense contractor who has been asked to explain his billing structure to a Senate committee — which is precisely what he was. He was convicted in November 1989 of one felony count of making false statements. He was sentenced to two years probation. In his photographs from the period — defense industry conference rooms, congressional testimony, the occasional press appearance — he looks like a retired senior officer who moved smoothly into the private sector. He did. The private sector he moved into was conducting American foreign policy through a Swiss bank account.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Pan Am 103 — Lockerbie
CIA Counterterrorism Center / FBI / Scottish police · London–New York route / Lockerbie, Scotland · December 21, 1988

At 7:02 p.m. on December 21, 1988, Pan Am Flight 103 exploded at 31,000 feet over Scotland. The 747 broke apart in the air over the Scottish Borders. 259 passengers and crew were killed; eleven residents of Lockerbie were killed by falling wreckage. The bomb — 340 grams of Semtex plastic explosive concealed inside a Toshiba radio-cassette player, packed in a brown Samsonite suitcase — had been loaded onto a feeder flight in Malta and automatically transferred through the baggage handling system to the 747 at Frankfurt, without a passenger matching the suitcase. The investigation that followed was the most complex multinational criminal investigation in history to that point: three governments, multiple intelligence services, nine years of forensic work. The MST-13 timer fragment recovered from the wreckage was eventually identified as one of a batch manufactured in Switzerland and sold to Libya; the clothing packed in the suitcase was traced through fiber analysis to a specific shop in Sliema, Malta, where the shopkeeper identified a customer matching Libyan intelligence officer Abdelbaset al-Megrahi. The CIA's Counterterrorism Center had been tracking Libyan intelligence operations and had also been watching Ahmed Jibril's PFLP-GC, which had been building bombs concealed in Toshiba radio-cassette players for exactly this type of operation; the PFLP-GC was initially the primary suspect. The question of Iranian sponsorship — seeking revenge for the Vincennes shootdown of Iran Air 655 in July 1988, which had killed 290 civilians — was never definitively resolved. Al-Megrahi was convicted by a Scottish court in 2001 and freed on compassionate grounds in 2009, dying in 2012 still maintaining his innocence. CIA officer Vincent Cannistraro, who led the early investigation, spent years arguing publicly about unresolved dimensions of the case. In the Lockerbie crater photographs — the nose section of a 747 resting in a Scottish field in winter, Christmas decorations visible in the houses of the village at the edge of the frame — the scene is the Cold War aesthetic in complete reversal. The compendium's premise is the invisible contained within the ordinary. The Lockerbie photographs document what it looks like when the invisible fails and the ordinary becomes the record of an operation. The nose section of the aircraft says Pan Am on the side. Thirty-five of the passengers were Syracuse University students returning home for Christmas.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Operation Earnest Will / Prime Chance — Persian Gulf
160th SOAR / SEAL Team Six · Mobile Sea Bases, Persian Gulf · 1987–1988

In 1987, Iran began mining international shipping lanes in the Persian Gulf. When the Reagan administration re-flagged Kuwaiti oil tankers under American colors, it also deployed Task Force 160 to modified oil-servicing barges anchored in international waters. The operators lived on the barges. They flew OH-6 and MH-6 Little Birds at night, at low altitude, with night-vision optics, looking for Iranian vessels laying mines. On September 21, 1987, a 160th SOAR crew caught the Iranian vessel Iran Ajr in the act of laying mines and disabled it with automatic fire; night-vision camera footage showed the crew frantically throwing mines overboard before Navy SEALs boarded and captured the ship's manifests and surviving crewmen. The footage aired on American television. The operation ran for fourteen months in an active conflict zone with no formal declaration of war. In their pre-deployment photographs at Fort Campbell, the 160th pilots and crew chiefs look like regional airline captains scheduled for a route change to somewhere warm. Their aircraft are in the background, modified beyond recognition. Neither the aircraft nor their crews look like what they were.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Operation Goldenrod — The Sigonella Standoff
SEAL Team Six / JCS / White House · Sigonella NAS, Sicily · October 10–11, 1985

On October 7, 1985, Palestinian militants seized the Italian cruise ship Achille Lauro, killed an American in a wheelchair, and threw his body overboard. After two days they surrendered to Egyptian authorities in exchange for safe passage. President Reagan ordered JSOC to intercept the EgyptAir Boeing 737 carrying the hijackers out of Egypt. Four Navy F-14 Tomcats from the USS Saratoga intercepted the aircraft over the Mediterranean and forced it to divert to Sigonella Naval Air Station in Sicily. SEAL Team Six operators who had been pre-positioned aboard ships were flown to Sigonella; they surrounded the aircraft on the tarmac before Italian carabinieri surrounded them. The standoff lasted through the night. JSOC operators in civilian clothes, weapons in hand, facing Italian military police across an airport tarmac: the Italians insisted this was Italian territory and an Italian matter. The Reagan administration decided not to push it. The hijackers were taken into Italian custody. The most culpable was convicted but never extradited to the United States; he was released in 1991, moved to Rome, and was stabbed to death there in 2011. In the photographs from the tarmac—taken at distance by Italian press—the American operators are indistinguishable from airline ground crew or civilian security contractors. Their clothing provides no identification. They are standing beside an aircraft with weapons pointed at the ground, being told by Italian police that this is not their operation anymore.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Sergei Motorin & Valeriy Martynov — The First Betrayals
KGB / GRU (acting for FBI / CIA) · Washington, DC · 1980–1986 · executed 1987

Motorin was a KGB Line PR officer at the Soviet Embassy in Washington who was recruited by the FBI in 1980 and run as an FBI asset under the code name GAUZE. Martynov was a GRU officer at the same embassy recruited by the CIA around 1982, run as GENTILE and then PIMENTA. Both men were passing intelligence from inside the Soviet diplomatic mission in Washington while their colleagues ate lunch in the same cafeteria. On April 16, 1985, Aldrich Ames walked into the Soviet Embassy on Wisconsin Avenue and offered to sell the KGB the identities of CIA sources for $50,000 — he had credit card debts. In June 1985 he provided his first substantive betrayal package: the identities of the Soviets who were working for the CIA and FBI. Motorin and Martynov were on the list. Both were recalled to Moscow shortly afterward under administrative pretexts. Both were arrested on arrival. Both were tried in 1986, convicted, and executed in 1987. The FBI and CIA spent the following seven years trying to understand why their sources inside Soviet intelligence were disappearing — burned cases attributed to various technical compromises, to suspected moles, to bad tradecraft. Ames was not identified until February 1994. By that point Motorin and Martynov had been dead for seven years, along with most of the other names from the June 1985 package. They are the specific human cost of the entry in this compendium attributed to Aldrich Ames. In the photographs that exist from their Washington service years — the group photographs from embassy functions, the visa documentation, the surveillance photographs — they look like Soviet diplomatic personnel at embassy events. That is what they were, and also not what they were, and the difference is what made the cost possible.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Operation STAUNCH — The Iran Arms Embargo
State Department / CIA / DIA · Washington; Middle East; Europe · 1983–1986

Operation STAUNCH was the Reagan administration's effort, conducted primarily through the State Department with intelligence community support, to prevent Iran from acquiring weapons during the Iran-Iraq War that began in September 1980. The strategic rationale was straightforward: the United States did not want either side to win decisively, but a revolutionary Iran that had seized American hostages, was exporting Islamic revolution, and was actively hostile to American interests in the Gulf was a more immediate strategic problem than the secular Ba'athist Iraq of Saddam Hussein. American intelligence support to Iraq during the war — sharing satellite imagery and SIGINT of Iranian troop dispositions, providing access to American strategic intelligence that gave Iraq tactical advantage — was conducted alongside STAUNCH's effort to prevent Iran from receiving weapons from American allies and other suppliers. STAUNCH involved diplomatic démarches to European and Asian governments asking them not to sell weapons to Iran, intelligence sharing on Iranian arms procurement networks attempting to buy weapons through third-country intermediaries, and monitoring of the international arms market to identify transactions that violated the embargo. The intelligence requirement was significant: identifying the front companies, intermediaries, and shipping networks Iran was using to circumvent the embargo required SIGINT, financial intelligence, and HUMINT on international arms brokers. Operation STAUNCH was simultaneously a sincere policy (the State Department's Dennis Ross and Richard Murphy advocated it seriously) and, by 1985, increasingly inconsistent with other elements of Reagan administration policy. National Security Council staff members — specifically Oliver North, with Robert McFarlane and later Poindexter's authorization — were at that moment planning to sell American weapons to Iran through Israeli intermediaries as part of the scheme to secure the release of American hostages in Lebanon. The secret Iran arms sales that became the Iran-Contra scandal violated STAUNCH's fundamental objective. The intelligence community officers who had been supporting STAUNCH as a policy were not informed of the parallel NSC operation. In the State Department cables and intelligence community assessments of Iranian arms procurement — the careful documentation of an embargo being enforced — the contradiction with what the NSC basement was simultaneously arranging is not visible. The information was compartmented on opposite sides of the institutional boundary.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
KGB Management of Warsaw Pact Intelligence Services
KGB / Warsaw Pact Security Services · Moscow; Prague; Warsaw; Budapest; East Berlin; Sofia; Bucharest; Tirana · throughout Cold War
