Goldwater-Nichols — The Joint Intelligence Revolution
DoD; Congress · Washington D.C. · signed October 4, 1986; implemented through 1990s

The Goldwater-Nichols Department of Defense Reorganization Act of 1986 was the most significant restructuring of American military command authority since the National Security Act of 1947, and its intelligence implications were as consequential as its operational ones. The act resolved decades of inter-service rivalry by establishing the Chairman of the Joint Chiefs as the principal military adviser to the President, strengthening the combatant commanders' authority over all military forces in their area, and creating a legal framework for "jointness" that made interoperability a statutory requirement rather than an organizational aspiration. For intelligence, the consequences were direct: the Defense Intelligence Agency was strengthened as the joint military intelligence production authority, service intelligence functions were subordinated to joint command requirements rather than parochial service interests, and the combatant commanders received the authority to task intelligence collection in their theaters in ways they previously could not. The proof of Goldwater-Nichols' intelligence dividend came in Desert Storm, where the Joint Intelligence Center at CENTCOM — operating under CINCCENT authorities that pre-Goldwater-Nichols law would not have permitted — integrated imagery, signals, and human intelligence in a joint targeting and battle damage assessment process more effective than any previous American military intelligence enterprise. The act also drove the structural changes that eventually produced the National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency, the consolidation of defense HUMINT under DIA, and the creation of intelligence structures in each combatant command that could actually process and exploit the intelligence the national systems were generating. Goldwater-Nichols turned out to solve, partially, a problem that had plagued American military intelligence since Korea: the tactical consumer didn't get strategic intelligence, and the strategic producer didn't hear tactical requirements.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Desert Storm — Intelligence Architecture
DIA / CIA / NSA / CENTCOM J-2 · Riyadh, Saudi Arabia; Washington, DC · 1990–1991

The intelligence architecture for Operation Desert Storm represented the largest deployment of American national-level intelligence assets to a single theater operation since Vietnam — and the first in which the full Cold War collection infrastructure was redirected to a conventional regional conflict. The NSA's signals collection against Iraqi military communications produced a picture of Iraqi force dispositions that complemented overhead imagery from KH-11 and LACROSSE satellite systems. DIA deployed analytical support teams to CENTCOM's intelligence staff in Riyadh. CIA established a dedicated analytical cell. The product flowed to General Schwarzkopf's staff and, through the Joint Intelligence Center, to corps and division commanders who in previous conflicts would have operated on much thinner intelligence. The famous "Left Hook" — VII Corps's wide flanking movement through the western desert — depended on intelligence assessments of Iraqi defensive dispositions that were, by the standards of previous conflicts, highly accurate. The Iraqi command-and-control structure had been degraded by the air campaign to the point where tactical adjustments to the American flanking movement were difficult to coordinate. The intelligence architecture also revealed its limitations: Battle Damage Assessment — determining what the air campaign had actually destroyed versus damaged versus missed — proved genuinely difficult, and post-conflict analysis showed that BDA assessments had been optimistic. The Iraqi Scud hunt, tasked to both special operations forces and intelligence collection, found the mobile launchers harder to locate and destroy than the pre-war analysis had suggested. The broader lesson from the intelligence side of Desert Storm was that Cold War collection systems, built to track Soviet conventional forces in European terrain, adapted reasonably well to a conventional adversary in desert terrain — but that the adaptation revealed gaps in the analytical methods as well as in collection. The photographs from Riyadh's intelligence center in the winter of 1991 — maps, screens, uniformed analysts at workstations, the fluorescent-lit architecture of the Joint Intelligence Center — show the Cold War collection enterprise being used for the first time at its full capability against a non-Soviet conventional adversary. It worked well enough to win the war in one hundred hours. The lessons about what it had not gotten right took years to assess.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Andrew Marshall — The Net Assessment Office
Office of Net Assessment, Office of the Secretary of Defense · Pentagon · 1973–2015

Andrew Marshall ran the Pentagon's Office of Net Assessment for 42 years — a tenure that spanned nine secretaries of defense and made him the longest-serving senior official in American national security history. ONA's mandate was to conduct long-range assessments of the military balance between the United States and its adversaries, identifying trends, vulnerabilities, and opportunities that might not be visible in the annual budget cycle or the current intelligence picture. Marshall's method — patient, historically grounded, often deliberately contrarian — produced assessments that shaped the Reagan-era competitive strategy: the idea that the Soviet Union could be pressed asymmetrically in areas where its weaknesses were structural (information technology, economic flexibility, logistics modernization) rather than meeting it symmetrically in areas where Soviet investment had been heaviest (mass, armor, artillery). The competitive strategy, developed through ONA-commissioned assessments in the late 1970s and early 1980s, provided the intellectual framework for the Reagan defense buildup's emphasis on precision munitions, stealth, command and control, and the Strategic Defense Initiative — all of which were designed to be areas where American technological and economic advantages could be exploited rather than Soviet strengths directly contested. Marshall himself rarely appeared in public and never published under his own name; his influence was entirely through the assessments his office produced, the seminars he convened, and the advisers he mentored. He became a legend in defense circles as "Yoda" — the ancient, inscrutable strategic sage whose ideas took years to understand fully and whose influence on the post-Cold War defense transformation outlasted the Cold War by a generation. ONA continued operating under his direction until 2015, when Marshall retired at age 93.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
CIA Special Activities Division / Ground Branch
Directorate of Operations · Central America, Afghanistan, Africa, the Balkans · c. 1982–1995

The officers of CIA Ground Branch—the paramilitary arm of the Directorate of Operations—maintained a dress code that could be summarized as: khakis, hiking boots, and whatever shirt implied they were with an agricultural NGO or a mining company or an infrastructure consultancy. Through the 1980s they were active in Nicaragua, El Salvador, Angola, Chad, and Afghanistan. They trained and equipped insurgent forces, conducted direct-action missions under Title 50 authority, and returned to Langley looking like they'd been at a mid-level project review meeting in Denver. They were photographed almost never. When they were, they looked like people who had been doing exactly what their cover said they were doing. That was the whole point.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Robert Baer — CIA Near East Division
CIA Directorate of Operations · Beirut; Khartoum; Dushanbe; Salahuddin, Kurdistan · 1976–1997

Baer joined the CIA in 1976 and spent the following twenty years as a case officer in Beirut, Khartoum, Djibouti, Tajikistan, and finally Northern Iraq, where he ran the agency's most ambitious post-Cold War covert operation: beginning in 1994, working from a CIA liaison office in Salahuddin in the Kurdish-controlled north, he assembled a coalition of Kurdish factions — the INC, the KDP, the PUK — along with Iraqi Army defectors and tribal leaders, into an organized coup attempt against Saddam Hussein. The operation involved thousands of committed Iraqi participants, had advanced to a state of readiness, and was aborted by the Clinton White House in March 1995, which had concluded the attempt would fail and was unwilling to accept attribution. The Iraqis who had been promised American support were left in place. Many were subsequently killed by Saddam's security services. Baer was recalled and subjected to an FBI investigation for allegedly soliciting the assassination of a foreign head of state — a charge stemming from a cable he had sent to headquarters. The investigation was closed without charges. He resigned from the CIA in 1997 and wrote See No Evil (2002), which became one of the defining field-officer memoirs of the era and the basis for the film Syriana. In his CIA photographs from across his postings — Beirut in the years after the embassy bombing, Kurdistan in the years before the Iraq War — he looks like an extremely fit man in his late thirties in civilian clothes who has arrived recently and is trying to understand the situation. He was. The situation was always more complicated than the briefings had suggested, and the cable he needed to send was always one that Langley did not want to receive.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Michael Scheuer — Alec Station
CIA Counterterrorism Center / Alec Station · Langley, Virginia · 1996–1999

Michael Scheuer was a CIA analyst with expertise in Afghanistan and the Afghan Arabs who had fought the Soviets when he was made chief of Alec Station — the newly established, compartmented unit within the Counterterrorism Center specifically dedicated to tracking Osama bin Laden — when it was created in early 1996. Alec Station was unusual in CIA's analytical architecture: it was named for a person (in the CIA's custom of naming stations after geographic locations, this was distinctive), organized around a single target rather than a region or function, and staffed by a mix of analysts and operations officers who were expected to work the problem continuously across all sources. Scheuer's unit produced some of the most urgent and specific warning intelligence about al-Qaeda in the pre-9/11 period, including identification of multiple opportunities to capture or kill bin Laden in 1998-1999 that were approved at the working level but declined by CIA leadership and the National Security Council for a variety of reasons — legal, operational, and risk-related. Scheuer himself became an increasingly frustrated voice within the bureaucracy, arguing that the institution was treating al-Qaeda as a manageable security problem rather than a strategic threat requiring a war-footing response. He resigned in 2004 after publishing (anonymously, then under his own name) "Imperial Hubris," a scathing assessment of American counterterrorism strategy. The existence of Alec Station and the targeting opportunities it identified had been known within the intelligence community, but Scheuer's decision to write publicly about them made the pre-9/11 intelligence failure a matter of public record and congressional inquiry. He was not the last intelligence professional to conclude that the institution he served had understood the threat and failed to act on that understanding.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Billy Waugh — CIA Paramilitary Operations Officer
CIA Special Activities Division · Khartoum, Sudan (and many others) · career 1961–2002+

Waugh served with U.S. Army Special Forces from 1961, was medically retired after Vietnam wounds in 1972, and spent the following three decades as a CIA contractor operating in places that did not require a uniform. In 1991, he was assigned to Khartoum, Sudan under light commercial cover, with one task among others: locate Ilich Ramírez Sánchez—Carlos the Jackal—who was living in Khartoum under Sudanese government protection. Waugh found Carlos' house, established his routine, and began jogging past it every morning at the same hour. Carlos was sometimes outside. Carlos sometimes waved. A CIA capture or kill operation was drafted and never approved during Waugh's tenure. The French DGSE, acting on CIA intelligence, eventually took Carlos from a Khartoum hospital in 1994. In photographs from across his career—Vietnam, Libya, Sudan, Afghanistan (where he was dropped in his early seventies after 9/11)—Waugh looks like a compact, fit man in late middle age with the bearing of a retired high school football coach. He is believed to be the most decorated living special operations soldier. He looks, in every photograph, like a very fit grandfather.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Operation RESTORE HOPE — Somalia 1992
UNITAF / USCENTCOM · Mogadishu, Somalia · December 1992 – May 1993

Operation RESTORE HOPE was the U.S.-led Unified Task Force deployed to Somalia in December 1992 to secure humanitarian aid distribution during the collapse of Somali state structures following the fall of Siad Barre. For the intelligence community, it represented something genuinely unfamiliar: a military operation in an environment with no Soviet-associated adversary, no established intelligence infrastructure, and no playbook derived from Cold War planning. The CIA station in Mogadishu had been closed in 1991; NSA had minimal collection coverage of Somali clan communication networks; DIA's order-of-battle assessments of Somali factions were rudimentary at best. The UNITAF command found itself operating largely blind in a fractured political environment it did not understand, dealing with clan leaders, warlord structures, and factional militias whose motivations and relationships were opaque to an intelligence apparatus optimized for the Soviet target. The transition from UNITAF to UNOSOM II in May 1993 — and the shift from humanitarian protection to nation-building tasks under the UN mandate — further complicated the intelligence picture. By the time of Task Force Ranger's arrival in August 1993, the intelligence enterprise supporting operations against Mohamed Farrah Aidid was improvised, politically constrained, and operating with a fraction of the human source coverage that would have been standard in a Cold War theater. The Black Hawk Down engagement in October 1993 was the product of many failures, but the intelligence failure — the inadequate understanding of the target environment, the overconfidence in technical means in a non-technical adversary environment — was foundational. It was the first major demonstration that the post-Cold War world required an intelligence apparatus the United States had not yet built.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Search Bloc — The Hunt for Pablo Escobar
Delta Force (advisors) / NSA / DEA · Medellín, Colombia · 1992–December 2, 1993

By 1992, Pablo Escobar had escaped from La Catedral — the prison he had effectively designed himself — and was a fugitive in the city where his cartel had been killing police officers, judges, politicians, and journalists for a decade. The Colombian National Police's Search Bloc was hunting him. The United States contributed SIGINT collection from NSA, DEA technical assistance, and, according to several accounts confirmed by former operators, a small element of Delta Force advisors who assisted with signals exploitation and targeting methodology. The operators were officially DEA-assigned; their actual function was to help Colombian police exploit Escobar's communications. The methodology — tracking a cellular phone to a specific address through signal triangulation — was the same methodology that JSOC operators would carry from Colombia to Iraq and Afghanistan over the following decade. Escobar was located at a middle-class house in the Los Olivos neighborhood of Medellín by tracking a radio call to his son. He was killed December 2, 1993, the day after his birthday. In photographs from their Colombia rotations, the American advisors in the Search Bloc look like athletic men in their thirties in civilian clothes at a police facility in a tropical country — a description that matches their DEA cover identities exactly. The radio-direction-finding methodology they refined in Medellín became the template for time-sensitive targeting that defined every subsequent JSOC campaign.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Task Force Ranger — Mogadishu
1st SFOD-D / 3rd Ranger Bn / 160th SOAR · Mogadishu, Somalia · August–October 1993

The pre-mission photographs of the Task Force Ranger operators—taken at their compound near the Mogadishu airport in the summer of 1993—show men who look like private contractors on a security detail in a hot country: tan, short-haired, wearing civilian clothes and carrying weapons in a practiced way that doesn't invite questions. Operation Gothic Serpent was a mission to capture the senior lieutenants of Somali warlord Mohamed Farrah Aidid. On October 3rd, 1993, an assault on the Olympic Hotel became an eighteen-hour battle. Eighteen Americans died. Delta operators Gary Gordon and Randall Shughart, who requested permission to insert by rope to protect a downed Black Hawk pilot knowing they were outnumbered, were posthumously awarded the Medal of Honor. In their photographs from before the mission, they look like guys you'd see at a Home Depot.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Mike Vickers — The Afghan Program Architect
CIA Directorate of Operations; OSD (Policy) · Langley; Pentagon · 1984–2011

Mike Vickers arrived at CIA's Near East Division as a young officer in 1984 with a Green Beret background and a facility for operational planning that Gust Avrakotos recognized immediately as exactly what the Afghan program needed: someone who could think about the Mujahideen supply program not as an arms bazaar but as a military problem to be solved with operational analysis. Vickers ran the numbers — on weapons systems, training requirements, logistics pipelines, communication equipment, and battle effects — and produced the strategic concept that transformed the program from ad hoc weapons supply into a coordinated effort to attrite Soviet forces systematically. His analysis drove the decision to transition from Egyptian and Chinese AK-pattern weapons to more sophisticated systems, including Milan anti-tank missiles, and ultimately the decision to recommend the Stinger man-portable air defense system that changed the war. Vickers left CIA in 1986, spent years in the private sector and later as a graduate student, and then returned to government under George W. Bush and Obama as the Assistant Secretary of Defense for Special Operations/Low-Intensity Conflict and later as Under Secretary of Defense for Intelligence — the senior civilian in the intelligence enterprise with responsibility for special operations policy. His career arc traced something unusual in the American national security world: a practitioner who had designed one of the most consequential covert action programs of the Cold War and then managed to think systematically about what that experience meant for how the United States should organize its covert and special operations capabilities for the next generation of threats.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Harold James Nicholson
CIA Case Officer / Station Chief (and SVR asset) · Malaysia; Thailand; Bucharest; "The Farm" · arrested November 1996

Nicholson joined the CIA in 1980, served as a case officer in Malaysia, Thailand, Japan, and the Philippines, and was posted as Chief of Station in Bucharest in 1990. He received consistently superior performance evaluations — precisely the kind of competent, presentable professional whose social fluency made him effective at recruiting foreign nationals in difficult postings. In 1994, he began selling intelligence to the Russian SVR while serving at CIA headquarters, and continued while posted as an instructor at the CIA's training facility at Camp Peary, where he was teaching new case officers the craft he was simultaneously selling. The material he provided identified CIA assets and operations across multiple field postings. He was arrested at Dulles International Airport in November 1996 while attempting to pass documents to an SVR contact; the FBI had been watching him for months. Total payments received: approximately $300,000. He pleaded guilty in 1997 and was sentenced to 23 years — the largest espionage sentence imposed on a CIA officer since Ames. In 2009, while still in prison, he recruited his son Bryan to collect additional SVR payments during a trip to Lima; both were charged and convicted. He was released in 2024. His CIA personnel photographs from his field officer years in Southeast Asia and Romania show a trim man in his late thirties in a jacket and tie who looks indistinguishable from the excellent officer his performance reviews describe. Those reviews were accurate. The standard vetting and evaluation process rated him extremely well on every criterion it measured. The question is which criteria were the right ones to measure.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
CIA in Bosnia — The Dayton Intelligence Framework
CIA Directorate of Operations; Defense HUMINT · Sarajevo; Tuzla · 1995–1999

The CIA's operational role in Bosnia represented the intelligence community's most complex post-Cold War challenge in Europe: providing intelligence support for a peace implementation operation in an environment saturated with competing intelligence services, war crimes suspects being protected by nationalist governments, and an Annex 6 to the Dayton Peace Agreement that explicitly tasked the parties to cooperate with the International Criminal Tribunal for the former Yugoslavia — a mandate that the United States had every intention of supporting and every operational interest in not being seen to support unilaterally. CIA officers deployed to Bosnia in the post-Dayton period operated under a unique mission set: tracking Radovan Karadžić and Ratko Mladić (the Bosnian Serb leadership indicted for genocide at Srebrenica), monitoring compliance with military annexes of the Dayton agreement, and assessing the activities of Iranian intelligence services that had used the Bosnian conflict to establish a presence in Europe. The Iranian connection — Teheran had provided weapons and fighters to Bosnian government forces during the war, with American knowledge and tacit permission, in direct violation of the arms embargo — left a significant intelligence problem that CIA had to manage after the war ended: a network of Iranian intelligence assets embedded in Bosnian government structures, the military, and Islamist organizations. CIA operations to map and assess that network while simultaneously supporting war crimes investigations created the kind of operational complexity the agency had not faced in Europe since the Cold War's most active years. The Dayton implementation period was the American intelligence community's first sustained experience with intelligence as a tool of peace implementation rather than competition with a strategic adversary.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Maj. Gen. William Garrison — After-Action
Commander, Joint Special Operations Command · Mogadishu, Somalia · October 1993

On October 7, 1993, four days after the battle that killed eighteen Americans and wounded seventy-three more, Garrison wrote a letter to the Secretary of Defense accepting personal responsibility for the operation's outcome. The letter is seventeen lines long. The second-to-last line reads: "The mission was not in vain." He was not formally reprimanded. He retired in 1996. In his photographs from the Somalia period—taken at the Task Force Ranger compound near the Mogadishu airport—he looks like a lean man in civilian clothes who has been asked to manage a difficult logistics project in a difficult location. He commanded the best-trained direct-action force in the world, deployed on a mission that was under-resourced, over-optimistic, and increasingly isolated from a unified chain of command. The operational after-action report identified seventeen specific failures across intelligence, planning, and execution. The letter accepted all of them. It was seventeen lines.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
The World Trade Center Bombing — February 26, 1993
FBI / CIA Counterterrorism Center · New York City; New Jersey · 1993

At 12:18 p.m. on February 26, 1993, a truck bomb detonated in the parking garage beneath the North Tower of the World Trade Center. The bomb — 1,200 pounds of urea nitrate and hydrogen gas, built in a rented garage in Jersey City by Ramzi Yousef and a network of conspirators associated with Sheikh Omar Abdel Rahman — killed six people, injured more than a thousand, and opened a crater five stories deep in the tower's foundation. Yousef's intent had been to topple the North Tower into the South Tower. The towers held. Yousef fled to Pakistan the evening of the attack. FBI's investigation — codenamed TRADEBOM — was the largest investigation in the bureau's history to that point, and it broke quickly because of a piece of operational tradecraft that Yousef and his cell had skipped: the rental truck was registered, and when investigators identified the vehicle identification number stamped on the truck's axle in the rubble, they found that Mohammed Salameh — one of the conspirators — had reported the truck stolen in an attempt to recover the $400 deposit. He was arrested at the rental agency when he came back to collect it. The investigation led to Sheikh Omar Abdel Rahman, the "Blind Sheikh," who had issued religious rulings justifying the attack and whose network — a cluster of mosques, training operations, and personal contacts in New Jersey and Brooklyn — CIA had been monitoring for connections to the Egyptian Islamic Group and Sudanese-based networks since the late 1980s. The TRADEBOM prosecution convicted four conspirators in 1994, and Rahman and nine others on seditious conspiracy charges in 1995. The institutional lesson the intelligence community drew was not uniformly absorbed: the network behind the 1993 bombing — the Egyptian jihadi diaspora, the Afghan Arab alumni, the Sudan-Pakistan-New York nexus — was the same network that eight years later would execute a second World Trade Center operation with substantially more planning, more resources, and a different outcome. The crater in 1993 was covered in plywood. The towers were reopened within a month. Both things were true simultaneously, and the second thing made the first thing easier to set aside.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Richard Clarke — The Counterterrorism Czar
NSC Staff, National Security Council · Washington D.C. · 1992–2003

Richard Clarke arrived at the National Security Council in 1992 from the State Department, where he had worked arms control and Middle East policy through the Reagan years, and spent the next decade building the American government's counterterrorism architecture essentially by personal force of will in an institution that did not yet believe terrorism was a strategic problem. Under Bush 41 he coordinated the interagency response to the first Gulf War's intelligence dimensions; under Clinton he became the de facto hub of the nascent counterterrorism bureaucracy, eventually holding the title of National Coordinator for Security, Infrastructure Protection, and Counterterrorism — the first time anyone had a cabinet-level coordination role explicitly for terrorism. Clarke sat at the intersection of the CIA, FBI, NSC, and Defense Department as the threat from al-Qaeda grew through the 1990s: the 1993 World Trade Center bombing, the 1995 Oklahoma City bombing (domestic), the 1996 Khobar Towers attack, the 1998 East Africa embassy bombings, and the 2000 USS Cole attack. His frustration at the intelligence community's and policymakers' inability to treat bin Laden as an existential threat was epic and ultimately public — his 9/11 Commission testimony, in which he turned directly to the families of the victims and said "I failed you," was the single most unusual moment of institutional accountability in the post-Cold War national security world. Clarke's career spanned the transition from a world organized around the Soviet threat to one organized around transnational terrorism, and he understood earlier than most that the transition had actually happened — that the institutions built for the Cold War were not equipped for what came after it.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
CIA Counterterrorism Center — The Transition
CIA · Langley, Virginia · 1986–1995

The Counterterrorism Center was established in March 1986 by Director Casey and Clair George, initially under Dewey Clarridge's direction, in response to the cascade of terrorist incidents in 1985: TWA Flight 847, the Achille Lauro, the Rome and Vienna airport massacres. Its structural innovation was controversial inside the agency: it merged operations officers and analysts in a single unit, breaking the traditional separation of functions that had defined CIA organization since its founding. The analytic wing assessed the threat; the operations wing acted on it; both reported to the same chain of command. Through the early 1990s, as the Soviet-Afghan war wound down and Afghan Arab fighters dispersed across North Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and Central Asia, the CTC began building the targeting database and source network for a threat that did not yet have a unified name in public discourse. By 1993 — when a truck bomb detonated in the parking garage below the World Trade Center — the CTC's analysts had been tracking the network responsible for nearly two years. In 1996 the CTC established Alec Station, the dedicated bin Laden tracking unit, the first time CIA had ever created a stand-alone unit focused on a single non-state actor. The officers who staffed the CTC in its 1990-95 formative period had been trained entirely in Cold War intelligence: Soviet order of battle, Warsaw Pact force structure, nuclear doctrine, counterintelligence methods against state services. They were learning a fundamentally different discipline — not a foreign state with defined geography and bureaucratic hierarchy, but a movement with no headquarters, no uniform, no fixed infrastructure, and no functional distinction between wartime and peacetime. The Cold War CIA had been optimized to understand and deter a specific state adversary. The CTC was being built to understand something the Cold War intelligence framework had not been designed to see. In photographs of the CTC from this period — the few that exist — it looks like any government interagency workspace: fluorescent lights, drop ceilings, men and women in their thirties and forties at government desks reviewing files. The distinction between the Cold War CIA and what followed was not in the workspace. It was in what those files described, and in what the people reviewing them understood about what would happen next.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Gary Schroen — CIA South Asia Division
CIA Directorate of Operations · Islamabad, Pakistan / Kabul, Afghanistan · 1990s

Schroen was a career CIA operations officer who spent the 1990s running the CIA's intelligence operations in Pakistan and Afghanistan as the Agency tried to understand what had happened to the Afghan Arab networks trained during the Soviet-Afghan war and now dispersed across the region. He became the first CIA officer to establish direct contact with Ahmad Shah Massoud — the Tajik Northern Alliance commander in northeastern Afghanistan — and built the liaison relationship that provided the CIA's primary HUMINT channel into Afghanistan throughout the 1990s. He met with Massoud in the Panjshir Valley in conditions that required traversing hostile territory on horseback and by foot. He also tracked the growing presence of Osama bin Laden in Sudan and then in Afghanistan: the progression from an organizing figure in the anti-Soviet jihad to the leader of an increasingly operational international network. The CIA's South Asia Division was one of the few institutional voices in the intelligence community, through the early 1990s, consistently assessing that the Afghan Arab networks were evolving toward direct threat status. Schroen retired in 2002 — but on September 14, 2001, three days after the attacks, he was recalled and led the first CIA team back into Afghanistan: six officers into the Panjshir Valley, with $3 million in cash, to reactivate the Northern Alliance relationship he had spent the 1990s building. In photographs from his South Asia period he looks like a middle-aged American official on a difficult assignment in a remote posting: practical clothing, unremarkable equipment, the expression of a man who has been traveling for a while and has more travel ahead. He was in Afghanistan before most of the men who later made it famous.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
The Rwanda Intelligence Failure — April 1994
CIA / DIA / State Department INR · Kigali, Rwanda · April–July 1994

In April 1994, the United States intelligence community had accurate information about preparations for mass killings in Rwanda and did not act on it. The specific intelligence included: detailed reporting on the Interahamwe militia training and arming in the months before April; radio intercepts indicating coordination between government officials and militia leadership; human source reporting from the Belgian peacekeeping force that described explicit plans for the killing of Tutsi civilians. The CIA station in Kigali was small; Africa was not a collection priority; the Rwandan government had been a cooperative partner. The DIA had assessments, circulated before the genocide began, that accurately projected what was being prepared. The State Department's Bureau of Intelligence and Research produced similar analysis. None of this produced a policy response. On April 6, 1994, the aircraft carrying Rwandan President Habyarimana was shot down over Kigali airport. Within hours, the coordinated killing of Tutsi and moderate Hutu civilians began across the country. Between April 7 and July 15, an estimated 500,000 to 800,000 people were killed — roughly 75 percent of Rwanda's Tutsi population — at a rate of killing that exceeded the Holocaust's pace per day. The Clinton administration avoided using the word "genocide" during the killing because its lawyers had concluded that using the term might trigger obligations under the Genocide Convention. Presidential Decision Directive 25, signed the same month, established criteria for U.S. participation in UN peacekeeping operations that effectively precluded intervention. The intelligence failure in Rwanda was not primarily analytical: the analysis was largely accurate. It was a failure of political will to act on intelligence that was uncomfortable, in a theater that was not a priority, at a moment when the administration was still processing the lessons of Mogadishu from six months before. The post-genocide congressional investigations did not find that intelligence was absent. They found that it was present and did not change what happened. This distinction — between intelligence failure and policy failure — is one of the defining questions of the intelligence record this compendium documents.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Srebrenica — July 1995
CIA / DIA / NSA · Srebrenica, Bosnia-Herzegovina · July 10–19, 1995

On July 11, 1995, Bosnian Serb forces under General Ratko Mladić overran the United Nations "safe area" of Srebrenica in eastern Bosnia. Over the following eight days, approximately 8,000 Bosniak Muslim men and boys were separated from the civilian population, transported in buses to execution sites across the region, shot, and buried in mass graves. It was the largest mass killing in Europe since the Second World War. American intelligence assets — satellite imagery, signals intelligence intercepts, and human source reporting — documented the events as they occurred. CIA imagery analysts identified the mass graves within days of the executions. NSA intercept traffic captured communications between Bosnian Serb commanders that left no operational ambiguity about what was happening. DIA's Bosnian analysis team had been tracking Mladić's force movements and had assessed, correctly, that an assault on Srebrenica was imminent and that the safe area's Dutch UN peacekeeping force was insufficient to defend it. The intelligence was timely, accurate, and specific. The policy response was the same as it had been for three years of Bosnian war: constrained by UN authorization requirements, alliance disagreements, and the Clinton administration's reluctance to commit American forces to a European land war. The intelligence community's assessments on Bosnia had been largely accurate since 1992 — the pattern of ethnic cleansing, the use of "safe areas" as holding mechanisms for civilian populations, the Bosnian Serb military command's systematic approach to population removal — and had not changed the political calculus. Srebrenica changed it. NATO began sustained air strikes in late August 1995 under Operation Deliberate Force; the Dayton Accords were signed in November 1995. The CIA imagery analyst who identified the first Srebrenica mass graves in the satellite photography, Stephanie Frease, testified about the imagery at the International Criminal Tribunal for the former Yugoslavia. Mladić was convicted of genocide in 2017. The intelligence failure at Srebrenica was, again, not primarily analytical. The analysis was correct. It was a failure of political institutions to respond to correct analysis in time to prevent what the analysis predicted. This pattern — accurate intelligence, inadequate response — is present in Rwanda, in Srebrenica, and in a number of entries earlier in this compendium. The satellite photographs of the mass graves were shown to the UN Security Council in 1995. They are still in the public record.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Fred Woodruff — CIA Tbilisi
CIA Senior Officer · Tbilisi, Republic of Georgia · August 8, 1993

Woodruff was the CIA's senior officer in Tbilisi, officially serving as an advisor to the Georgian President Eduard Shevardnadze's protective service as the former Soviet republic navigated an independence that had come faster than its institutions could absorb. On August 8, 1993, he was shot in the back of the head while riding in a vehicle driven by a senior Georgian intelligence official on a road outside Tbilisi. He died immediately. He was 45. A young Georgian soldier was subsequently convicted of a shooting; he later recanted his confession and claimed it had been coerced. The circumstances have never been fully explained. The Georgian intelligence official driving the car was under pressure from Russian intelligence services; Russian intelligence had strong operational motives to remove American advisors from Shevardnadze's inner circle; the shooting occurred in conditions inconsistent with a casual encounter. Woodruff was the first CIA officer killed in the territory of the former Soviet Union after the Cold War's formal end. His official cover as a government advisor made his presence in Tbilisi semi-public; his actual function was to help Shevardnadze build a Georgian intelligence service that would not simply function as an FSB extension. In his photographs from his Tbilisi period he looks like an American official in a warm country on a difficult assignment: fit, slightly tan, the kind of quietly purposeful middle-aged professional appearance that government advisory work produces in the field. He was 45 years old. The case was never closed. The soldier who confessed spent years in a Georgian prison and maintained his innocence. The road where it happened is not marked.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Nunn-Lugar — Cooperative Threat Reduction
DOD / DTRA / CIA (collection support) · Washington, DC; former Soviet states · 1991–present

In November 1991, Senators Sam Nunn (D-GA) and Richard Lugar (R-IN) introduced the Soviet Nuclear Threat Reduction Act, authorizing the use of American defense funds to help the Soviet Union — and after December 1991, its successor states — safeguard and dismantle nuclear, chemical, and biological weapons. The legislation was counterintuitive: it used American defense money to help former adversaries secure weapons that those adversaries no longer had the institutional resources to control. The strategic rationale was straightforward: 45,000 Soviet nuclear warheads distributed across four successor states (Russia, Ukraine, Belarus, Kazakhstan), guarded by military personnel who hadn't been paid in months, in a context of institutional collapse, organized crime penetration, and potential state failure, constituted an intelligence and security problem of a scale that made the Cold War's nuclear standoff manageable by comparison. CIA's role in Nunn-Lugar was collection: assessing which facilities were most at risk, which personnel were most vulnerable to approaches by proliferators, which warhead movements were unaccounted for, and what the signals intelligence indicated about Russian compliance with the program's terms. The operational footprint — American defense contractors and DOD teams entering former Soviet nuclear facilities that had been entirely closed to outside observation for decades — produced an extraordinary secondary intelligence windfall: detailed technical access to Soviet nuclear weapons infrastructure that the imagery and signals collection programs had been trying to characterize for forty years. Ukraine surrendered the world's third-largest nuclear arsenal under Nunn-Lugar's framework, completed in 1996. Belarus and Kazakhstan followed. By 2012, Nunn-Lugar had deactivated more than 7,600 warheads, destroyed more than 900 ballistic missiles, and decommissioned hundreds of bombers and submarines. The program's broader consequence — that the Soviet nuclear arsenal was secured rather than scattered in the chaos of the Soviet collapse — was the most significant non-combat security achievement of the post-Cold War decade, conducted by government officials and defense contractors who carried credentials and clipboards rather than weapons and who photographed facilities where forty years of American intelligence analysts had tried, less directly, to see inside.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Louis Freeh — FBI Director
Federal Bureau of Investigation · Washington, DC · September 1993–June 2001

Louis Freeh was appointed Director of the Federal Bureau of Investigation in September 1993, succeeding William Sessions, who had been fired for ethics violations. Freeh was a former FBI agent, federal prosecutor, and federal judge — an institutional resume that positioned him as the serious professional the post-Sessions FBI needed. He served until June 2001, through the transition from Cold War counterintelligence to post-Cold War counterterrorism, and his tenure defined how the FBI attempted to make that transition. Freeh dramatically expanded the FBI's international presence: he established FBI Legal Attaché offices (Legats) in major capitals worldwide, transforming the bureau from a domestically focused law enforcement agency into one with significant international reach, and he traveled personally to foreign capitals to establish relationships with foreign counterpart services that he believed were more reliable than interagency coordination mechanisms. In counterintelligence, his tenure covered the Aldrich Ames prosecution (1994), Robert Hanssen's continued undetected espionage through the decade (Hanssen would not be caught until 2001), and the Harold James Nicholson case (1996) — a pattern of CIA and FBI penetrations that the counterintelligence community could not consistently detect or prevent. His relationship with the Clinton White House deteriorated from early in his tenure and became openly adversarial by the late 1990s; he was effectively operating the FBI as a quasi-independent institution that reported to Congress as readily as to the executive. The Khobar Towers bombing investigation of 1996 — in which Freeh personally cultivated Saudi Crown Prince Abdullah to obtain Saudi cooperation on evidence — exemplified his approach: direct personal diplomacy with foreign leaders, circumventing State Department channels, on the theory that individual trust produced faster results than bureaucratic process. The FBI under Freeh was building a counterterrorism capacity that would be needed before his tenure ended; whether it built the right kind, in the right way, is the question that September 11, 2001 — three months after his departure — answered with brutal clarity. In his director's photographs — the FBI's formal institutional photography, the Hoover Building backdrop — he looks exactly like the serious, independent-minded federal law enforcement officer his career had made him.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
The Predator — First UAV Combat Reconnaissance
CIA / USAF / General Atomics · Balkans; Persian Gulf; Afghanistan · 1994–2001

The RQ-1 Predator — a medium-altitude, long-endurance unmanned aerial vehicle developed by General Atomics and first flown operationally in 1994 — entered CIA and Air Force service at the precise moment when both organizations needed persistent surveillance capability that manned aircraft could not provide at acceptable cost and risk. Its first combat deployments were over Bosnia in 1995 and 1996, where it provided continuous imagery of Serbian military positions, troop movements, and the Srebrenica aftermath that diplomatic missions and manned reconnaissance flights could not supply with sufficient frequency or duration. The Predator's characteristic operating profile — circling a target area for up to 24 hours at altitudes between 15,000 and 25,000 feet, transmitting real-time imagery via satellite link to ground stations thousands of miles away — was immediately recognized by both CIA and military commanders as a transformative capability. It was also, in its initial Balkans deployments, fragile and unreliable: the early systems suffered from frequent attrition in bad weather, and the satellite link bandwidth required to transmit real-time video was not always available in the required quantity. The operational lessons from Bosnia were incorporated into Predator development through the late 1990s. By 1999 and 2000, as CIA's counterterrorism focus shifted to Osama bin Laden in Afghanistan, the Predator was being considered for use to track and potentially target al-Qaeda leadership at Tarnak Farms — a possibility that exposed the fundamental legal and institutional question that the armed Predator would eventually force: was tracking a terrorist target with an unmanned aircraft and then striking him from the same platform an intelligence operation, a military operation, or something new? The armed MQ-1 variant reached operational service in 2001. The last Cold War reconnaissance platform had become the first post-Cold War precision strike weapon. The legal architecture had not caught up with the technology, and would spend the following decade trying.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
George Tenet — Director of Central Intelligence
CIA · Langley, Virginia · July 1997–June 2004

George Tenet was CIA's deputy director when John Deutch resigned in December 1996, and served as acting DCI long enough to become, by default, the most credible available candidate for a Senate too exhausted with CIA director confirmations to resist. He was confirmed unanimously in July 1997 — the first DCI in a decade to be confirmed without controversy. He was an effective institutional manager: a Capitol Hill staffer by formation, comfortable in the congressional oversight culture that his predecessors had found adversarial, able to build the relationships with the Senate Intelligence Committee that Woolsey had ignored and Deutch had mismanaged. He oversaw the CIA through the intelligence failures of the late 1990s — the bombing of the Chinese Embassy in Belgrade in May 1999, attributed to targeting errors in which outdated maps were used; the intelligence community's failure to penetrate al-Qaeda before the August 1998 Africa embassy bombings; and the sustained debate about the CIA's counterterrorism analytical and operational posture in the years leading up to September 2001. His particular talent was for building political relationships in Washington, which made him uniquely effective at protecting the CIA's budget and institutional equities through three successive administrations. The phrase for which he is most quoted in the public record — "slam dunk," reportedly used to describe the strength of the intelligence case for Iraqi weapons of mass destruction in a December 2002 Oval Office briefing — has become the permanent shorthand for the most consequential intelligence failure of the post-Cold War era. Tenet resigned in June 2004, citing personal reasons, receiving the Presidential Medal of Freedom six months later. The CIA he left had been rebuilt around counterterrorism and was in the middle of its most controversial period since Vietnam: enhanced interrogation, black sites, extraordinary rendition, and the accountability questions that would follow. In his official portrait he looks like a serious man who has been carrying an enormous institutional weight for seven years and has decided to put it down. The weight did not get lighter when he left. He was the last DCI who bridged the Cold War world the agency had been built for and the counterterrorism world it had become.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Gen. Colin Powell — The Chairman and Intelligence
Joint Chiefs of Staff / National Security Council · Washington, D.C. · National Security Adviser 1987–1989; Chairman, JCS 1989–1993

Colin Powell served as National Security Adviser under Reagan from 1987 to 1989 and then as Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff under both Bush and Clinton from 1989 to 1993 — spanning the Cold War's end, the Gulf War, the Panama invasion, and the early post-Cold War period — making him the senior military officer during two of the most significant intelligence transitions in American military history. As National Security Adviser, Powell inherited the Iran-Contra wreckage and worked to restore the NSC's institutional credibility and its relationship with the intelligence community; the NSC staff under his tenure returned to a primarily coordinating role rather than the operational covert action platform the North-Poindexter NSC had become. As Chairman during the Gulf War, Powell was the primary military consumer of Desert Shield/Desert Storm intelligence — the satellite imagery of Iraqi force dispositions, the HUMINT reporting on Iraqi order of battle, the bomb damage assessment that determined when the ground campaign was ready to proceed. His relationship with DCI Robert Gates (who replaced William Webster in November 1991) and with CIA Director William Webster before that shaped the intelligence-military relationship during the Cold War's final period and the Gulf War. The intelligence failure of the Gulf War was not collection but assessment: the unexpectedly rapid ground campaign success reflected partly superior American tactical execution and partly the degree to which Iraqi forces had been more degraded by air operations than pre-campaign BDA (bomb damage assessment) had indicated. Powell's "pottery barn rule" logic applied to intelligence as well: the intelligence community owned its assessments, and during the Gulf War the assessments were both extensive and, in key respects, conservative about the damage air power was achieving. Powell's subsequent service as Chairman through the Somalia and Haitian operations and his advocacy for the "Powell Doctrine" — overwhelming force, clear objectives, defined exit strategies — reflected an intelligence consumer's lessons about what strategic clarity required of intelligence support. He retired as Chairman in 1993 and served as Secretary of State from 2001 to 2005, making his February 2003 UN presentation on Iraqi WMD the final act of a career that had been deeply intertwined with intelligence from the Cold War through the war on terror.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Vladimir Zhirinovsky — Security Services and Russian Ultra-Nationalism
KGB / LDPSS / LDPR · Moscow · 1991–present

Vladimir Volfovich Zhirinovsky's political career began, in a form that was entirely characteristic of late-Soviet politics, with the founding of the Liberal Democratic Party of the Soviet Union in March 1990 — a party that was neither liberal nor democratic but which was the first non-communist party legally registered in the Soviet Union, a registration that required KGB authorization and was, in the assessment of most Soviet-era analysts and subsequent Russian political scientists, provided because the KGB's political operations department saw value in having a controlled nationalist party as an alternative to genuine opposition movements. Zhirinovsky's relationships with the security services were consistent throughout his career: he was seen by Western intelligence as a figure the security services could use as a political tool — extreme enough to make the Communist Party look moderate, nationalist enough to crowd out more genuinely threatening nationalist political formations, and personally compromised enough to be controllable. His party's surprisingly strong second-place finish in the December 1993 Russian parliamentary elections (24 percent of the proportional vote) shocked Western analysts who had not taken the nationalist appeal seriously. The intelligence implications of Zhirinovsky's political success were immediate: it demonstrated that Russian nationalism, resentment at Soviet dissolution, and humiliation at Russia's post-1991 diminishment were politically exploitable at scale. Western intelligence communities, which had focused their post-Soviet Russia analysis on Yeltsin's reform coalition and the successor security services, had underestimated the depth and political availability of the nationalist constituency that Zhirinovsky had identified. His subsequent role in Russian politics — as a reliable nationalist voice who could say things the Putin government preferred not to say officially, who could gauge nationalist sentiment without the government endorsing its content, who could perform political functions the security services found useful — exemplified the relationship between institutional intelligence priorities and political figures that the KGB had developed over decades. In the Duma chamber photographs and the electoral rally images — Zhirinovsky in his theatrical uniform-adjacent outfits, the nationalist crowds — he looks exactly like what his political function required: a credible ultranationalist who gave the security services a political tool they could use without publicly owning.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
The Aspin-Brown Commission — Roles and Capabilities
Commission on the Roles and Capabilities of the United States Intelligence Community · Washington, D.C. · 1995–1996

The Commission on the Roles and Capabilities of the United States Intelligence Community — called the Aspin-Brown Commission after its two chairmen, former Defense Secretary Les Aspin and former Secretary of Defense Harold Brown — was commissioned by Congress in October 1994 and delivered its report, "Preparing for the 21st Century: An Appraisal of U.S. Intelligence," in March 1996. It was the most comprehensive official review of American intelligence since the Church Committee twenty years earlier, and it arrived at a moment of genuine institutional uncertainty: the Soviet Union was gone, the intelligence community had shed roughly 20 percent of its workforce since 1989, and no consensus existed about what the primary targets, missions, and organizational structures of post-Cold War intelligence should be. Les Aspin died in May 1995 during the commission's work; Harold Brown completed it. The report's findings were extensive: it recommended organizational changes to the DCI's authority over the intelligence community (which in practice extended only to CIA, not to NSA, DIA, or the military intelligence agencies that consumed the majority of intelligence spending), suggested reforms to the security clearance process, addressed collection and analysis priorities for the emerging threat environment, and catalogued the persistent friction between intelligence producers and policy consumers that had characterized the Cold War era. Its most consequential finding was structural: the Director of Central Intelligence had community-coordination authority without community-management power, meaning that NSA, DIA, the National Reconnaissance Office, and the other intelligence components answered to the Defense Secretary, not the DCI, for budget and personnel decisions. The report recommended strengthening DCI authority to match the coordination mandate. These recommendations were partially implemented, partially not — a pattern consistent with every major intelligence reform effort in American history. The commission's work influenced the Intelligence Authorization Acts of the mid-1990s and contributed to the organizational thinking that eventually produced the Director of National Intelligence position after 2004. In the formal hearing rooms of the Hart Senate Office Building where the commission took testimony — witnesses from the intelligence community, from academia, from the policy world — the proceedings were publicly documented in a way that the Church Committee hearings had been, and similarly produced a detailed record of institutional shortcomings that the institutions themselves preferred to characterize differently.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
R. James Woolsey — Director of Central Intelligence
CIA · Langley, Virginia · February 1993–January 1995

Woolsey was appointed the 18th Director of Central Intelligence by Bill Clinton in January 1993 — a Yale-educated lawyer, Rhodes scholar, SALT II negotiator, former Under Secretary of the Navy, and known quantity from the Democratic national security establishment. He was serious, experienced, and institutionally credible. Clinton essentially never met with him. The two men reportedly met alone perhaps twice during Woolsey's entire tenure. The joke that circulated in Washington when a small plane crashed on the South Lawn in September 1994: that was Woolsey, still trying to get a meeting. He was DCI when Aldrich Ames was arrested in February 1994 — the most damaging penetration in CIA history, more than a decade in the making, dozens of sources compromised, most of them subsequently executed. The post-arrest damage assessment was catastrophic and detailed. Woolsey's response was to issue letters of reprimand to the officials who had supervised Ames. Not dismissals. Not forced retirements. Not the institutional accounting the case seemed to demand. Congressional oversight committees and the press were uniformly unimpressed. He submitted his resignation in December 1994. In his official photographs he looks like an extremely intelligent lawyer who has recently received information he would have preferred not to have. This is a reasonable description of his entire two-year tenure.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Gen. Wayne Downing — USSOCOM and Counterterrorism
U.S. Special Operations Command / NSC · MacDill AFB, Florida; Washington, D.C. · 1993–2001

Wayne Downing commanded Joint Special Operations Command (JSOC) during the Gulf War — the command under which Task Force Ranger and its predecessors operated — and was appointed Commander in Chief, Special Operations Command (CINCSOC) in 1993, the position that oversaw all American special operations forces across all services and geographic commands. His CINCSOC tenure from 1993 to 1996 was the period in which USSOCOM was navigating what it was for in a post-Cold War environment: the Soviet threat that had defined American special operations doctrine had disappeared; the counterterrorism mission that would come to dominate the command in the following decade was still being argued at the policy level. Downing commissioned and led the independent investigation of the Khobar Towers bombing in Saudi Arabia (June 1996) after CENTCOM and the military chain of command's initial response was judged inadequate by congressional investigators. His Downing Report concluded that CENTCOM had not taken adequate force protection measures against a threat that was well documented, and recommended significant changes to how the American military assessed and responded to terrorism threats against deployed forces. He retired from the Army in 1996. After September 11, President Bush recalled him as Deputy National Security Advisor for Counterterrorism, a position he held for several months before resigning in June 2002, reportedly over disagreements about how aggressively the White House was pursuing al-Qaeda and associated organizations. He died of West Nile virus in 2007 at seventy. In his photographs from JSOC and CINCSOC years — the lean, direct bearing of a career special operations officer, the unadorned expression of a man who has been measuring adversaries against his own capabilities for decades — he looks exactly like the institutional commander of American special operations in its transitional decade.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Cofer Black — CTC Before September 11
CIA Counterterrorism Center · Langley, Virginia / Khartoum, Sudan · 1995–2002

Cofer Black ran the CIA station in Khartoum in the mid-1990s during the period when Osama bin Laden was resident in Sudan, oversaw what the station assessed as an assassination attempt against him, and came back from that assignment with a particular understanding of al-Qaeda's intent and capability that shaped his subsequent thinking. He became Chief of the Counterterrorism Center in 1999, inheriting an organization that had been tracking bin Laden for years without the authority or resources to act decisively against the threat. Black spent his CTC tenure arguing for more aggressive action — direct action authority against al-Qaeda leadership, increased resources for the Jawbreaker teams he wanted to deploy to Afghanistan, greater liaison cooperation with services that had access to the Taliban environment — and losing most of those arguments to a risk-averse policy process that consistently stopped short of the options he was recommending. His testimony to the joint congressional inquiry in September 2002 described the institutional experience with particular precision: "We were at war. They were at war with us." The CTC under Black had produced increasingly urgent assessments through the summer of 2001 — the August 6 Presidential Daily Brief was the most publicly prominent, but there were others — that a major al-Qaeda attack against the United States was imminent. After September 11, Black testified that if CIA had been given the authorities and resources it had requested, the outcome might have been different. Whether that assessment is accurate is contested. What is not contested is that the pre-9/11 CTC's institutional experience matched his description: an organization that knew the threat, could characterize it in operational detail, and could not get the policy system to take the actions the threat assessment logically implied. In his official CIA photographs from the 1990s and 2002, Black looks like a large, purposeful man who would rather be arguing for action than explaining retrospectively why the action he wanted was not taken. He was both.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Ambassador Jack Matlock — At the Embassy at the End
U.S. Embassy Moscow · Ambassador, 1987–1991; Senior Director for Soviet and European Affairs, NSC · Washington, D.C.

Jack Matlock served as U.S. Ambassador to the Soviet Union from 1987 to 1991 — a tenure that encompassed the entire arc of Gorbachev's reform period, the negotiation of the INF Treaty and START, and the dissolution of the Soviet state. He had been the senior Soviet affairs specialist on Reagan's National Security Council staff before his appointment, a career Foreign Service officer fluent in Russian who had served previously in Moscow during earlier postings and understood the Soviet system in the granular way that most ambassadors to Moscow had not. His ambassadorship was characterized by the substantive engagement that Reagan's Soviet policy required: the bilateral dialogue that accompanied arms control negotiations, the management of the relationship as Soviet domestic politics moved in directions that American diplomacy had not anticipated, and the cultivation of a working relationship with Gorbachev and Shevardnadze during the period when American intelligence was still debating how durable the reforms would prove. Matlock's cables from the Moscow embassy during 1990-1991 — portions of which have been declassified — document a diplomat watching a great power's political structure dissolve from the inside. He was present in Moscow during the August 1991 putsch, maintained contact with the reformers who would survive it, and departed after the formal dissolution of the USSR in December 1991. His memoir, "Autopsy on an Empire" (1995), is the most detailed firsthand diplomatic account of the Soviet collapse available in English — a sustained analysis by someone who had been present for the entire process and who brought the analytical instincts of a career Soviet specialist to the retrospective assessment of what he had witnessed. In photographs from the Moscow ambassadorship years — formal receptions at Spaso House, meetings at the Soviet Foreign Ministry, the controlled-access routines of an embassy operating in a surveillance environment — he looks like the institutional diplomat he was: precise, purposeful, at home in a building that KGB technical teams had spent decades trying to compromise.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Sandra Grimes & Jeanne Vertefeuille — The Hunt for NIGHTMOVER
CIA Counterintelligence Center · Langley, Virginia · 1991–1994

In late 1991, the CIA Counterintelligence Center assembled a small team to pursue what had been known internally, since 1985, as the "1985 Mole" — the source responsible for the systematic compromise of CIA assets in the Soviet Union beginning that year. Multiple investigations over six years had stalled, partly from analytical confusion and partly from institutional reluctance to conclude that the source was inside CIA itself. The team included Sandra Grimes, a career Soviet/Russia Division officer who had personally worked the operations that had been compromised and who knew, at a granular level, exactly who had access to them; and Jeanne Vertefeuille, another career Soviet operations officer who had been investigating the problem since 1985 and had assembled years of inconclusive analysis. By 1993, their work had focused unmistakably on Aldrich Ames. Grimes drove the financial analysis: Ames had paid $540,000 in cash for a house, drove a Jaguar, wore expensive Italian suits, and maintained a lifestyle whose cost could not be explained by any combination of his CIA salary and his wife's business income. The financial picture, overlaid against the access profile for the compromised operations, constituted probable cause. The FBI opened the criminal investigation. On February 21, 1994 — nine years, ten months after Ames's first approach to the Soviet Embassy — FBI agents arrested Aldrich and Rosario Ames outside their Langley home as they prepared to drive to work. Ames had been CIA's counterintelligence officer for Soviet operations for part of the period he was betraying them. In government photographs from the mid-1990s, Grimes and Vertefeuille look like what they were: senior CIA professionals of the Soviet operations generation, in the unremarkable institutional clothes of Langley's office culture, with the expressions of people who have finished a very long piece of work. They had. It had a name. The name was on the warrant.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
John Stockwell — The Angola Chief Who Wrote the Book
CIA Africa Division · Langley, VA / Angola · resigned 1977; published 1978

John Stockwell was CIA's Chief of the Angola Task Force — the senior officer managing covert support for UNITA and the FNLA against the MPLA and the 36,000 Cuban troops the Soviet Union had helped deploy — from 1975 until his resignation in 1977. His book, "In Search of Enemies," published in 1978 without submitting to the CIA's prepublication review process, described the Angola operation from the inside: the incompetence, the casual falsehoods told to congressional oversight committees, the indifference to the consequences of the operations for the Angolans involved, and the institutional culture that sustained all of it. The book's publication was the second major case — after Philip Agee's "Inside the Company" in 1975 — in which a former CIA officer publicly disclosed classified operations in book form, and it accelerated CIA's determination to build enforceable legal structures around the prepublication review obligation. CIA took Stockwell to court and lost the injunction — the First Amendment, the court found, constrained the government's ability to prevent publication of information the officer had already published, as distinct from preventing future unauthorized disclosure of classified material. The legal battle over "In Search of Enemies" contributed directly to the development of the CIA's Publications Review Board as a systematic mechanism for enforcing secrecy agreements before publication rather than seeking to suppress books after publication — contractual review rather than prior restraint. Stockwell went on to write a second book ("The Praetorian Guard," 1991), give extensive public lectures through the 1980s about CIA's Third World operations, and become one of the most prominent public critics of the agency from within its former ranks. In photographs from his CIA period — the Angola task force, the Langley offices — he looks like a capable, frustrated officer of the institution he later described. The institution's response was to develop the legal tools to prevent the next Stockwell from publishing without review. Most officers who have since written books submitted them. The board exists. Stockwell built it, inadvertently, by not submitting his.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Frederick Hitz — CIA Inspector General
CIA Office of Inspector General · Langley, Virginia · 1990–1998

Frederick Hitz was appointed the first Statutory Inspector General of the Central Intelligence Agency in 1990, a position created by Congress in 1989 — the first independent inspector general in CIA's history. All previous inspectors general had reported directly to the Director of Central Intelligence and served at his pleasure; the statutory IG reported to Congress independently, a structural change specifically motivated by the Iran-Contra affair's revelation of how thoroughly internal CIA accountability had failed when officers wanted to conceal operations from oversight. Hitz was a former clandestine service officer with career experience in Europe and at Langley headquarters; his selection reflected the Congressional requirement for someone who understood the operational environment he would be investigating. His tenure covered the institution's two most consequential post-Cold War self-examinations. The first: the damage assessment following Aldrich Ames's arrest in February 1994, which the Inspector General's report documented with unprecedented specificity — naming compromised assets, tracing the nine-year timeline of Ames's intelligence production, and documenting the CIA management failures that had allowed a counterintelligence problem of indicators-in-plain-sight to persist for nearly a decade. The IG report provided the factual basis for the subsequent accountability board proceedings in which eleven current and former CIA officers were formally criticized, and became the canonical document for understanding how institutional culture enabled the penetration. The second: the 1998 investigation of CIA's relationships with drug traffickers during the Contra support operations, a report whose conclusions about agency knowledge of trafficking by Contra-associated networks generated significant controversy about what the CIA had chosen not to see and not to report during the Reagan years. In photographs from his congressional testimony — suits and documents, the hearing-room environment of oversight at work — he looks like a careful institutional accountant delivering findings to people who had authorized him to find them. The institution that authorized the findings was not always pleased by what he found. That was the point of making the office statutory.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
The Ames Damage Assessment — The Full Reckoning
CIA Inspector General / Counterintelligence Center · Langley, Virginia · 1994–1995

After Aldrich Ames and his wife Rosario pleaded guilty in April 1994 — he to espionage and tax evasion, she to conspiracy — the CIA and the Senate Intelligence Committee launched the damage assessment that the agency had been unable or unwilling to complete during the nine years Ames had been operational. The Inspector General's report, released in partially declassified form in 1994, was the most comprehensive public accounting of a single penetration's damage in the history of American intelligence. The scope: Ames had, beginning with his initial walk-in to the Soviet Embassy in April 1985, identified for the KGB virtually every significant CIA and FBI asset operating against the Soviet Union. At the June 13, 1985 lunch with KGB officer Sergei Chuvakhin that Ames called "the big dump," he delivered the names of every Soviet national he knew to be working for CIA or FBI, filling a shopping bag with documents and receiving $50,000 for them at the lunch's end. At least ten of those assets were subsequently executed by the Soviet government. Named in public versions of the assessment: Dmitri Polyakov (compromised; executed 1988), Sergei Motorin (compromised; executed 1987), Valeriy Martynov (compromised; executed 1987). Boris Yuzhin received a fifteen-year labor camp sentence. At least fifteen other operations were compromised in ways whose full damage remained classified. The assessment's most consequential finding was not the asset losses alone but their systematic nature: the KGB had known for nine years what CIA thought it was collecting from Soviet sources, meaning that any Soviet agent who continued to operate after 1985 — reporting that CIA found credible, that CIA acted upon — was operating under KGB knowledge, and the intelligence produced in those nine years required reassessment. How much of it had been controlled, distorted, or fabricated the assessment could not conclusively determine. Jeanne Vertefeuille, who had investigated the problem since 1985 and co-led the team that identified Ames, wrote in her memoir that the full damage was probably larger than what the assessment captured. She was almost certainly correct. In the photographs from the 1994 congressional hearings on the Ames case, the CIA officials testifying look like what they were: senior government employees in government suits, being asked questions about institutional failures, providing the minimum amount of information required by the moment. The damage assessment document itself runs to hundreds of pages. Some of it remains classified. Some of the assets whose names are in it are still classified, thirty years later.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Robert M. Gates — The Institutional Man
CIA Deputy Director for Intelligence; Acting DCI; Director of Central Intelligence · Langley, Virginia · 1966–1993

Gates joined the CIA in 1966 as an analyst and spent the following quarter century rising through every tier of the agency's analytic and managerial structure — the only CIA career officer to rise from entry-level analyst to Director. As Deputy Director for Intelligence under Casey, he supervised the production of assessments that Casey found strategically useful and that CIA career analysts found, in some cases, politicized. When Casey was hospitalized in December 1986 with the brain tumor that would kill him in May 1987, Gates served as acting DCI through the final arc of Iran-Contra. His first DCI nomination in 1987 was withdrawn after the Senate Intelligence Committee's Iran-Contra investigation raised questions about what he had known about the diversion. His second nomination in 1991 generated a sustained and unusually public revolt from career CIA analysts who testified that he had pressured analytic conclusions on Soviet capabilities to conform to predetermined policy positions — an accusation that, if accurate, went to the institutional foundation of what the CIA's analytic function was supposed to do. He was confirmed 64–31 in November 1991. He served until January 1993, overseeing the dissolution of the Soviet Union, the closure of scores of Cold War programs and stations, and the beginning of a transition to a threat environment the CIA had not been designed to understand. His institutional photographs from across the 27 years — the analyst, the DDI, the acting director, the DCI — show the same composed, academic-looking man at a government desk, aging gradually from junior official to senior, the expression unchanging across three decades of institutional transformation. He later became Secretary of Defense under both George W. Bush and Barack Obama. He is the figure of the intelligence community as permanent institution: not defined by any single administration or crisis, but by the patient accumulation of authority across every administration from Johnson through Obama. The government desk and the composed expression traveled with him for five decades.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Samuel "Sandy" Berger — National Security Adviser
NSC · Washington, DC · January 1997–January 2001

Sandy Berger succeeded Anthony Lake as National Security Adviser in January 1997 — the transition from the first to the second Clinton term that also brought George Tenet to CIA and Madeleine Albright to State. Berger's NSC operated in the specific transitional environment of the late 1990s: a national security apparatus built for Cold War threat assessment, adapted imperfectly to post-Cold War crises in Haiti, Bosnia, Rwanda, and Somalia, now beginning to reckon with the rising threat of al-Qaeda-organized international terrorism and the difficulty of calibrating military and intelligence responses to non-state actors operating from sovereign territory. Berger chaired the Principals Committee meetings that reviewed the Predator surveillance footage of Osama bin Laden at Tarnak Farms in 2000 and assessed the options for a strike — meetings at which the legal framework, the risk of error, the military utility, and the political consequences of an armed drone strike were all weighed and found insufficient for action. The 9/11 Commission's examination of the pre-9/11 period identified a series of decision points at which stronger action against al-Qaeda might have disrupted the September 11 plot; Berger's NSC was at several of them. He left office in January 2001. In 2003, while reviewing classified documents at the National Archives in preparation for Clinton administration officials' testimony to the 9/11 Commission, he removed and destroyed classified terrorism policy documents — an act that resulted in a misdemeanor plea, a fine, and the suspension of his security clearance.
