He attributed the removal to inadvertence; the Archives staff attributed it to deliberate concealment. In the official photographs from his NSC tenure — the West Wing offices, the Principals meetings — he looks like the senior Washington lawyer and Democratic foreign policy professional he was: a man managing a threat environment that the institutions he ran had not been designed for, trying to calibrate responses that his successors would judge with the benefit of what those responses had failed to prevent.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Operation UPHOLD DEMOCRACY — Haiti Intelligence
CIA / DIA / 82nd Airborne · Port-au-Prince, Haiti · September 1994

Operation Uphold Democracy — the September 1994 American military intervention in Haiti that restored President Jean-Bertrand Aristide after his ouster by the military junta of Gen. Raoul Cédras — was notable in intelligence terms for the extent to which American covert action and diplomatic pressure had failed before the military option was authorized, and for the specific intelligence role played in the last-minute diplomatic resolution. CIA had maintained a presence in Haiti since before the September 1991 coup, with sources inside the Haitian military and political establishments. The intelligence community's assessment of Cédras and the junta — their willingness to negotiate, their likely response to imminent military force, the loyalty and reliability of the FADH officer corps — shaped the Carter-Nunn-Powell delegation's approach when they flew to Port-au-Prince on September 18, 1994, hours before the 82nd Airborne was airborne with 20,000 troops en route. The last-minute negotiation, conducted while American forces were literally in the air, resulted in the junta's agreement to step down in exchange for amnesty — a negotiated departure that owed its success partly to the intelligence assessment that Cédras understood the military force was genuine, close, and not subject to further negotiation. DIA had simultaneously developed detailed assessments of FADH capabilities and likely resistance to U.S. forces, assessments that shaped the military planning and the delegation's confidence that Cédras had no viable military option. The Haiti operation represented a specific type of post-Cold War intelligence challenge: a situation where the adversary was a domestic military junta rather than a Soviet-aligned revolutionary government, where the American interest was restoring a democratically elected government, and where the key intelligence questions — junta cohesion, officer loyalty, negotiating positions, will to resist — were political rather than military-technical. CIA's performance in Haiti received less retrospective examination than its Cold War programs; the operation succeeded quickly enough that its intelligence component became a footnote rather than a case study in the post-Cold War literature.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Office of Naval Intelligence — The Maritime Intelligence Service
Office of Naval Intelligence · National Maritime Intelligence Center, Suitland, Maryland · established 1882; continuous operation

The Office of Naval Intelligence is the oldest American intelligence organization still in existence — established in 1882, eighteen years before the CIA's precursor, and continuous in operation through every subsequent conflict and peacetime period. Its Cold War mission was defined by its subject matter: Soviet naval forces, their capabilities, their organization, their order of battle, their submarine programs, and the threat they posed to the sea lanes on which American and NATO logistics depended. The ONI's technical intelligence function — tracking the design evolution of Soviet submarines, surface combatants, and naval aircraft through acoustic signatures, satellite imagery analysis, intercepted communications, and the reporting of HUMINT sources — produced assessments that shaped everything from Navy ship construction programs to ASW (anti-submarine warfare) tactics. The Walker case — John Walker's sale of Navy cryptographic keying material to Soviet intelligence from 1967 to 1985 — was an ONI failure in the sense that it involved Navy personnel and Navy communications, and the Walker network's product had compromised the Navy's communications security for nearly two decades. ONI's technical collection on Soviet submarines was among the most sophisticated in the American intelligence apparatus: SOSUS (Sound Surveillance System), the underwater acoustic array that tracked Soviet submarine transits across the Atlantic and Pacific, was an ONI-managed system that provided continuous real-time reporting on Soviet submarine deployments. The Walker compromise had told the Soviets about SOSUS coverage in specific areas; they redesigned their submarine quieting programs in response. After the Cold War, ONI's mission shifted to include non-state threats, piracy, maritime terrorism, and the naval programs of rising regional powers — a mission expansion that required different collection methods and analytical specialties than the Soviet order-of-battle focus. The transition from the bipolar Cold War framework, in which ONI's primary adversary was well-defined and thoroughly studied, to the post-Cold War environment of diverse maritime threats, required institutional adaptation at a pace the Navy's intelligence establishment found difficult. In the ONI photographs from the Cold War decades — the acoustic analysis rooms at Suitland, the submarine tracking displays, the analysts with headphones processing SOSUS contacts — the organization looks exactly like what it was: a technical intelligence service that had spent decades becoming expert in the acoustic signatures of a specific adversary's submarine fleet. That expertise was irreplaceable. The adversary's fleet still existed. The Cold War framework within which the expertise had meaning was gone.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
The National Intelligence Council — The Community's Assessors
National Intelligence Council · McLean, Virginia / Langley, VA · reorganized 1979; post-Cold War restructuring 1992–1995

The National Intelligence Council — the body responsible for producing National Intelligence Estimates, the intelligence community's most authoritative coordinated assessments — was reorganized from the earlier Board of National Estimates in 1979 under DCI Stansfield Turner, and restructured again in the early 1990s as the community adapted to the post-Soviet threat environment. The NIC occupied a structurally unusual position: it was analytically independent of CIA's own directorate of intelligence (the CIA's analysts produced independent assessments) but also responsible for coordinating assessments that represented the view of the entire intelligence community — NSA, DIA, the State Department's INR, and the military intelligence services, all of which had distinct analytical traditions, different source bases, and occasional institutional incentives to reach different conclusions. A National Intelligence Estimate required a coordinated process: the NIC chairman assigned a national intelligence officer (NIO) to manage the drafting, the relevant analytical components contributed assessments, and the resulting document represented a community judgment that — in theory — identified where the services agreed and where they dissented. The dissents were sometimes as important as the consensus. The post-Cold War reassessment of Soviet capabilities — and then, after 1991, the assessment of successor state capabilities — was among the most consequential analytical tasks the NIC undertook: the conclusion that Soviet strategic nuclear forces remained substantially capable even as the political entity controlling them collapsed had direct implications for arms control negotiations, defense planning, and the Nunn-Lugar cooperative threat reduction program. The NIC's product was consumed unevenly: some presidents read NIEs carefully; others received summary briefings; others operated on the basis of the PDB (Presidential Daily Brief) alone. The intelligence-policy interface that NIEs were designed to serve was structurally imperfect — assessments that reached policymakers too late to shape decisions, or that were too hedged by coordination requirements to be operationally useful, or that were shaped by pre-existing policy positions in ways that compromised analytical independence. The tension between honest assessment and institutional comfort with policymakers' existing views was chronic. In the NIC conference rooms where coordinating sessions produced the community's most sensitive estimates — the analysts from different services around the table, the institutional interests represented in every draft comment — the work looks like a bureaucratic editing process. The quality of the finished product depended on whether the editing improved the analysis or smoothed over the disagreements that made the analysis real.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
The Malta Summit — Bush, Gorbachev, and Cold War's End
CIA / NSC · Malta; Washington; Moscow · December 1989

The Malta Summit — the shipboard meeting between President George H.W. Bush and Soviet General Secretary Mikhail Gorbachev on December 2-3, 1989 aboard the USS Belknap and the Soviet cruise ship Maxim Gorky — took place in the immediate wake of the Berlin Wall's fall (November 9, 1989) and amid the accelerating collapse of Soviet satellite governments across Eastern Europe. It was not a formal summit with negotiated agreements; it was a first meeting between two leaders in a rapidly changing strategic environment, with the purpose of taking stock of where the relationship stood and signaling intentions for the next phase. The intelligence preparation for the Malta meeting was extensive. CIA's Directorate of Intelligence had been producing analyses of Gorbachev's intentions and the Soviet domestic situation throughout 1989; the question of whether perestroika represented a genuine systemic change or a tactical adjustment to relieve Western pressure was the central analytical question that consumed Soviet analysts at Langley and the State Department's INR through the 1980s. By December 1989 the intelligence community's assessment — largely confirmed by events — was that Gorbachev was committed to domestic reform and would not use military force to reverse the Eastern European revolutions, a conclusion that was politically important because it gave the Bush administration confidence to support German unification without triggering Soviet military response. Bush came to Malta with an intelligence assessment that Gorbachev was in a position of domestic weakness — the Soviet economy was deteriorating, the Eastern European buffer was dissolving, and Soviet nationalist pressures in the Baltic states were intensifying. The CIA's read was that Gorbachev needed Western economic and political support to maintain his reform coalition. Bush used the Malta framework to signal American willingness to work with Gorbachev rather than to exploit Soviet weakness, a strategic judgment that the intelligence assessment supported. In the storm-tossed December seas off Malta — the meetings delayed by weather, the negotiators moving between warships in rough seas — the meeting that didn't produce a treaty was in some ways the more consequential one, because it established that the Cold War's endgame would be managed between governments that had decided to cooperate rather than compete in the final phase.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
PFIAB — The President's Foreign Intelligence Advisory Board
President's Foreign Intelligence Advisory Board · White House · established 1956; reorganized post-Cold War 1993

The President's Foreign Intelligence Advisory Board — PFIAB, pronounced "pee-fee-ab" in the permanent government — was established by President Eisenhower in 1956 as the President's Board of Consultants on Foreign Intelligence Activities, renamed by Kennedy in 1961, and has operated continuously (with a two-year abolition under Carter in 1977-1981) as an advisory body of distinguished private citizens providing independent review of the intelligence community's activities and effectiveness. The board's membership has characteristically included former senior government officials, defense industry executives, academics with relevant expertise, and political figures whose party affiliations were balanced to provide the bipartisan cover that a presidential advisory body touching intelligence requires. Its function — providing the president with independent review outside the regular intelligence community chain of command — was structurally valuable and operationally uncertain: the board had access to classified material, could commission studies, and could provide findings directly to the President, but it had no operational authority and its recommendations were implemented only if the president chose to act on them. PFIAB's most significant documented contributions included the "Surprise Attack Study" of 1957 (which contributed to Eisenhower's decision to accelerate U-2 flights), the "Gaither Report" review of American strategic posture (1957), and repeated assessments of CIA analytical performance during periods when political leadership doubted the community's product. President George H.W. Bush, who had himself served as DCI, was in general better-informed about intelligence than PFIAB and used the board primarily as a source of credentialed outside review rather than as a policy input mechanism. The Clinton administration reorganized PFIAB in 1993 as part of the broader post-Cold War intelligence community restructuring, reducing its size and narrowing its mandate. Under Clinton, the board included figures from the former Soviet policy community — people who had spent their careers assessing Soviet power and now needed to apply their expertise to a different threat environment. The board's review function in the post-Cold War period included assessments of how well the intelligence community was adapting to the new environment, whether collection and analytical capabilities designed for the Soviet target could be reorganized for the emerging threats, and what the appropriate size and structure of the intelligence community should be given the reduced threat. In the photographs from PFIAB meetings — the distinguished members around a conference table in the Old Executive Office Building — they look exactly like the senior advisory board they are: accomplished people who have time to read intelligence and experience to judge it, providing a check on a system they cannot operate.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
James Baker — Secretary of State and the Intelligence Transition
U.S. Department of State · Washington, D.C. · January 1989–August 1992

James Baker served as Secretary of State from January 1989 to August 1992 — the precise span of the Cold War's institutional dissolution — managing American diplomatic policy through the Malta summit (December 1989), the German reunification negotiations (1990), the Gulf War coalition assembly (1990-1991), the Soviet coup attempt (August 1991), and the dissolution of the Soviet Union itself (December 1991). His relationship to intelligence was structural: as the State Department's principal, he was a consumer of intelligence community product (the Secretary received the PDB and was briefed by CIA's director of operations on relevant collection), a producer of diplomatic reporting through the cables that State's embassies transmitted to Washington, and a player in the inter-agency processes through which intelligence assessments informed diplomatic decisions. Baker's State Department intelligence function — the Bureau of Intelligence and Research (INR) — was among the most respected analytical shops in the community, with a smaller analytical staff than CIA but a directness to policymakers and an absence of bureaucratic insulation that made it operationally influential. During the German reunification negotiations, INR's assessment of Soviet military intentions and Gorbachev's domestic political constraints was particularly relevant; Baker's negotiating approach with Genscher, Kohl, and Shevardnadze was informed by intelligence community assessments of how much concession the Soviet leadership could accept without triggering military backlash. During the Gulf War coalition building, Baker's diplomatic work was the public face of an intelligence-diplomatic partnership that was providing assessments of Saudi, Turkish, and Arab League political constraints in near-real time. His August 1991 visit to Ukraine — where he delivered a speech discouraging Ukrainian independence ("the Chicken Kiev speech") that he later regarded as a mistake — reflected an intelligence community assessment that Soviet dissolution would produce more instability than managed autonomy, an assessment that events subsequently challenged. In the State Department photographs from Baker's tenure — the meetings with Shevardnadze, the press conferences after shuttle diplomacy sessions, the formal signing ceremonies — he looks like what he was: a lawyer and political strategist of the highest competence managing a transition in world order that the intelligence community was simultaneously trying to assess and that the diplomatic community was simultaneously trying to shape. The two functions were not always in productive alignment. They were always operating in the same building.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
John Deutch — The Reluctant Director
Director of Central Intelligence · CIA · May 1995–December 1996

Deutch was a MIT chemistry professor and Deputy Secretary of Defense when Clinton appointed him DCI in May 1995, reportedly because he was the only candidate who could survive Senate confirmation and who the Pentagon trusted. He did not want the job — reportedly told the White House he would do it for two years and no more — and made no secret within the agency that he considered CIA's culture dysfunctional and its human intelligence collection capabilities inadequate. He was right about some of it. His relationship with the career clandestine service ranged from frosty to hostile; the inspector general's 1995 Guatemala report — which revealed that CIA assets on the payroll had been involved in the murders of American citizens and a Guatemalan insurgent married to an American — came out on his watch and forced terminations and reprimands that the clandestine service felt were unfair. He created a new standard for human source vetting that critics called the "scrub" and that field officers said made it impossible to recruit anyone with actual access to anything dangerous. He left in December 1996. Two years later, CIA security investigators discovered that for months he had been storing classified CIA documents — including clandestine service information — on personal laptop computers he also used for internet access at his home in Maryland. The computers had not been given the security configurations required for classified material. He was stripped of his security clearance. Clinton pardoned him on the last day of his presidency in January 2001, hours before the pardon deadline. In official photographs from the DCI year he looks like a professor at a government briefing — which is, accurately, what he was: a distinguished physical chemist trying to run an intelligence service he regarded with professorial skepticism, in a two-year term he had accepted with institutional reluctance.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
GRU in the Post-Soviet Period — Institutional Survival
Главное разведывательное управление · Moscow · 1991–2000s

The GRU's institutional survival of the Soviet collapse was, by the standards of the 1991-1993 period, remarkable. While the KGB was publicly dissolved, subjected to dramatic public criticism, and broken into competing successor agencies whose leadership and functions were publicly contested, the GRU continued operating with minimal structural disruption as the intelligence arm of the Russian military. The reasons were partly political — the military had retained more institutional prestige than the KGB through the coup and dissolution period — and partly practical: the GRU's foreign intelligence collection networks, agent relationships, and technical programs were too valuable to disrupt, and the successor military leadership (however demoralized by the collapse) needed a functioning intelligence apparatus. The GRU's HUMINT networks in Europe — including illegals who had been in place for years — continued to operate through the transition, representing a level of institutional continuity that the SVR's inherited networks also achieved but that the FSB's domestic counterintelligence apparatus could not, given the degree of public attention and political disruption it faced. The GRU in the 1990s faced the same resource constraints that afflicted all Russian military institutions: officers went unpaid for months, foreign collection operations were suspended or curtailed for lack of funding, and the technical collection programs that had required sustained investment suffered from deferred maintenance. But the GRU never disappeared, never faced a dissolution demand, and never lost its fundamental institutional identity. When Russian foreign intelligence resumed aggressive operations in the late 1990s and early 2000s, the GRU's structures were still in place — battered, underfunded, but intact. The Cold War-era investment in officer development, network infrastructure, and doctrine had not been wasted. It was simply waiting for resources to resume.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
The Second Chechen War — FSB Counterinsurgency
FSB / GRU / Russian Army · Chechnya; North Caucasus · 1999–2009

The Second Chechen War — launched in August 1999 by Prime Minister Putin following Chechen incursions into Dagestan and a series of apartment bombings in Moscow attributed to Chechen terrorists — was the defining counterinsurgency campaign of the Russian security services in the post-Soviet period, and the intelligence lessons it produced shaped Russian military and intelligence doctrine for the following two decades. Unlike the first war (1994-1996), which had been improvised and catastrophically mismanaged from the start, the second campaign was preceded by months of FSB surveillance, agent development inside Chechen networks, and systematic targeting of the leadership and logistical infrastructure of the Chechen resistance. FSB director Nikolai Patrushev ran the intelligence preparation for the campaign; his operations inside Chechnya, using assets recruited from within the Chechen population (including former resistance members who had become disillusioned), provided targeting intelligence that the first campaign had completely lacked. The apartment bombing attribution — which remained fiercely disputed, with credible allegations that FSB had prior knowledge or involvement — served politically to mobilize Russian public opinion for the campaign in a way that the first war had never achieved. The campaign itself demonstrated the FSB's evolution into a genuine counterinsurgency intelligence organization: the systematic use of HUMINT to target the mid-level leadership of armed groups, the precise sequenced elimination of the field commanders (Khattab, Maskhadov, Basayev) through a combination of agent-delivered intelligence and technical tracking, and the integration of signals intelligence with human source development that the first war had fumbled. By 2009, when the counterterrorism operation regime was formally lifted, the Russian security services had developed a counterinsurgency intelligence model that, whatever its moral costs, had achieved its operational objective.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet/Warsaw Pact
Stasi Officers After 1990 — The Normalization
Ministry for State Security (MfS) — dissolved January 1990 · East Germany / Federal Republic · 1990–1995

When East German citizens stormed the Stasi's regional offices in Leipzig, Erfurt, and Rostock in early January 1990, the Ministerium für Staatssicherheit had 91,015 full-time employees, 173,081 registered informal collaborators (IMs), and eleven years of operational records that its officers were destroying as rapidly as the shredders could process them. The pace of destruction was insufficient: citizens and the interim government's security commission recovered millions of documents — the archive eventually held by the Gauck Authority amounted to approximately 111 kilometers of files, preserved in building after building at the Normannenstrasse headquarters complex. What happened to the 91,000 officers after dissolution followed a pattern consistent with the institutional logic of German reunification: most were not prosecuted. Prosecution required proof of specific criminal acts, not membership in the organization or performance of functions that — in the context of a state that defined its own legality — had been legal. The percentage of MfS officers convicted of crimes related to their service was small. The percentage who found employment in the reunified German legal, business, or security sectors was larger. Several hundred former MfS officers were subsequently identified as having been employed by Treuhandanstalt — the privatization agency that transferred East German state enterprises to private ownership, a process that involved the largest concentration of economic decision-making authority in postwar German history. The Gauck Authority received approximately three million requests for files from German citizens and institutions in the 1990s — the process of learning which neighbor, which colleague, which family member had been a registered IM was the most sustained domestic confrontation with a security service's records in modern history. Former General Markus Wolf — who had run the HVA, the Stasi's foreign intelligence arm, for thirty years — was tried for treason, convicted, and acquitted on appeal; the German constitutional court ruled that espionage conducted under the authority of the East German state could not be prosecuted under West German treason law after reunification. He died in 2006, having written two memoirs and given hundreds of interviews. In the early-1990s photographs of former Stasi officers at administrative dissolution hearings and employment offices, they look like middle-aged German bureaucrats in conservative civilian clothing. They were. The organization they had worked for had defined the institutional meaning of that sentence differently than the new government did.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Gen. Mikhail Barsukov — Director of FSB
Federal Security Service (FSB) · Lubyanka Square, Moscow · 1995–1996

Mikhail Barsukov was appointed Director of the Federal Security Service by Boris Yeltsin in July 1995, succeeding Sergei Stepashin, who had resigned in the aftermath of the Budyonnovsk hostage crisis — the seizure of a hospital in southern Russia by Chechen fighters under Shamil Basayev in June 1995 that took more than 1,500 hostages and exposed catastrophic Russian intelligence and security failure. Barsukov's background was in state protection rather than intelligence operations — he had led the Main Guards Directorate (GUO), the Kremlin security service, and his appointment reflected Yeltsin's preference for trusted personal loyalty in positions of institutional authority over professional expertise. The FSB he inherited had been renamed from FSK just weeks earlier, in April 1995, with a broader mandate covering organized crime and economic crime alongside counterintelligence — an expansion intended to give the service tools for the political environment Yeltsin's Russia had become. The First Chechen War continued under Barsukov's directorship, a grinding conflict the Russian security services had failed to shape through intelligence and political means before it began. His tenure lasted until June 1996, when the reorganization following Yeltsin's reelection campaign — which removed Korzhakov, Yeltsin's powerful personal bodyguard, from the informal security circle — also removed Barsukov. Nikolai Kovalev succeeded him. In the institutional sequence of FSB directors between 1994 and 1998 — Stepashin, Barsukov, Kovalev, Putin — Barsukov represents the appointment of a security loyalist over a security professional, the political middle stage between the institution's post-Soviet chaos and its eventual stabilization and empowerment under the director who came after Kovalev.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
FAPSI — The Signals Intelligence Successor
Federal Agency for Government Communications and Information · Moscow · 1991–2003

The Federal Agency for Government Communications and Information — FAPSI, from the Russian acronym — was established in 1991 as one of the formal successor organizations to the dissolved KGB, absorbing the KGB's Eighth Chief Directorate (communications intelligence and cryptological security) and its Sixteenth Directorate (signals intelligence and electronic surveillance of foreign targets). It was Russia's functional equivalent of the National Security Agency: responsible simultaneously for protecting Russian government communications, conducting signals intelligence collection against foreign targets, and — through a mandate with no precise American institutional parallel — controlling cryptographic standards and licensing encryption technology within the Russian Federation. Alexander Starovoitov, who had a background in government communications rather than operations, served as FAPSI's director for most of its existence and built it into an organization with approximately 50,000 employees. The agency's most consequential domestic program was SORM — the System for Operational Investigative Activities — enacted by law in 1995 and updated in 1998 as SORM-2. SORM required all Russian telecommunications providers to install FSB/FAPSI-compatible monitoring equipment on their infrastructure and provide direct law enforcement access to all voice and data traffic without requiring specific judicial authorization for individual intercepts. Internet service providers were added under SORM-2. The architecture gave Russian security services continuous wiretap access to the country's communications infrastructure as a condition of operating licenses — a legal framework that Western telecommunications regulators described as without parallel in democratic systems, and that the Russian government described as equivalent to Western lawful intercept requirements, which it was not. FAPSI also retained the signals collection assets from the Soviet period: satellite intercept facilities, the undersea cable monitoring program, and the network of SIGINT stations across former Soviet territory. Vladimir Putin's government dissolved FAPSI in March 2003, redistributing its functions among the FSB (domestic SIGINT and SORM administration), the SVR (foreign signals intelligence), and the GRU (military signals intelligence). The institutional wrapper dissolved. The signals intelligence capabilities did not.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
The Khobar Towers Bombing — June 25, 1996
FBI / CIA / CENTCOM · Dhahran, Saudi Arabia · June 25, 1996

The truck bomb that detonated outside Building 131 of the Khobar Towers housing compound in Dhahran, Saudi Arabia on June 25, 1996, killed 19 American Air Force personnel and wounded 372, making it the deadliest attack on Americans since the Beirut barracks bombing in 1983. The intelligence and institutional response to Khobar Towers defined much of the American counterterrorism enterprise in the late 1990s and created the political context in which the intelligence community's counter-Iran and counterterrorism capabilities were assessed. The FBI's investigation of the bombing — conducted under the jurisdiction of the 1986 Omnibus Diplomatic Security and Antiterrorism Act — was complicated by Saudi reluctance to share evidence, restrict American investigative access, or acknowledge Iranian involvement in a way that would force Saudi Arabia to choose between its relationship with the United States and its relationship with Iran. Director Louis Freeh's repeated conflicts with the Clinton administration over the investigation — he felt the White House was not pressing Saudi Arabia hard enough for cooperation — became one of the sustained institutional disputes of the period. CIA's parallel investigation, drawing on HUMINT and SIGINT collection against Iranian and Hezbollah networks, concluded that the attack was carried out by the Saudi Hezbollah (Hezbollah Al-Hejaz) with the support of Iranian Revolutionary Guard Corps Quds Force, under the direction of Iranian intelligence. A federal indictment unsealed in 2001 charged 13 Saudis and one Lebanese national; Iran's role was cited but Iranian officials were not charged. The FBI-CIA institutional tension over Khobar Towers reflected a broader jurisdictional conflict between law enforcement and intelligence approaches to counterterrorism: the FBI wanted prosecutable evidence for criminal proceedings, while CIA's intelligence collection methods were incompatible with law enforcement chain-of-custody requirements. This tension, and the Khobar Tower's specific lessons about Iranian covert action capabilities and intelligence collection against American military facilities, was part of the institutional context the 9/11 Commission later assessed as contributing to counterterrorism failures. In the photographs from the bombing's aftermath — the sheared face of Building 131, the rubble, the American military investigators — the site looks like what it was: a successful intelligence operation by an adversary that had studied American facilities, acquired the material, and executed at a moment and location that maximized casualties.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
SVR London Residency — Post-KGB Intelligence Against Britain
SVR First Service (European) / Illegals Directorate · London · 1991–present

When the KGB was dissolved in December 1991 and its foreign intelligence functions transferred to the newly-created SVR (Foreign Intelligence Service), the London residency continued operating from the Soviet and then Russian Embassy in Kensington Palace Gardens with minimal institutional disruption. The officers who had been KGB First Chief Directorate officers in London became SVR officers; the same rezidentura covered the same targets under a new organizational name. The SVR's London operations in the 1990s and beyond reflected the continuity and the adaptation that characterized post-Soviet intelligence more broadly: the fundamental mission of monitoring the British government, collecting political and economic intelligence, and maintaining active measures capabilities in Britain was unchanged, but the operational environment had shifted in ways that required tactical adjustment. The end of the Cold War had not ended British intelligence interest in Russian operations — MI5's counter-espionage coverage of the Russian Embassy continued as the primary counterintelligence mission it had always been — but the political context changed the diplomatic and public consequences of exposure. The Gordievsky case, the most significant British success against the KGB London residency, had been managed with care during the Cold War; the post-Cold War expulsions of Russian intelligence officers from Britain (several in the 1990s, a major wave after the 2018 Salisbury poisoning) operated in a different diplomatic climate where public attribution was politically acceptable in a way it had not been during détente. The SVR's London operations in the post-Cold War period included continuing collection against British government and parliamentary targets, operations against Russian émigré and dissident communities in London (London having become the primary destination for Russian political exiles with resources), and the active measures and assassination operations against specific individuals that the GRU Unit 29155 and related organizations conducted on British soil. The 2006 Litvinenko polonium poisoning, the 2018 Salisbury Novichok attack against Sergei Skripal, and the broader pattern of what the British government characterized as "hostile state activity" all involved Russian intelligence operations in London that the SVR residency had some role in enabling through infrastructure and intelligence support. In the residency's communications back to Yasenevo — the intelligence product from London sources, the operational reports on émigré community monitoring, the assessments of British policy — the Cold War intelligence relationship between Britain and Russia looks continuous from 1947 to the present, interrupted only by the brief period in 1991-1992 when both sides were genuinely uncertain what the relationship would become.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Vadim Bakatin — The KGB's Last Chairman
Chairman, KGB of the USSR · Moscow, Lubyanka · August–December 1991

Vadim Bakatin was appointed KGB Chairman by Yeltsin on August 23, 1991 — the day after the August coup collapsed and Kryuchkov was arrested — with a specific mandate: dismantle the organization. He was not a career KGB officer. He had served as Interior Minister under Gorbachev, acquired a reputation as a reformer in a system that offered few of them, and was selected precisely because his relationship to the KGB was adversarial rather than institutional. Yeltsin and Gorbachev wanted a man who would not try to protect the institution or its personnel. Bakatin served 102 days before the Soviet Union ceased to exist on December 25, 1991, and with it the legal basis for the KGB's continued existence as a Soviet state organ. In those 102 days he formally proposed and partially implemented the KGB's division into successor agencies — the split that created the SVR (foreign intelligence), FAPSI (communications intelligence), FSK/FSB (domestic counterintelligence), and border service. He took one action that would define his legacy in Russian memory for decades: he gave the American ambassador, Robert Strauss, the technical specifications and schematics for the sophisticated eavesdropping system the KGB had secretly installed in the new U.S. Embassy building under construction in Moscow. The system had been discovered by American technical teams; the embassy had never been occupied because of it; and Bakatin's decision to provide the documentation was a calculated transparency gesture intended to demonstrate a genuine break with the KGB's methods and establish trust with the United States at the moment the Cold War was formally ending. The Russian intelligence community viewed it as betrayal. The system's technical capabilities — representing decades of specialized engineering — had been revealed to the principal adversary. Bakatin defended the decision as compatible with a democratic security service's obligations to transparency. He was never again appointed to government office. In photographs from his brief tenure — in the formal halls of the Lubyanka, at Kremlin meetings — he looks like what he was: a civilian official handed the responsibility of ending one of the most powerful security organizations in history, approaching the task with the conviction that its methods should end with it.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
The FSB — The KGB's Domestic Heir
FSK / FSB · Lubyanka Square, Moscow · 1991–present

When Gorbachev formally abolished the KGB in October 1991, its functions were split among multiple successor organizations: foreign intelligence to the SVR, signals intelligence to FAPSI, border security to a separate service, and domestic counterintelligence and security to what was initially called the AFB (Agency for Federal Security) and then the FSK (Federal Counterintelligence Service). Yeltsin signed the law creating the FSK in December 1993; in April 1995 it was renamed the FSB — the Federal Security Service of the Russian Federation — with a broadened mandate that included investigation of organized crime, terrorism, corruption, and economic crimes in addition to counterintelligence. The institutional question from the beginning was whether the change in name and formal mandate represented a genuine transformation or a bureaucratic rebranding of the same personnel, methods, and institutional culture. The FSB's staffing came substantially from the KGB's Second Chief Directorate (domestic counterintelligence) and from the Fifth Chief Directorate (ideological counterintelligence and suppression of dissent) — an institutional lineage that was in tension with the democratic commitments of the early Yeltsin government. In 1998, Vladimir Putin — who had returned to St. Petersburg after his KGB posting in Dresden, worked his way through Sobchak's city government, then moved to Moscow as a senior Kremlin official — was appointed FSB Director by Yeltsin. He served fourteen months before being appointed Prime Minister in August 1999. The apartment building explosions in Moscow and Buynaksk in September 1999 — attributed by the Russian government to Chechen terrorists, investigated by Russian opposition journalist Anna Politkovskaya (who was later murdered) and former FSB officer Alexander Litvinenko (who was later poisoned with polonium-210 in London) as potentially organized by the FSB itself — occurred on the FSB's operational watch, under Putin's successor as director. Putin was appointed acting President on December 31, 1999. The institutional continuity between KGB and FSB is visible in personnel records, operational methods, and in the fact that the organization that runs Lubyanka Square in the twenty-first century operates from the same building, with many of the same files, as the organization it formally replaced. The nameplate on the building changed. The building did not.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
SVR Active Measures — The Institutional Continuity
Служба внешней разведки · Yasenevo, Moscow · 1991–present

When the KGB was formally dissolved in December 1991, Western analysts and Russian liberals alike hoped that the apparatus of dezinformatsiya — the systematic use of fabricated documents, disinformation campaigns, controlled media, and political influence operations — had dissolved with it. The hope was incorrect. The SVR — Sluzhba Vneshney Razvedki, the Foreign Intelligence Service carved from the KGB's First Chief Directorate — inherited the active measures infrastructure along with the personnel files, the agent networks, and the institutional culture that had run it for four decades. The Service A of the old KGB, responsible for coordinating active measures worldwide, became a directorate within the new SVR. Many of the officers who had staffed it remained. The early 1990s were a period of reduced capacity — budgets collapsed, some foreign operations were rolled up, the political will for aggressive active measures against Western democracies wavered — but the methodological knowledge and the network infrastructure survived the transition. By the mid-1990s, Russian active measures had quietly resumed: manipulation of the Baltic information space, support for European far-right movements that served Russian foreign policy interests, disinformation operations targeting NATO expansion debates. The SVR had inherited something more durable than any single operation: a playbook refined over decades, a cadre of trained practitioners, and an institutional conviction that information warfare was a legitimate and permanent instrument of state power.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
SVR Directorate S — The Program Continues
SVR (successor to KGB First Chief Directorate) · Yasenevo, Moscow · 1991 onward

On December 25, 1991, Mikhail Gorbachev resigned and the Soviet flag was lowered from the Kremlin. The KGB's First Chief Directorate, which had run Soviet foreign intelligence for forty years, survived the dissolution intact—renamed the SVR, the Foreign Intelligence Service of the Russian Federation, and relocated to the same headquarters at Yasenevo. Directorate S, which managed the illegals, transferred with it. The officers embedded in the United States, Canada, the United Kingdom, and Australia under false identities received no instruction to surface. Their cover identities were unchanged. Their missions were essentially unchanged. Some had children who had grown up as American or Canadian citizens. Some had mortgages and car payments and library cards. The Cold War had ended for their neighbors but had not ended for them in any operational sense that affected their daily lives. The FBI's 2010 sweep—which arrested ten Russian illegals in a single operation—revealed how unbroken the program had been: several of those arrested had been embedded since the mid-1980s, their cover lives spanning the entire post-Soviet period. In the family photographs from the 1990s and 2000s, the illegal families look exactly like their neighbors at every neighborhood function. The organization that deployed them no longer existed under its original name. The families at the barbecues remained exactly where they had been placed.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Sergei Tretyakov — SVR New York Station
SVR (Russian Foreign Intelligence Service) · New York, United Nations · 1995–2000 (defected 2000)

Tretyakov served as deputy chief and eventually acting chief of the SVR's New York station — the largest Russian intelligence residency in the United States — under diplomatic cover at the Russian UN Mission from 1995 to 2000. The New York station's primary platform was the UN Mission: UN accreditation provided movement freedom and diplomatic immunity in a city where the density of diplomatic activity, media, financial institutions, and think-tank presence made intelligence collection operationally rich. Tretyakov ran agent networks, managed source relationships with Americans and foreign nationals who had access to UN deliberations and U.S. foreign policy circles, and supervised collection operations against the kind of influence targets — policy advisers, UN officials, journalists with foreign policy access — that the SVR's mandate under Primakov and his successors had prioritized. He defected to the FBI in October 2000, walking into a pre-arranged contact after years of informal cooperation that had developed into full defection in place. His debrief produced substantial intelligence on SVR operations in New York and Washington, on specific agent cases, and on the SVR's operational priorities in the late 1990s. Pete Earley wrote a book about his debrief — "Comrade J" — based on extensive interviews, which Tretyakov apparently provided in the conviction that public disclosure of what Russian intelligence was still doing in the post-Cold War period was itself a contribution to American security. He died in Florida in 2010, officially of a heart attack, at age 53. In the photographs from his UN years he looks like a Russian diplomat of the 1990s: the careful dress of post-Soviet official Russia, the practiced social ease of the intelligence officer who has learned to present as exactly what his cover says he is, in the corridors and receptions of the United Nations where the Cold War's successor games were being played.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Alexander Korzhakov — The Presidential Security Service
Presidential Security Service (SBP) / Federal Protective Service · Moscow, Kremlin · 1991–1996

Alexander Korzhakov had been Boris Yeltsin's personal KGB bodyguard since 1985, when Yeltsin was First Secretary of the Moscow City Party Committee. When the KGB removed Yeltsin from political life in 1988, Korzhakov was the officer who stayed with him through the years of political eclipse. When Yeltsin rose to power in 1990-91 and became Russia's first president in June 1991, Korzhakov rose with him — not as a functional security officer but as the one man in Russia's new political establishment who had been present at everything, who knew where the loyalties actually were, and whom Yeltsin trusted completely. As director of the Presidential Security Service (SBP) from 1991 to 1996, Korzhakov controlled Yeltsin's physical security and, through it, his access: who reached the president, what documents crossed his desk, which officials were allowed into secure conversations. In a political environment where Yeltsin's declining health and increasing dependence on alcohol made access control politically decisive, this was consequential power. Korzhakov used it. He was not formally the head of any intelligence service, but in the early Yeltsin years he functioned as an informal intelligence czar — with relationships into the FSK/FSB, the SVR, FAPSI, and the military intelligence community, and with the operational authority that came from the president's personal trust and physical proximity. He was dismissed on June 20, 1996, during the first round of the presidential election, in a dismissal orchestrated by reformers who convinced Yeltsin that Korzhakov had become a political liability. With him went Barsukov (FSB director) and First Deputy Prime Minister Soskovets. The dismissal was publicly attributed to unauthorized actions by security service personnel; the underlying dynamic was that Korzhakov had accumulated too much informal power, and the people closest to Yeltsin had decided he had to go. He later wrote memoirs of his years with Yeltsin that were less anodyne than most political memoirs from the period — describing what he had observed with the candor of a man who knew he would never be trusted with anything again and had little to lose from frankness. In photographs of Yeltsin from the early 1990s, Korzhakov is often present at the edge of the frame: the man in the dark suit, large and watchful, between the president and the room. He was always there. Until he wasn't.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Boris Yeltsin and the Successor Services — The Intelligence Politics
Russian Federation / President's Administration · Moscow · 1991–1999

Boris Yeltsin's relationship with Russia's intelligence and security services was the central and largely unresolved drama of the post-Soviet decade. He had risen to political prominence partly by positioning himself against the KGB and the CPSU apparatus, and in the immediate aftermath of the August 1991 coup — in which KGB chairman Kryuchkov had been a leading conspirator — he moved to dissolve the KGB, distributing its functions among multiple successor agencies in a deliberate fragmentation designed to prevent any single security service from accumulating the power the KGB had wielded. The MVD, the FSB, the SVR, FAPSI, and the FSO (Federal Protective Service) all emerged from this fragmentation with different functions and different access to state power. But Yeltsin's relationship with the siloviki — the security services and their leadership — proved impossible to rationalize into liberal democratic governance. He needed them for information he couldn't get elsewhere; he needed them for political muscle when the parliamentary opposition (especially after the October 1993 constitutional crisis) threatened his position; and he eventually needed them to manage the Chechen crisis in ways that civilian institutions couldn't. The appointment of Alexander Korzhakov to run the Presidential Security Service gave Yeltsin a personal security apparatus that operated parallel to and sometimes against the formal intelligence structure. The appointment of Vladimir Putin as FSB director in 1998 — after a series of FSB chiefs who Yeltsin found inadequate — and his subsequent elevation to prime minister and then acting president, was the culmination of this decade-long pattern: a leader who had tried to tame the security services finding that the only person he fully trusted to run them was one of their own.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Vyacheslav Trubnikov — SVR First Deputy Director
SVR (External Intelligence Service) · Yasenevo, Moscow · 1992–1996 as First Deputy; Director 1996–2000

Vyacheslav Trubnikov was a career KGB First Chief Directorate officer who had served his formative years as a HUMINT officer in South Asia — India above all, where Soviet intelligence maintained deep institutional relationships dating to the Nehru era and cultivated assets across the government, military, academic, and political establishments. The Soviet-Indian intelligence relationship had been among the most productive in the FCD's global network: India's non-alignment was genuine but its counterbalancing relationship with the USSR was substantial, and KGB officers operating under diplomatic cover in New Delhi worked in an environment where the host service's counterintelligence pressure was, by European standards, comparatively relaxed. Trubnikov's regional expertise and operational record in this environment advanced his career through the FCD hierarchy and positioned him for senior roles in the post-Soviet successor service. He became First Deputy Director of the SVR when Primakov took over in December 1991, managing the operational continuity of the service during its most disorienting period: the loss of Warsaw Pact partner services, the financial collapse that reduced foreign station staffing, the ideological disorientation of officers who had joined an organization with a clear adversarial mission and now served a state whose foreign policy orientation was uncertain. He succeeded Primakov as SVR Director in January 1996, when Primakov was appointed Foreign Minister, and served as director until 2000 — the period during which the SVR rebuilt its operational capacity and clarified its post-Cold War priorities around the successor states, NATO expansion, and the emerging targets of economic espionage. Putin appointed him Deputy Foreign Minister after his SVR tenure, a trajectory consistent with the movement of senior intelligence officials into foreign policy roles that characterized the early Putin period. In the Yasenevo directorate photographs from the 1990s — a building that had housed the FCD and now housed the SVR, with the same corridors and the same organizational culture and a different flag — Trubnikov looks like a senior intelligence professional managing institutional continuity under conditions of state transformation: which is exactly what he was doing.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Evgenii Primakov — SVR Director
Director, External Intelligence Service (SVR) · Yasenevo, Moscow · December 1991–January 1996

Primakov was appointed director of the External Intelligence Service on December 26, 1991 — the day after Gorbachev resigned and the Soviet flag was lowered. He had spent decades as an Arabist, academic, and trusted Soviet foreign policy operative: the kind of figure who had moved through the intersection of intelligence and foreign policy always in the clothes of an academic or diplomat, always with the deniability of an institutional role that was not quite intelligence. His task was to transform the KGB's First Chief Directorate — which had just lost its country, its budget, and its ideological reason for existing — into a viable foreign intelligence service for a government that did not yet know what it wanted. He kept the illegals program. He kept Yasenevo. He kept the analytical pipelines and the rezidentura network and the full institutional staff. He added diplomatic sophistication: he was the first SVR director with genuine foreign policy experience, and he ran the service less as a secret police operation than as a foreign policy instrument — more like the BND or the DGSE than like the KGB it had been. He served until 1996, when Yeltsin appointed him Foreign Minister — a trajectory that had not been available to KGB chairmen in the Soviet system. He later became Prime Minister. In his directorial photographs from the early 1990s he looks like a distinguished Russian academic and diplomat of the Brezhnev-era foreign policy establishment, slightly rumpled in the Eastern European academic tradition, with the expression of a man who has thought carefully about the situation. The institution he rebuilt survived him by several decades. So did its methods.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Shamil Basayev — The FSB's Asymmetric Problem
FSB / GRU Adversary Assessment · Chechnya; Russia · 1991–2006

Shamil Basayev represented, from the perspective of the FSB and its predecessor KGB successor services, the Cold War's most uncomfortable post-Soviet intelligence challenge: an adversary who understood Russian security service methods at a sophisticated level, operated within Russian state territory, and demonstrated repeatedly that the tools built for Soviet-era counterintelligence and counterinsurgency were inadequate to contain him. Basayev had first come to Russian intelligence attention during the Georgian civil conflicts of the early 1990s, where Chechen fighters participated on the Abkhazian side in ways that suggested coordination with Russian military intelligence — a murky episode that reflected the chaotic intelligence politics of the immediate post-Soviet period. His subsequent emergence as the most capable Chechen military commander during the First Chechen War (1994-1996) demonstrated the catastrophic failure of Russian military and intelligence assessment of the Chechen resistance: the FSB had not tracked his organizational capacity, the GRU had not assessed his tactical sophistication, and the military entered Grozny in January 1995 with intelligence that was not merely inadequate but actively wrong about the nature of what they would face. The 1995 Budyonnovsk hospital siege — in which Basayev led approximately 150 fighters 300 kilometers into Russian territory, seized a hospital with 1,500 hostages, and negotiated a cessation of Russian military operations in exchange for his withdrawal — was the single most humiliating failure of Russian internal security in the post-Soviet period, exposing the impossibility of defending a large, diverse country against an adversary willing to carry the war into the heartland. Russian intelligence spent the following decade in active pursuit; Basayev was eventually killed in Ingushetia in 2006 by an FSB operation that remains officially described as an ambush.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
CIA Moscow Station After the Coup — Operating in Post-Soviet Russia
CIA Soviet/East European Division / Central Eurasia Division · Moscow · 1991–1995

The August 1991 coup attempt against Gorbachev and its failure created an immediate operational challenge for CIA Moscow Station that was simultaneously practical and conceptual: the KGB — the adversary institution against which Moscow Station had been operating for its entire existence — had been formally dissolved in October 1991, but the personnel, files, and operational capabilities of the KGB had been distributed among successor services whose missions, leadership, and operational priorities were uncertain. CIA Moscow Station's operating environment after December 1991 was technically transformed — the foreign surveillance and counterintelligence mission of the KGB's Second Chief Directorate was now the FSK's (Federal Counterintelligence Service) responsibility — but in practice the new Russian counterintelligence services were staffed by the same people, operating in the same buildings, using the same technical infrastructure, and pursuing largely the same operational priorities as the KGB units they had formally replaced. The CIA's analytical problem — who now ran Russian intelligence, what were their priorities, how much of the KGB institutional culture survived — was also an operational problem, because the counterintelligence environment Moscow Station officers were working in was operated by people who had been trained by the KGB and who maintained the KGB's institutional knowledge of CIA officer identities and tradecraft. The early 1990s also created new collection requirements: the Yeltsin government's relationship with the CIA was formally cooperative in ways the Soviet government had never been — there were official intelligence exchanges, cooperative programs on nuclear materials security, and official contacts at levels that would have been impossible during the Cold War — while the operational counterintelligence environment remained adversarial. CIA officers in Moscow in 1992-1995 were simultaneously participants in official liaison relationships with Russian successors and targets of those same services' counterintelligence operations. The 1994 arrest of Aldrich Ames demonstrated that the KGB's penetration of CIA had compromised Moscow Station's operations for nearly a decade — making the early post-Soviet period in Moscow a period when American intelligence was operating in the ruins of its own compromised network, trying to rebuild while the services that had run the penetration were reconstituting themselves. In the new Moscow of the early 1990s — the opened borders, the foreign businessmen, the rapid-turnover governments — the CIA's presence looked like all the other Western institutional presences. The counterintelligence competition beneath it was unchanged.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Nikolai Patrushev — The FSB Director
FSB / Security Council · Moscow · FSB Director 1999–2008; Security Council Secretary 2008–present

Nikolai Platonovich Patrushev rose through the KGB's Leningrad apparatus alongside Vladimir Putin, serving as a counterintelligence officer and later as an FSB department director before succeeding Putin as FSB director in August 1999 when Putin became Prime Minister. His tenure as FSB director from 1999 to 2008 covered the Second Chechen War, the apartment bombings that preceded it, the Beslan school siege, the Litvinenko murder, and the period of consolidation in which the FSB rebuilt the authority and reach that had been reduced under the FSK in the early 1990s. Patrushev's FSB was the institutional executor of the security services' reassertion of institutional power under Putin's presidency: the Chechen counterinsurgency gave the FSB operational authority that combined domestic security, counterintelligence, and paramilitary functions; the criminal investigation authority the FSB accumulated through the 2000s allowed it to intervene in commercial disputes, business acquisitions, and political contests in ways that made the line between law enforcement and political operation indistinct. His subsequent role as Secretary of the Security Council (the Russian equivalent of the National Security Council, which he has led since 2008) made him the most enduring figure in Russian national security affairs after Putin himself. Western intelligence services that monitored his career through the 1990s and 2000s found him significant not for tactical intelligence value — he generated little useful insight into specific Russian operations — but for his role as an institutional continuity figure: the person who most clearly embodied the KGB-to-FSB transition's essential character, which was not transformation but consolidation of authority under new institutional names. The Litvinenko inquiry's finding that Patrushev "probably" approved the polonium assassination alongside Putin placed him at the center of the most consequential intelligence operation the reconstituted Russian services had conducted on British soil since the Cold War. In the Security Council photographs — Patrushev in suits at the long table, the other siloviki around him — he is the figure who connects the current Russian security state to the KGB whose institutional culture and methods he inherited. He does not look like a transitional figure. He looks like a continuity one.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Boris Berezovsky — The Oligarch's Security Services Nexus
Logovaz Group / ORT / Russian Federation · Moscow; London · 1990–2013

Boris Berezovsky emerged from the Soviet academic establishment — a mathematician with a doctorate from Moscow State University and a position at the Institute of Control Sciences — to become, by the mid-1990s, one of Russia's wealthiest men through a series of privatization acquisitions and business arrangements whose mechanisms illustrated the specific relationship between the security services and the oligarch class in the Yeltsin period. His central assets included Logovaz, the automobile dealership that served as his initial capital accumulation vehicle; a controlling stake in ORT, the state television network that was the primary media instrument of Yeltsin's 1996 reelection campaign and through which Berezovsky exercised political influence that no businessmen in Western democracies possessed; and interests in Sibneft, the oil company whose privatization through the 1996 loans-for-shares program has been described as the single most significant transfer of public assets to private hands in Russian history at below-market valuation. Berezovsky's relationship with the FSB was the subject of intense Western intelligence interest: he was close to Nikolai Patrushev, who became FSB director in 1999; he had financial relationships with FSB officers that crossed the line between professional liaison and informal partnership; and he claimed, after his political break with Putin and his exile to London beginning in 2000, that elements of the FSB had orchestrated the 1999 Moscow apartment bombings as a pretext for the Second Chechen War and for Putin's political consolidation. The apartment bombing allegations — which Berezovsky promoted through media channels he controlled — were extensively investigated by Russian human rights organizations and the Litvinenko inquiry, which found the allegations credible but could not confirm them evidentiary. Berezovsky was found dead in his bathroom in Ascot on March 23, 2013, with a ligature around his neck. The British inquest returned an open verdict. He was, at the time of his death, the subject of multiple Russian criminal proceedings and the most prominent opponent of the Putin government living in exile. In the photographs from his Moscow years of influence — the silver-haired oligarch at Kremlin functions, the man at the center of everything — he looks precisely like what the Russian power structure he was photographed in looked like: indistinguishable from the other men in those rooms, until suddenly he wasn't in those rooms at all.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
The Soviet Nomenklatura After 1991 — The Elite's Transition
Soviet Union / Russian Federation · Moscow; former Soviet republics · 1991–2000

The Soviet nomenklatura — the system of approved appointments that defined the ruling class of the USSR — encompassed approximately 750,000 positions across the party, state, military, and security apparatus: the list of posts whose occupants required Central Committee approval and through which the CPSU controlled every institution of consequence in Soviet society. The people who held these positions in 1991 were the Soviet Union's governing elite by definition, and their transition to the post-Soviet order was one of the most consequential social processes of the late twentieth century. The outcomes were neither uniform nor just. Some nomenklatura members lost everything: party secretaries whose positions had no successor institution in the new order, ideological officials whose functional roles ceased to exist when the institution that had defined those roles dissolved. Others made the transition with their social capital largely intact — or enhanced. Industrial ministers became directors of the enterprises they had previously administered, in some cases negotiating terms with themselves as they moved from one desk to the other. Regional party secretaries became governors of newly constituted administrative regions covering the same geography. State-enterprise managers participated directly in the privatization of the assets they managed, using access to internal accounting records and government contracts to position themselves advantageously in the voucher auctions of 1992–1994. Security service officials occupied a distinctive position: KGB officers whose skills and contacts remained valuable in the new private economy moved from state intelligence into private intelligence, corporate security, financial investigation, or direct participation in the oligarch networks that were assembling control over Soviet industrial assets. The speed with which former nomenklatura members adapted to market conditions — the same people who had spent careers managing a planned economy became the most aggressive practitioners of privatized capitalism — told CIA and academic analysts something important about what the Soviet system had actually selected for: not ideological believers who required a party structure to function, but pragmatic operators who could manage bureaucratic and political environments and who transferred that competence without difficulty to the new environment. The ideology had been largely instrumental. When the instruments changed, so did they. The photographs of former nomenklatura officials at privatization hearings in 1992 and at press conferences announcing business acquisitions in 1994 show the same faces: the suits changed. The faces didn't.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Gen. Leonid Shebarshin — One Day as Chairman
KGB First Chief Directorate chief; Acting KGB Chairman · Yasenevo; Moscow · February 1989–August 1991

Shebarshin ran the KGB's foreign intelligence directorate from February 1989 through the August 1991 coup — the most consequential two and a half years in the history of Soviet foreign intelligence. He read the reports from Gordievsky's case as it closed. He supervised the rezidenturas that were receiving material from Ames. He was at Yasenevo when the Berlin Wall came down in November 1989 and when German reunification eliminated the HVA and the entire East German intelligence apparatus. On August 19, 1991, when the Emergency Committee announced its coup and Gorbachev was placed under house arrest in Crimea, Shebarshin was managing the FCD's response to an event that was simultaneously a constitutional crisis and a potential earthquake that could end the organization he ran. When the coup collapsed three days later and Kryuchkov was arrested at Vnukovo Airport on August 21, Yeltsin moved to dissolve the KGB by decree. For approximately twenty-four hours on August 22, 1991, Shebarshin was Acting Chairman of the KGB — the last officer to hold that title in the Soviet Union. Yeltsin appointed Vadim Bakatin as KGB Chairman the following day, tasked with dismantling the organization. Shebarshin retired and founded a private intelligence consultancy in Moscow that provided geopolitical and commercial analysis to Russian corporations — the first generation of the post-Soviet intelligence advisory industry. He wrote careful memoirs and gave thoughtful interviews. He was known for precise, slightly melancholy assessments of Russian foreign policy and Western misunderstandings of Russia. He died on March 30, 2012, of a gunshot wound in his Moscow apartment. The circumstances were ruled a suicide. He was 77. In his photographs from the FCD years — at Yasenevo, at diplomatic events — he looks like a Soviet diplomat of the Gromyko generation: composed, slightly formal, the expression of a man who has processed a great deal of information and arrived at his conclusions. He had. The organization he had run for two and a half years outlasted him by several decades under a different name.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Operation LUCH — KGB Networks in the Baltic States
KGB · Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania · 1988–1991; successor operations through 1990s

As the Baltic independence movements grew from cultural and political campaigns in 1988–1989 into formal declarations of independence in 1990, the KGB's residencies in Vilnius, Riga, and Tallinn were tasked with building intelligence networks in the emerging independence movements — identifying their leaders, penetrating their organizations, assessing their external contacts with Western governments and émigré communities, and determining the degree to which CIA and other Western services were providing operational support. Operation LUCH (Beam) was the KGB program for this purpose. The operation ran into the same fundamental problem that Soviet domestic intelligence encountered everywhere that the population had decided the Soviet system was over: potential informants and agents were making their own calculations about which side they were on, and an increasing number concluded that cooperating with the KGB was a longer-term liability than declining to cooperate. The networks the KGB had built in the Baltic states during the Soviet decades — informants in the political and academic establishments, penetrations of cultural institutions — were not performing as expected because the people running them were in the same political environment as everyone else. Some informants began providing false information. Others established contact with the independence movements and attempted to leverage their KGB relationships for protection. The KGB files from the Baltic residencies — partially destroyed, partially preserved, partially transferred to Russian Federation archives — were recovered and partially made public by Estonian, Latvian, and Lithuanian intelligence services after independence. They documented both the scope of Soviet penetration of Baltic society and its declining effectiveness as Soviet authority collapsed. After Baltic independence in September 1991, Russian intelligence continued running networks in the former Soviet republics — now redesignated foreign intelligence operations — through the SVR. The post-Soviet intelligence relationship between Russia and the Baltic states has been a continuous feature of their security environment since 1991.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
HVA — The Archive and the End
HVA (Hauptverwaltung Aufklärung) · East Berlin; West Germany · 1990–1993

When the Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1989, the HVA — the Stasi's foreign intelligence directorate — had approximately 4,000 officers running what it considered highly successful penetration operations against the West German government, the BND, and NATO institutions. Markus Wolf's successor Werner Großmann was its director. The HVA's response to the Wall's fall was not to stop operations but to accelerate the destruction of files that would expose its networks — and, simultaneously, to establish the organizational survival arrangements that would allow as many officers as possible to transition into the unified German civilian economy before the files could be fully analyzed. The HVA's files were partially destroyed in the days after November 9: burn barrels in East Berlin's offices, shredding equipment working around the clock, the same destruction operation that the broader Stasi was running across East Germany. The citizens who occupied MfS offices in January 1990 arrived in many cases after significant destruction had already occurred. What survived from the HVA's records was fragmentary: the central registry of agents and assets was partially preserved; the operational case files were more thoroughly destroyed. In 1990, as West German and CIA teams worked through the surviving fragments, the scale of HVA penetrations of West German institutions became visible in a way that had not been fully understood even by CIA's own assessments of East German capabilities. Gast was identified in October 1990. Rupp was identified later. Others were identified, warned through back channels, and allowed to disappear. The unified German state, BfV, and CIA engaged in a sustained effort through the early 1990s to reconstruct what the HVA's files had contained, using surviving fragments, informant accounts, and the KGB files that Vasili Mitrokhin had copied. The full reconstruction was never completed. The HVA destroyed more than it preserved. In that respect, it succeeded at its final operation.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Soviet Nuclear Materials — The 12th Chief Directorate Legacy
12th Chief Directorate, KGB / Ministry of Atomic Energy · Former Soviet territory · 1991–1995

The Soviet Union's dissolution created the most serious nuclear security crisis since the advent of the atomic age: approximately 30,000 nuclear warheads, scattered across the former Soviet republics of Russia, Ukraine, Kazakhstan, and Belarus, secured by an apparatus that had been built on the assumption of a unified state and was now fractured among competing successor governments with different levels of competence, different relationships with the remnants of Soviet security structures, and different degrees of financial desperation. The KGB's 12th Chief Directorate had been responsible for nuclear weapons physical security within the Soviet military — maintaining the elaborate system of two-person control rules, permissive action link equivalents, and armed guard forces that kept the weapons under political control. The 12th Directorate's successor functions were distributed among several Russian agencies after 1991, creating gaps and overlaps that Western intelligence services assessed with considerable alarm. The material security problem was distinct from the weapons problem: the Soviet civilian nuclear program had produced and stored enormous quantities of highly enriched uranium and weapons-grade plutonium in facilities that had neither the physical security infrastructure nor the financial resources to maintain adequate protection. Senator Sam Nunn and Senator Richard Lugar's Cooperative Threat Reduction program — the Nunn-Lugar initiative — was the American legislative response, providing funding for Russian nuclear security improvements that the Russian government could not itself finance. CIA and DIA tracked nuclear material movements, assessed facility security, and monitored for indicators of diversion — an intelligence priority that had no Cold War precedent and for which the community had to develop new methodologies essentially in real time. The period from 1991 to approximately 1995 represented the maximum vulnerability window; no catastrophic diversion was ever publicly confirmed, though the full accounting of that period remains incomplete in the public record.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
KGB Alpha — Vilnius TV Tower, January 1991
KGB Directorate A (Alpha Group) / Soviet Army · Vilnius, Lithuanian SSR · January 13, 1991

On January 13, 1991, KGB Alpha Group and Soviet Army paratroopers assaulted the Lithuanian TV tower in Vilnius at 2:00 a.m. Fourteen Lithuanian civilians who had gathered to defend the tower as a symbol of national sovereignty were killed. The assault was ordered by the Soviet military command and, the evidence suggests, authorized at the Kremlin level — though specific authorization was subsequently disputed in the pattern of every atrocity committed by Soviet forces against civilian populations, where responsibility always migrated away from the decision-makers as the political consequences became clear. Alpha Group's participation in Vilnius was different in character from its Kabul operations of December 1979. In Kabul, Alpha had operated against a foreign government under conditions of overt military conflict. In Vilnius, Alpha personnel were killing civilians — people who had come to the TV tower with cameras and lit candles — in a Soviet republic that had voted for independence with 90 percent of the electorate. Lithuania had declared independence in March 1990; Moscow had been applying economic pressure and political coercion for ten months without reversing the declaration. The January 13 assault was the military option. It killed fourteen people, failed to suppress Lithuanian independence, and accelerated both the delegitimization of the Soviet state and the demoralization of the Soviet security services.
