Eight months later, when the Emergency Committee staged its coup in Moscow and tasked Alpha Group to assault the Russian White House — a much more significant mission — Alpha Group refused. The officers who voted not to assault the White House in August had participated in, or were institutional colleagues of those who had participated in, the Vilnius tower assault eight months earlier. In the photographs from January 13 in Vilnius — the crowds at the tower in darkness, Soviet armored vehicles on Lithuanian streets, the civilians who had chosen to stand in front of them — it looks exactly like what it was: a state using violence against its own people to prevent them from leaving. That use of violence ended the state's legitimacy faster than the independence it was meant to prevent.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
KGB Alpha Group — The Vote, August 1991
KGB Directorate A · Moscow · August 19–21, 1991

On August 19, 1991, the Emergency Committee that had seized power from Mikhail Gorbachev tasked Alpha Group with arresting Boris Yeltsin, who was barricaded in the Russian Parliament building—the White House—with tens of thousands of civilians gathered outside. Alpha Group assessed the mission. Their internal estimate was 500 civilian casualties minimum to execute it. They could do it. They had stormed the Tajbeg Palace in forty-five minutes in 1979 and eliminated the head of a sovereign state. On the afternoon of August 20, Alpha Group's officers voted—by show of hands, inside their unit—not to carry out the order. They notified KGB leadership that the mission was not feasible without a direct written order from the President of the Soviet Union. Gorbachev was under house arrest in Crimea and was not writing orders. The coup collapsed the following day. The unit that had ended the career of one head of state in forty-five minutes had decided, by vote, that this particular mission was not one they were going to execute. The Soviet Union dissolved four months later. In photographs from this period, Alpha Group officers look like men in their thirties and forties at an athletic facility. They do not look like the people who made that decision.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Viktor Barannikov — The Security Ministry
Ministry of Security of the Russian Federation · Moscow · December 1991 – September 1993

Viktor Barannikov was one of the least remembered figures in the post-Soviet security transition but one of the most significant: as the founding and only minister of the Ministry of Security of the Russian Federation (MB RF), he oversaw the brief and turbulent period when the KGB's domestic successor was called something different, organized differently, and politically contested in ways that the more stable FSB that followed it would not be. The MB RF was established in December 1991 to replace the Russian Republic's KGB, absorbing the functions of counterintelligence, internal security, and surveillance that the all-union KGB had performed. Barannikov, who had come up through the Soviet Ministry of Internal Affairs rather than the KGB itself, found himself running an organization whose personnel were largely KGB veterans, whose institutional culture he could not fully control, and whose political masters — Yeltsin and his circle — were deeply uncertain about what role the security services should play in the new Russia. The October 1993 constitutional crisis, in which the parliamentary opposition barricaded itself in the White House, forced a choice: Barannikov sided with the opposition, not Yeltsin. He was dismissed. The Ministry of Security was dissolved three weeks later, replaced by the Federal Counterintelligence Service (FSK) and then by the FSB. Barannikov's brief tenure illustrated the degree to which the post-Soviet security transition was not a clean institutional reform but a political struggle within the security services themselves, with the outcome — the FSB under Putin — reflecting the specific balance of forces that had emerged by the mid-1990s rather than any planned design.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
The GKChP — The August Coup, 1991
State Emergency Committee of the USSR · Moscow; Foros, Crimea · August 19–21, 1991

On August 18, 1991, a delegation of senior Soviet officials flew to Gorbachev's vacation compound at Foros in Crimea and presented him with an ultimatum: sign a declaration of a state of emergency and transfer power, or accept house arrest. Gorbachev refused. The following morning, August 19, the State Emergency Committee — the Gosudarstvenny Komitet po Chrezvychaynomu Polozheniyu, GKChP — announced on Soviet television and radio that Gorbachev was ill and that the Committee had assumed governing authority. Its members were Vice President Gennady Yanayev, who nominally led it; Prime Minister Valentin Pavlov; Interior Minister Boris Pugo; Defense Minister Dmitri Yazov; KGB Chairman Vladimir Kryuchkov; and four senior officials from industry and agriculture. Tanks moved into Moscow. The plotters had planned badly and miscalculated the political environment in several fundamental ways: they had not arrested Boris Yeltsin before he reached the Russian White House parliament building, where he climbed onto a tank in the image that became the coup's defining symbol. KGB Alpha Group was ordered to storm the building; its officers met, deliberated, and voted — a vote whose outcome is documented elsewhere in this compendium — not to comply. Vympel also refused its assigned assault role. Marshal Yazov ordered the tanks withdrawn on the evening of August 20. By the morning of August 21 the coup had collapsed without a sustained attempt to execute the violence that its success would have required. Kryuchkov and the Committee members were arrested at Vnukovo Airport on August 21 as they attempted to fly to Crimea. Pugo shot himself that afternoon. Akhromeyev, who had rushed to Moscow to support the coup when it was announced, hanged himself in his Kremlin office on August 24. Yanayev sat at the Committee's August 19 press conference with visibly shaking hands. The photographic record of that press conference — eight officials in dark suits at a long table, the shaking hands, the expressions of men who already understood the operation had failed — is the document of the decision that ended the Soviet Union. They did not intend to end it. They intended to save it. They ended it instead, by demonstrating conclusively that the forces required to defend it had already decided it was not worth defending.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Col. Aleksandr Zaporozhsky — CIA's Moscow Asset
SVR (Foreign Intelligence Service) / CIA asset · Moscow; Washington, D.C. · 1994–2001

Aleksandr Zaporozhsky was a colonel in the SVR — the Russian Foreign Intelligence Service that succeeded the KGB's First Chief Directorate — who began providing information to CIA in 1994, meeting his handlers while posted abroad and continuing cooperation after returning to Moscow. The precise scope of his reporting has never been fully disclosed; what has been established through Russian court records, American government statements, and reporting by former intelligence officials is that Zaporozhsky's access encompassed SVR counterintelligence operations against American intelligence — which would have given CIA visibility into SVR efforts to penetrate CIA and identify CIA's assets inside Russian intelligence. He is credited in intelligence community circles with providing the initial information that allowed CIA to focus its investigation on Robert Hanssen, the FBI special agent who had been providing KGB and SVR with American intelligence information since 1979. Whether Zaporozhsky identified Hanssen directly or provided information that allowed CIA analysts to narrow their investigation is disputed; what is clear is that his information was among the inputs that led to Hanssen's arrest in February 2001. After retiring from SVR, Zaporozhsky moved to the United States, which made him a target for a lure operation. In late 2001 he was induced to return to Moscow — the pretense for the trip has never been publicly confirmed — and was arrested immediately on arrival. He was tried in secret for treason, convicted, and sentenced to eighteen years. He served six before being included in the July 2010 spy exchange at Vienna airport — the exchange that also returned Anna Chapman and nine other Russian SVR officers arrested in the United States in June 2010. In his available photographs — a large, unremarkable man in the clothes of a professional in late middle age — he looks nothing like what he was: one of the most productive American assets inside Russian intelligence in the post-Cold War period.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
The FSK — Federal Counterintelligence Service
Federalnaya Sluzhba Kontrrazvedki · Moscow · 1991–1995

Between the dissolution of the KGB in December 1991 and the establishment of the Federal Security Service (FSB) in April 1995, Russian domestic counterintelligence was housed in a transitional institution called the Federal Counterintelligence Service — the FSK. The dissolution of the KGB under Vadim Bakatin in December 1991 split the KGB's functions among several successor services: foreign intelligence to the SVR, signals intelligence to FAPSI, border guards to a separate service, and domestic security and counterintelligence to what became the FSK. The FSK was smaller than the KGB had been and nominally more constrained — the post-putsch political environment made the security service's public role sensitive in a way it had not been under the Soviet system. Three directors served in the FSK's four-year existence: Viktor Barannikov (dismissed after involvement in the 1993 constitutional crisis), Nikolai Golushko, and Sergei Stepashin. The institutional instability of the FSK period reflected the broader instability of the Yeltsin government: the service changed directors three times in four years, operated under budget constraints that were severe by KGB standards, lost officers to the private security industry, and struggled to define a mission appropriate to a country that was nominally no longer an adversary of the West. The transformation of the FSK into the FSB in 1995 coincided with the beginning of the First Chechen War and the political rehabilitation of the security service as a necessary instrument of state power. Vladimir Putin, appointed FSB director in 1998, would demonstrate that the institutional continuity the FSK preserved — the officers, the files, the operational methods, the Lubyanka headquarters — had survived the transition without fundamental disruption to the enterprise. The FSK photographs — new organizational letterhead on old stationery, faces in offices that had been KGB offices two years earlier — look like exactly what they were: the same institution under different management, waiting for its authority to be restored.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Vympel — The Second Refusal, October 1993
Russian Federal Counterintelligence Service (former KGB) · Moscow · October 4, 1993

In October 1993, the constitutional crisis between Boris Yeltsin and the Russian Parliament produced a second standoff at the Moscow White House—the same building where, two years earlier, Alpha Group had voted not to arrest Yeltsin. This time Yeltsin was using force. After army tanks shelled the parliament building on October 4th and opened a breach, Vympel—which had survived the Soviet Union's dissolution as a Russian Federation security unit—was ordered to enter the building and secure it. Vympel's commanders refused, assessing the order as a political mission rather than a counterterrorism one. The building was secured by other forces. Two days later, Yeltsin signed an order transferring Vympel to the Ministry of Internal Affairs—effectively disbanding it from the intelligence apparatus it had been built to serve. Most of the unit's officers resigned rather than serve under MVD authority. The unit that had been created for wartime deep-penetration operations against NATO targets, and had trained for a decade to be the most capable sabotage force in the Soviet arsenal, was disbanded because it declined a political assignment. Alpha Group had refused an order in 1991 and survived. Vympel refused an order in 1993 and did not. In their final unit photographs at Balashikha, they look like men at the end of something. The photographs do not explain what that something was.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Gen. Pavel Grachev — The Chechen Miscalculation
Russian Ministry of Defense · Moscow; Grozny · 1992–1996

Pavel Grachev became the first Defense Minister of the Russian Federation in May 1992, appointed by Boris Yeltsin in part because Grachev had been loyally decisive during the August 1991 coup — as Commander of the VDV (Airborne Troops), he had refused to deploy paratroopers against the Russian White House when coup plotters requested it and instead aligned with Yeltsin, a decision that may have been the single most consequential military choice of the coup's seventy-two hours. He was a decorated Afghan veteran, commander of the 103rd Guards Airborne Division during the Kabul airport operations in December 1979 and through the war, holder of the Hero of the Soviet Union award for his Afghanistan service. The Defense Ministry he inherited was in organizational dissolution: Soviet military forces were dividing along national lines across fourteen former republics, nuclear weapons were distributed across multiple newly independent states, officer corps morale had collapsed under the combined weight of Afghanistan, the dissolution, and the discontinuity between the military's self-image as the Soviet state's indispensable institution and the speed with which the Soviet state had ceased to exist. Grachev managed this inheritance while conducting a simultaneous withdrawal of Russian forces from Eastern Europe — 500,000 troops, their families, and their equipment returning to a country that had not built housing for them — and navigating the domestic political turbulences that included the October 1993 constitutional crisis, where he personally ordered tanks to fire on the parliament building. In November 1994 he told journalists that a single Russian airborne regiment could take Grozny, the capital of the breakaway Chechen Republic, in two hours. Russian forces entered Grozny on New Year's Eve 1994 in armored columns without infantry support, without coherent urban warfare doctrine, and without the combined-arms integration that the operation required. The 131st Maikop Brigade drove its vehicles to the central train station, where Chechen fighters in basements and upper floors destroyed the column at close range over eighteen hours. Russian military dead in January 1995 alone are estimated at one to two thousand. Grachev resigned as Defense Minister in June 1996, following Yeltsin's reelection and the negotiated Russian withdrawal from Chechnya. His boast had been wrong by approximately two years and more than ten thousand Russian lives.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
FSB Filtration — Intelligence Screening in Chechnya
FSB / MVD · Chechnya; North Caucasus · 1994–1996; 1999–2009

Filtration operations — the systematic detention and interrogation of Chechen males at checkpoints, holding facilities, and purpose-built camps throughout the First and Second Chechen Wars — were the FSB's primary mechanism for human intelligence collection and counterintelligence screening in the Chechen theater. The concept: all men between approximately 15 and 60 passing through checkpoints were detained, their documents checked against FSB and MVD databases, and their identities, movements, and associations interrogated. Those who could not adequately account for their whereabouts during the preceding period of active conflict — or whose names appeared on targeting lists, or whose interrogators assessed as suspicious for any reason — were detained in filtration camps for further processing. The human rights record of filtration camps was documented by Memorial, Human Rights Watch, and the Council of Europe in exhaustive and consistent detail: beatings, torture, enforced disappearances, extrajudicial killings. As an intelligence collection mechanism, filtration was blunt and often counterproductive: the mass detention and mistreatment of civilian males generated a reservoir of grievance that extended the insurgency's potential recruiting base and produced conditions under which accurate intelligence was difficult to distinguish from coerced false statements. But it also produced actionable intelligence: information about arms caches, insurgent routes, cell structures, and the identities of mid-level commanders that more precise operations could then target. The FSB's filtration apparatus in Chechnya represented a specific post-Soviet adaptation: an intelligence service that had built its institutional methods on population surveillance in a closed society attempting to apply those methods to an active insurgency in a partially open one, with the result that the methods that had worked in Soviet conditions — comprehensive screening of populations who could not resist — worked against an insurgency that had an alternative to surrendering to that screening. The Second Chechen War's filtration operations were more methodically coordinated than the First, incorporating lessons about which detainee profiles were likely to produce actionable intelligence and which were producing noise. The coordination between FSB's screening apparatus and GRU's targeting intelligence improved substantially between the two campaigns. The human cost of the improvement was not recorded in Russian official sources.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
GRU Spetsnaz — First Chechen War, Grozny
Russian Armed Forces · Grozny, Chechnya · December 1994–August 1996

Defense Minister Pavel Grachev told the press in November 1994 that a single airborne regiment could take Grozny in two hours. On New Year's Eve, Russian forces entered the city in armored columns without infantry support, without coherent air coordination, and without the kind of urban warfare doctrine the mission required. The 131st Maikop Brigade drove its armored vehicles into the city center and stopped at the central train station, where Chechen fighters — positioned in basements and upper floors with RPG-7s — destroyed the column at close range over the following eighteen hours. Estimates of Russian military dead in January 1995 alone range from one thousand to two thousand. The army that had defeated Nazi Germany, maintained nuclear parity with the United States for forty years, and garrisoned Eastern Europe through the entire Cold War era was being destroyed by will and rocket-propelled grenades in the rubble of a provincial city. GRU Spetsnaz and Interior Ministry special forces performed significantly better than line units — they trained for small-team urban operations, they adapted faster, they avoided the armored-column tactics that the regular army kept using after those tactics had demonstrably failed — but they could not compensate for the structural collapse everywhere else. The 1996 Khasavyurt Accords acknowledged Russian defeat after twenty-one months of war. Grachev's two-hour estimate had been wrong by approximately two years and ten thousand Russian lives. The GRU Spetsnaz who fought in the rubble of Grozny in January 1995 had trained their entire careers for war against NATO in Central Europe. They had not trained for this. The men who survived Grozny came out with a different set of operational lessons. The Russian Army that returned to Chechnya in 1999 was meaningfully different from the one that had entered in 1994. The men who made it different had been in the rubble in January 1995, and they remembered what had not worked.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Gen. Alexander Lebed — 14th Army, Transnistria
Russian Army · Tiraspol, Transnistria (Moldova) · 1992–1995

Lebed commanded the 14th Guards Army stationed in Transnistria — the strip of predominantly Russian-speaking eastern Moldova that had declared independence after the Soviet dissolution, backed by Russian officers, Russian weapons, and the presence of 9,000 Russian soldiers under Lebed's command. When active fighting between Transnistrian forces and the Moldovan government erupted in 1992, Lebed was the decisive military factor in the conflict. He deployed his forces in July 1992 and told both sides, with characteristic directness, that if the shooting did not stop he would make it stop. It stopped. He was blunt in a way that post-Soviet Russian military figures had rarely been in public: he held press conferences, gave candid interviews to Western journalists, and said openly that Russian security interests required Russian forces in place. He was not performing. He was the template for the post-Soviet Russian military commander as political actor: technically competent, contemptuous of civilian politicians, genuinely popular with the men who served under him, potentially very dangerous to anyone who mistook his directness for simplicity. He ran for the Russian presidency in 1996, came in third, sold his endorsement to Yeltsin in exchange for a national security appointment, used that appointment to negotiate the Khasavyurt Accords ending the First Chechen War, and was dismissed by Yeltsin five months later for being too prominent and too independent. He died when his helicopter struck power lines in Siberia in April 2002. In his photographs from the Transnistrian period he looks like a large, gray, close-cropped Russian military officer at a checkpoint who is not satisfied with how the day is going. He was not. He never was, anywhere. This is accurate.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Gen. Anatoly Kulikov — MVD Internal Troops Commander
Russian Ministry of Internal Affairs (MVD) · Moscow; Grozny · 1992–1997

Anatoly Kulikov commanded the MVD Internal Troops — the Vnutrenniye Voiska, or VV — during the First Chechen War, then served as Interior Minister from 1995 to 1998, making him the senior Russian security official who had to reconcile the First Chechen War's military catastrophe with the political imperative of continuing the campaign. The MVD Internal Troops were constitutionally distinct from the Russian Army: they were the paramilitary force responsible for internal security operations — suppressing civil disorder, guarding strategic facilities, conducting cordon-and-search operations — and in Chechnya they bore the urban warfare mission that the regular Army had failed catastrophically in the initial January 1995 Grozny assault. Kulikov's Internal Troops were the units that fought block by block through Grozny in February and March 1995 after the regular Army's armored columns had been destroyed. The casualties were severe. The intelligence integration was inadequate. The VV's human intelligence collection capabilities — built for peacetime internal security operations, not for fighting an insurgency in rubble against fighters who knew every building — were insufficient for the mission they were executing. Kulikov's 1995 appointment as Interior Minister gave him authority over both the VV and the MVD militia at a moment when the First Chechen War was grinding through its second year, Chechen fighters were conducting major operations inside Russia itself (Budyonnovsk, the Pervomayskoe hostage crisis), and the intelligence architecture for the campaign was demonstrably inadequate. The Khasavyurt Accords of August 1996 — the negotiated Russian withdrawal that ended the First Chechen War — were acknowledged as a Russian defeat within the military and security establishment. Kulikov continued as Interior Minister through the early Putin period, responsible for the institutional rebuild that prepared the MVD and its intelligence functions for the Second Chechen War's more systematic campaign. In his photographs from the Chechen period — an MVD general in working dress at command posts in Grozny, the specific expression of a senior officer managing an operation that is going badly in circumstances he did not fully control — he looks like what he was: a professional who was handed a situation that was failing before he got there and was expected to make it work regardless.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
The Russian Oligarchs and Siloviki — Intelligence-Business Nexus
FSB / SVR / GRU — former officers · Moscow; London; Cyprus; Zurich · 1992–2000

The privatization of Soviet state enterprises between 1992 and 1998 — conducted through voucher auctions, insider loans-for-shares deals, and the systematic underpricing of assets whose true value was understood only by the small number of people with access to the accounting records — transferred controlling stakes in the Soviet economy's most productive assets to a group of businessmen who became known in Russian and Western press as oligarchs. The intelligence dimension of this process was not incidental. Former KGB and GRU officers participated directly in privatization, either as principals acquiring assets or as security directors for the oligarch groups that emerged from the auctions. The security services' access to the financial records, debt instruments, and legal vulnerabilities of the enterprises being privatized — gathered through decades of surveillance and economic intelligence collection — gave former officers an informational advantage in a process that rewarded information asymmetry. The SVR and FSB maintained relationships with the major oligarch groups that were simultaneously protective and controlling: intelligence on Western business partners, foreign government attitudes, and domestic political developments provided the businessmen something of value; in exchange, the services maintained access to international financial flows and foreign contacts that official services could not develop as rapidly through formal channels. By the late 1990s the line between the siloviki — the security service veterans who populated the Yeltsin and early Putin administrations — and the oligarch class had become blurred in ways that Western intelligence services were slow to assess. CIA's analytical product on Russia in the 1990s focused on political stability, nuclear security, and conventional military capability; the intelligence-business nexus that was actually determining Russian power structure received less systematic attention until it was fully established. The pattern that emerged — security service officers using institutional access to participate in privatization, and the oligarchs who emerged using former intelligence officers as fixers, protectors, and political intermediaries — became the structural template for the political economy Putin inherited and systematized after 1999. In the photographs of the oligarchs from the early 1990s — men in Western suits at Moscow press conferences announcing their acquisition of state enterprises — the figures who are not at the press conference are the ones whose institutional roles shaped the outcome.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Rainer Rupp — TOPAZ at NATO Headquarters
HVA asset (TOPAZ); NATO Economic Directorate · Brussels · recruited 1968; arrested 1993

Rainer Rupp was a German student in 1968 when he was recruited by an HVA officer at a point of maximum political opportunity: a young man of leftist sympathies in the year of the Prague Spring and European student rebellions, persuaded that providing information to the socialist bloc was consistent with his values. He was 20. He worked briefly as a journalist, then joined the European Commission in Brussels, then transferred in the mid-1970s to NATO headquarters — also in Brussels — as an economic analyst. For the next seventeen years, operating as TOPAZ, he provided the HVA with intelligence on NATO's economic assessments, political decision-making processes, strategic analytical products that circulated at NATO headquarters, and — most significantly — his professional access to the internal deliberations of the alliance. Most historians who have examined his case conclude that his single most operationally consequential report came in November 1983, during the Able Archer crisis: he reported to Moscow that the Able Archer 83 exercise was exactly what it appeared to be, a NATO command-post exercise rather than the cover for a nuclear first strike that the KGB's RYAN network had been reporting as a possibility. Whether his reporting influenced the Soviet leadership's decision to stand down during the most dangerous intelligence confrontation of the Cold War is debated; what is documented is that his report was received and processed at the KGB First Chief Directorate level. German unification in 1990 opened the Stasi files. Investigators identified Rupp from his HVA case file. He was arrested in 1993, tried in German federal court, convicted of treason, and sentenced to twelve years — later reduced. In later interviews, after his release, he acknowledged that the system he had served had not proved worth his service but maintained that the values that had motivated him in 1968 were genuine. In the photographs from his NATO years — a mid-level Brussels official in the standard presentation of international civil servants, unremarkable in every dimension — he is invisible. For seventeen years, invisible had been sufficient.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
GRU Unit 29155 — Assassination and Destabilization
GRU (Main Intelligence Directorate) · Moscow · 1990s–present

GRU Unit 29155 is a covert Russian military intelligence unit whose existence and operational record became reconstructable by Western investigative journalists and intelligence services primarily through the forensic analysis of travel records, passport data, and physical evidence following the March 2018 novichok poisoning of Sergei Skripal and his daughter Yulia in Salisbury, England. The unit's two officers identified in the Salisbury attack — traveling under aliases "Petrov" and "Boshirov" and publicly confronted by Bellingcat's open-source analysis — were subsequently identified by investigative reporters as GRU colonels with specific unit affiliations. Subsequent reporting linked Unit 29155 to a broader pattern of operations across Europe during the 1990s and 2000s: the 2015 poisoning of Bulgarian arms dealer Emilian Gebrev with what Bulgarian investigators determined was a nerve agent; an alleged 2016 coup attempt in Montenegro intended to derail the country's NATO membership; a 2019 assassination in Berlin's Tiergarten park; and other operations whose attribution to GRU has been established at varying levels of confidence. The unit's operational mandate appears to focus on operations below the threshold of acknowledged military action — assassination of defectors and intelligence officers who have cooperated with Western services, destabilization of Eastern European governments considering closer Western alignment, and sabotage operations in European countries supporting Ukraine — with plausible deniability maintained through the use of cover identities and the GRU's institutional separation from the FSB's foreign operations directorate. The Salisbury attack failed in its presumed primary objective (Skripal survived) while creating an unprecedented forensic record of GRU covert operations methodology: the cover identities, the travel patterns, the weapons logistics, and the operational security failures that open-source investigators reconstructed from publicly accessible data. In the tourist photographs from Salisbury cathedral that "Petrov" and "Boshirov" published as their explanation for being in England that weekend — two men in ordinary tourist clothing, expressionless, not looking at the camera — GRU's operational methodology is represented in its working form.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Alexander Litvinenko — The Former FSB Officer
FSB (Federal Security Service) / Exile · Moscow; London · 1988–2006

Litvinenko was an FSB lieutenant colonel who in 1998 held a press conference with several colleagues and publicly accused senior FSB officials of ordering the assassination of oligarch Boris Berezovsky. The act was unprecedented: serving FSB officers making public, named accusations against their own institution's leadership. He was arrested, tried twice, acquitted, rearrested, and finally released in 2000, after which he defected to the United Kingdom with his family. In London he worked as a consultant for British intelligence, wrote a book accusing FSB of involvement in the 1999 apartment bombings that had served as the pretext for the Second Chechen War, and maintained an active public profile as a critic of the Putin security services. On November 1, 2006, he met two Russian contacts — Andrei Lugovoi and Dmitri Kovtun — at a London hotel and a sushi restaurant. He fell ill within hours. He was hospitalized on November 3 and diagnosed on November 19, three days before his death, with polonium-210 poisoning. Polonium-210 is a byproduct of nuclear reactor operation, is produced in significant quantities in Russia's nuclear complex, and was used to contaminate not only Litvinenko but the multiple locations where he and his visitors had been in the preceding days — a trail of radioactive contamination extending through London hotels, restaurants, and aircraft. The British public inquiry completed in 2016 concluded with high confidence that Lugovoi and Kovtun had administered the poison, and that the operation had "probably" been approved by Putin and Patrushev, then FSB director. Russia declined to extradite the suspects. In his hospital photographs in the final days — bald from radiation poisoning, connected to monitoring equipment, looking directly at the camera — Litvinenko left a statement that was widely reproduced. It was addressed, pointedly, to his killers.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Vasili Mitrokhin
KGB First Chief Directorate — Archivist · defected to UK, 1992

For twelve years, from 1972 to 1984, Mitrokhin was the KGB's chief archivist in the First Chief Directorate's foreign intelligence archive. He spent those years copying by hand the most sensitive operational records in Soviet intelligence history: agent networks, dead drops, clandestine meeting sites, technical operations, assassination plots, intelligence sources inside Western governments. He wrote them in tiny script on scraps of paper, hid them in his shoes, transferred them to typewritten pages at home, and buried them in milk canisters under his dacha. When the Soviet Union fell, he tried to give the archive to the CIA. They passed. He went to MI6. They sent a team to Estonia to dig up the milk canisters—six trunks of material—and flew him to England. His photographs show the world's most unremarkable man: cardigan, glasses, a face that has never caused anyone to look twice. He was carrying the most consequential intelligence archive of the Cold War under his vegetable garden.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Col. Stanislav Lunev — GRU Defector
GRU (Soviet/Russian Military Intelligence) · United States under cover · defected 1992

Lunev was the highest-ranking GRU officer to defect to the United States, crossing in 1992 — after the Soviet Union's dissolution but at a moment when Russian military intelligence was continuing operations with minimal institutional interruption under its renamed organizational identity. He had served as a GRU officer in the United States under diplomatic cover and had been tasked with — among other missions — identifying and pre-positioning caches of weapons and communications equipment for use by GRU agents in the event of war or a period when normal diplomatic access was severed. His debriefs confirmed the existence of this pre-positioning program and its general methodology. He testified before Congress in 1998 and described GRU's continued targeting of the United States after 1991, the operational continuity through the dissolution period, and GRU's ongoing interest in the RA-115 nuclear suitcase devices — portable nuclear demolition weapons whose existence he was among the first former Soviet intelligence officers to confirm publicly in a congressional setting. His book, "Through the Eyes of the Enemy," published in 1998, described GRU's collection priorities and tradecraft in terms that the American government did not publicly contradict. He lived in the United States under a protected identity for years following his defection. In congressional testimony photographs he looks like a middle-aged Russian businessman in a tie at an American conference table — which he was, in the only sense that mattered for that afternoon's session. The pre-positioned caches he had described, if they existed as located, were never publicly accounted for.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Gennady Vasilenko
KGB officer · Moscow; Guyana; Washington, DC · imprisoned by KGB 1988, 2005; freed 2010

Vasilenko was a KGB officer who was genuinely, innocently friends with CIA counterintelligence officer Jack Platt. The KGB did not believe this was possible. They arrested him in Guyana in 1988, brought him back to Moscow, and interrogated him. They arrested him again in 2005, after Aldrich Ames had been in American custody for a decade and Robert Hanssen had been arrested in 2001—and the KGB had, by process of elimination, concluded that Vasilenko must have been the source. He wasn't. He spent years in Lefortovo Prison. Robert De Niro, who had become friends with Vasilenko through Platt, advocated for his release. Vasilenko was freed in the June 2010 spy swap—the same one that brought Anna Chapman home. In every photograph from his KGB years, he looks like a Soviet physical education teacher, which was his cover. He was genuinely friends with a CIA officer. That was his whole crime.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Col. Sergei Skripal — The Career Arc
GRU / MI6 asset · Madrid; Moscow · recruited c. 1995 · arrested December 2004 · freed July 2010

Skripal was a GRU colonel who was recruited by MI6 during his posting in Madrid in the mid-1990s and provided Russian military intelligence for several years: the identities of GRU officers operating in Europe, Russian military capabilities assessments, the structure of Russian intelligence networks. He returned to Moscow, continued passing material, and was arrested by the FSB in December 2004. He was convicted of high treason in August 2006 and sentenced to thirteen years. He was released in July 2010 as part of the same spy swap that freed Gennady Vasilenko and returned Anna Chapman and nine other Russian illegals to Moscow — the exchange conducted at Vienna's Schwechat Airport in the same atmospheric grammar as the Glienicke Bridge exchanges fifty years earlier, with the same geography of men in overcoats crossing a neutral threshold in both directions. He settled in Salisbury, England. He fed ducks. He walked his dogs. He drove a used BMW. On March 4, 2018, he and his daughter Yulia were poisoned with Novichok nerve agent in Salisbury — a GRU-organized assassination attempt that nearly killed both of them, killed a local woman who came into contact with the discarded perfume bottle used to transport the agent, and produced the largest coordinated diplomatic expulsion of Russian intelligence officers in Western history since the Cold War. He and his daughter survived. The two GRU officers responsible were identified by British intelligence within days from CCTV footage; they appeared on Russian television claiming to be tourists who had come to Salisbury to admire the cathedral's spire. They looked, in those interviews, exactly like two men with something to explain who were trying very hard not to look like it. The cathedral's spire is 123 meters tall. Skripal's Salisbury photographs — a heavy, gray-haired man in a park, slightly stooped, entirely ordinary in appearance — are the photographs of the man his cover said he was. The cover was not false by the time those photographs were taken. He was that man. He had been that man for years. The GRU that trained him sent men to kill him anyway.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
The Soviet General Staff — Strategic Intelligence in the Final Years
Soviet General Staff / Main Intelligence Directorate · Moscow; military districts; forward command posts · 1985–1991

The Soviet General Staff in its final years — under Marshal Sergei Akhromeyev (Chief 1984-1988) and then General Mikhail Moiseyev (Chief 1988-1991) — was simultaneously managing the military's adaptation to Gorbachev's political reforms, the withdrawal from Afghanistan, the restructuring of Soviet conventional forces under the "defensive sufficiency" doctrine, and the enormous intelligence task of assessing American responses to the accelerating Soviet strategic retreat. The General Staff's intelligence function was formally lodged in the GRU — the Main Intelligence Directorate, which provided the General Staff with military intelligence — but the General Staff's analytical work on strategic questions drew on KGB products as well, through the sharing arrangements that connected the parallel Soviet intelligence architectures. The central analytical question for the General Staff through the late 1980s was whether Gorbachev's concessions — the INF Treaty elimination of the SS-20 force, the unilateral reduction of Soviet forces in Eastern Europe announced in December 1988, the acceptance of on-site arms control inspections — were producing reciprocal American restraint or were being exploited by American negotiators who recognized Soviet weakness and were extracting maximum concessions. General Staff war planners who had spent careers planning the correlation of forces in a potential NATO-Warsaw Pact conflict now found their planning assumptions invalidated by political decisions that eliminated the systems they had planned around. Akhromeyev's resignation in 1988 — he cited age and health, but the context was clear — reflected the professional military's discomfort with the pace of concessions. His subsequent role as military advisor to Gorbachev, and his participation in the August 1991 coup attempt and suicide in its aftermath, completed a trajectory from Soviet strategic planner to tragic figure of the system's collapse. The General Staff's intelligence assessments in the final Soviet years — the estimates of American intentions, the evaluations of NATO's response to Soviet force reductions, the analysis of whether CFE Treaty compliance would be reciprocal — were produced in an institution that could see the strategic retreat happening but could not stop or shape it through the intelligence function. In the General Staff's operations rooms in the final months before the Soviet flag came down — the maps of military districts that would become independent states' territories, the force structure charts of armies that would cease to exist — the intelligence function was assessing an environment whose most important development was the dissolution of the system that had created the General Staff itself.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Russian Intelligence in Central Asia — The Near Abroad Priority
FSB / SVR / GRU · Kazakhstan; Uzbekistan; Turkmenistan; Kyrgyzstan; Tajikistan · 1991–1995 and beyond

When the Soviet Union dissolved in December 1991, five of the fifteen successor states — Kazakhstan, Uzbekistan, Turkmenistan, Kyrgyzstan, and Tajikistan — were in Central Asia, a region that had never had significant independent statehood before the Soviet period and whose security institutions, political systems, and economic structures had been built entirely within the Soviet framework. The Russian Federation's intelligence services regarded the Central Asian states — along with Ukraine, Belarus, and the South Caucasus states — as the "near abroad": former Soviet territory in which Russia had legitimate security interests, requiring intelligence coverage and active engagement that went beyond normal foreign intelligence operations. In the Central Asian states specifically, this interest was reinforced by several factors: the presence of ethnic Russian minorities in Kazakhstan and other states; the location of former Soviet nuclear weapons facilities and missile test ranges (the Baikonur Cosmodrome remained in Kazakhstan, leased to Russia); the proximity of Afghanistan and the narcotics trafficking and Islamist extremism that the Soviet-Afghan war had intensified in the border regions; and the energy resources of the Caspian basin that Russian commercial and political interests sought to control. The FSB's engagement in Central Asia operated through multiple channels: formal liaison relationships with the newly established national security services of each state (which were largely staffed by former KGB personnel who had been the KGB's local cadre), the deployment of Russian intelligence officers under diplomatic and commercial cover in the capital cities, and in some cases direct presence at military facilities that remained under Russian operational control. The Tajik civil war (1992-1997), in which Russian 201st Motor Rifle Division troops and FSB advisors supported the pro-Russian faction against Islamist and nationalist opposition, was the most intensive early manifestation of Russian intelligence engagement in the near abroad — a model of direct military-intelligence involvement in a successor state's internal conflict that would be repeated in different forms in later post-Soviet conflicts. In the new presidential palaces and government ministries of Almaty, Tashkent, and Dushanbe, in the early 1990s — the newly-independent states trying to establish national security institutions from the inherited KGB cadre — the Russian liaison officers and intelligence advisors were, in many cases, the same people who had run the KGB offices these states had just inherited. The institutional transition and the intelligence presence were, in these capitals, difficult to distinguish.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
East German Military Intelligence — The NVA Nachrichtenwesen
NVA (Nationale Volksarmee) Military Intelligence / MfS coordination · East Berlin · 1956–1990; files transferred to German Military Archive 1990

The German Democratic Republic maintained a military intelligence apparatus within the Nationale Volksarmee (NVA) that operated separately from, though closely coordinated with, the Ministerium für Staatssicherheit's foreign intelligence arm, the HVA. The NVA's intelligence function — the Nachrichtenwesen, or intelligence service — was responsible for tactical and operational military intelligence on West German and NATO forces, order of battle assessment, electronic warfare, and the military intelligence support requirements of the Warsaw Pact's northern strategic grouping. The NVA was the most capable non-Soviet military in the Warsaw Pact: its forces were better equipped, better trained, and more operationally integrated with Soviet doctrine than those of Poland, Czechoslovakia, or Romania, a consequence of German military professionalism applied to socialist organizational models. Its intelligence apparatus reflected the same combination of German technical competence and Soviet methodological influence. The NVA Nachrichtenwesen's collection against West Germany's Bundeswehr was facilitated by the unique intelligence environment of the two German states: shared language, family connections across the inner-German border, and the deep HVA penetration of West German society created collection opportunities that other Warsaw Pact services could not replicate. The military intelligence and HVA functions were formally separate but operationally intertwined — both reported into the KGB liaison architecture that coordinated Warsaw Pact intelligence at the Soviet level, and both operated in complementary collection domains against the same target. After German reunification in October 1990, the NVA was dissolved and its personnel — those not dismissed outright — were reviewed for integration into the Bundeswehr or discharged. The NVA's intelligence files were transferred to the German Military Archive and later to the Gauck Authority (the agency managing Stasi files), where they became available to German investigators and historians after the initial decade-long review process for classified material. The files documented operational methods, collection priorities, and agent networks that had been targeted against the Bundeswehr for thirty-four years. In the NVA photographs from the Cold War decades — the officers in their Soviet-influenced uniforms at military exercises, at headquarters briefings, at Warsaw Pact coordination meetings — they look like what they were: East German military professionals of high competence serving a state that dissolved at the moment its military was defending it from enemies it had been preparing against for a generation. The Bundeswehr officers who reviewed the NVA intelligence files after reunification found detailed assessments of Bundeswehr capabilities that the West German military had not known existed. Some of the assessments were accurate. Some were not. The quality varied by collection domain in ways that reflected which access the NVA had actually achieved.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
GRU After 1991 — Military Intelligence Continuity
GRU (Main Intelligence Directorate) · Khodinka Aerodrome, Moscow · survived Soviet collapse intact; continued as Russian GRU

When the Soviet Union dissolved in December 1991, the KGB was dramatically fragmented: broken into the SVR (foreign intelligence), the FSK/FSB (domestic counterintelligence), the FAPSI (signals intelligence and communications security), the FSO (federal protection service), and several smaller successor agencies. The GRU — the Main Intelligence Directorate of the General Staff — was not. It transferred intact from the Soviet armed forces to the Russian Federation's armed forces, with the same organizational structure, the same headquarters in the building complex near Khodinka Aerodrome known in GRU argot as "the Aquarium," the same directorate designations, and most of the same personnel. The institutional decision to leave the GRU intact was made by the defense ministry leadership — Shaposhnikov as the last Soviet defense minister and then Grachev as the first Russian defense minister — on the grounds that military intelligence was a function of the armed forces rather than of the party-state, and that the transformation of Soviet to Russian military institutions could be managed without disrupting military intelligence continuity. This logic was partly correct and partly institutional self-interest: the military services retained the GRU in part because breaking it up would have reduced military institutional power in the post-Soviet reorganization. The practical consequence was that the GRU's collection networks — the military attaché system, the illegals program, the foreign station infrastructure, the SIGINT collection architecture — remained operational throughout the Soviet collapse, without the organizational disruption that the KGB's fragmentation imposed on foreign intelligence collection. GRU officers who had been running sources in NATO capitals in 1991 were still running the same operations in 1993 under the same directorate structures. The GRU's HUMINT continuity through the transition was among the least-studied aspects of the Soviet-to-Russian intelligence inheritance. It was also, from the perspective of Western counterintelligence services that had to track and assess Russian intelligence collection in the post-Soviet period, among the most consequential. The KGB successor services needed years to rebuild institutional coherence after the disruption of 1991-1993. The GRU did not need to rebuild. It had not been disrupted. The Aquarium building continued to serve its function, under a different national flag, with largely the same officers, pursuing a mission whose fundamental content had not changed. The organization that trained Rudolf Abel, ran the Spetsnaz units in Afghanistan, and managed the Tokyo residency's scientific-technical collection was still operational. It had changed its name from Soviet to Russian. Everything else remained.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
KGB Archive Destruction — The 1991 Shredding
KGB / MGB · Lubyanka and worldwide · August–December 1991

In the weeks following the failed August 1991 coup attempt, the KGB destroyed files. The scale and systematic character of the destruction — shredding machines running continuously in the Lubyanka basements, burning in KGB regional offices, the organized disposal of operational records in rezidenturas worldwide — was documented by Russian investigators, Western intelligence services that monitored Soviet communications during the period, and KGB officers who subsequently described what they had been ordered to do and what they had done. The destruction was not random. It followed a logic of category: agent files were prioritized for destruction because the exposure of KGB informants and recruited sources in the post-Soviet environment posed legal, political, and physical risks to those individuals. Operational files documenting active collection operations were destroyed to protect ongoing intelligence relationships that the SVR and other successor services intended to continue. Files documenting KGB involvement in political crimes, deaths, and specific operations that would be damaging in a democratic legal environment were selectively destroyed. What was preserved — transferred to successor services, retained in archives, or allowed to be examined by the Mitrokhin Commission and subsequent investigators — was a fragment of the total record. Vasili Mitrokhin's systematic note-taking before his 1992 defection produced the most complete surviving account of KGB operational history, precisely because Mitrokhin had spent years in the archive before the destruction and had handcopied files that were subsequently destroyed or inaccessible. The files he buried in milk jugs at his dacha and extracted to Britain with MI6's assistance represented the most significant surviving primary source for KGB Cold War operations. East German Stasi archives had been seized by West German authorities before they could be destroyed, producing the Gauck Authority's preservation of MfS files — a contrast with the KGB's destruction that made East German intelligence history dramatically better documented than Soviet intelligence history after 1991. In the Lubyanka basements in August and September 1991 — the shredding rooms, the incinerators, the officers who had been ordered to prioritize specific file categories for immediate destruction — the Soviet intelligence record was being permanently altered. The people doing the shredding understood what they were destroying. Some of them had been the case officers whose operational files they were feeding into the machines. Others were the archivists who had maintained the files for decades. The institutional knowledge of what was being destroyed was held by the same people whose institutional obligation was to destroy it.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
George Trofimoff — Highest-Ranking American Military Spy
U.S. Army Reserve / KGB / SVR · Nuremberg, West Germany / Vienna · 1969–1994 · arrested 2000

George Trofimoff was a US Army Reserve colonel who worked as a civilian intelligence officer at the Joint Interrogation Center in Nuremberg, Germany, where his position gave him access to classified intelligence documents including assessments of Soviet military capabilities, intelligence reporting from human sources, and material obtained from debriefings of Eastern Bloc refugees and defectors. From 1969 to 1994 — a span of twenty-five years — he provided KGB and later SVR intelligence officers with copies of classified documents. He was the longest-serving American espionage case of the Cold War and the post-Cold War transition period, and he was the highest-ranking American military officer ever convicted of espionage. His recruitment had come through an unlikely channel: a childhood friend named Igor Susemihl, who was a Russian Orthodox Church archimandrite and also an officer working for Soviet intelligence. Trofimoff met with Soviet intelligence officers at locations including Vienna, providing documents that he had copied from the Nuremberg facility over a period when his access gave him a window into American intelligence on Soviet forces. He was arrested in June 2000 after an FBI undercover operation in which he met with an agent posing as a Russian intelligence officer. He was convicted in September 2001 at age 73 and sentenced to life imprisonment. He died in federal custody in September 2014. His case ran from the beginning of the détente era through the Reagan military buildup, the end of the Cold War, and into the post-Soviet decade — illustrating that espionage on behalf of the Soviet state did not necessarily end when the Soviet state did.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Operation GHOST STORIES — SVR Illegals in America
SVR Directorate S / FBI Counterintelligence · United States (New York / New Jersey / Boston / Washington) · ~2000–2010

Operation Ghost Stories was the FBI counterintelligence investigation that monitored a network of SVR deep-cover illegals embedded across the northeastern United States for years before arresting all ten in coordinated operations on June 27, 2010. The network represented the SVR's continuation of the KGB's Directorate S illegals program — officers placed under long-term false identities (stolen identities of deceased Western citizens, in some cases; purpose-built legends in others) with instructions to build cover lives and develop sources with access to American policy, political, and military decision-making. The ten officers who were arrested had lived in the United States for years, in some cases decades: as couples with children in New Jersey suburbs, as real estate professionals in Manhattan, as academic researchers in Boston. Some had mortgages. Their children had grown up American. The operational security failures that allowed the FBI to monitor them for years — including tradecraft errors in covert communications and the identification of one officer through a separate channel — reflected the institutional difficulty of running long-duration cover operations in the age of digital surveillance. Their intelligence product was assessed as limited relative to the investment: the SVR was building a long-term capability, not running high-yield current collection. The network was exchanged for Sergei Skripal and three others in Vienna in July 2010 — the same geometry as every Cold War exchange, on the same neutral European ground, completing the same institutional ritual that the KGB and CIA had performed since the 1950s. The Directorate S illegals program that created them had run continuously from the NKVD's pre-war networks through Rudolf Abel through the Cold War to this network, the most visible demonstration that the SVR regarded itself as the institutional heir to everything the KGB had built.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Wen Ho Lee — The Los Alamos Scientist
Los Alamos National Laboratory / FBI counterintelligence · Los Alamos, New Mexico · 1999–2000

Wen Ho Lee was a Taiwanese-born American nuclear physicist who worked at the Los Alamos National Laboratory for twenty years as a computational hydrodynamics expert involved in nuclear weapons design calculations. In 1999 the Clinton administration indicted him on 59 counts of mishandling nuclear secrets, alleging that he had transferred classified nuclear weapons design data — specifically legacy codes representing the design parameters of US nuclear warheads — to portable storage devices in ways that violated security protocols and potentially placed that data at risk of reaching a foreign power. The Justice Department, FBI, and Congress treated the case as a potential espionage case of historic significance; he was held in solitary confinement for nine months awaiting trial, shackled during exercise, in conditions that a federal judge would later describe as more severe than those applied to accused traitors in comparable cases. The case collapsed: all but one of the 59 counts were dropped, Lee pleaded guilty to the single count of improperly handling restricted data, and the federal judge at sentencing offered a remarkable public apology to Lee, stating that the government had misled the court about his status as a flight risk and that the prolonged pretrial detention had been unjust. The evidence that he had actually transferred nuclear weapons data to a foreign government was never established. His case became a significant civil liberties controversy about racial profiling in national security investigations: the FBI had focused on Lee partly because he was Chinese-American in an institutional environment of concern about Chinese nuclear espionage, and the investigative process reflected the institutional pressure to find a suspect rather than to follow evidence. The actual pathway through which Chinese nuclear weapons data had been obtained — the question that triggered the investigation — was never publicly identified. Lee received $1.6 million from news organizations that had published information about him from government leaks, settling privacy claims. He published a memoir. He looks, in his available photographs, like a physicist in a laboratory hallway: unremarkable, a man who worked on weapons design calculations, who became for a year and a half the center of a national security controversy that never resolved its central question.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
SVR Intelligence and the Yugoslav Wars
SVR (formerly KGB First Chief Directorate) · Belgrade / Sarajevo / Zagreb / Moscow · 1991–1999

As Yugoslavia disintegrated into ethnic wars from 1991 onward, Russian intelligence — transitioning from KGB foreign intelligence to SVR as the Soviet Union collapsed — maintained and deepened its longstanding relationship with Serbian intelligence services, providing a window into Balkan dynamics that served multiple Russian foreign policy interests simultaneously. The Soviet Union had maintained a complex relationship with Yugoslavia since the Tito-Stalin split of 1948, and the KGB had built intelligence relationships in Yugoslavia that coexisted with the officially non-aligned status of the Yugoslav state. As that state fragmented, the SVR's regional residencies tracked the competing Serbian, Croatian, Slovenian, Bosnian, and Kosovar intelligence services that emerged from the wreckage. Russia's foreign policy position — broadly sympathetic to Serbia on historical and Orthodox Christian grounds, opposed to NATO military intervention, and concerned about the precedent of European borders being redrawn by force — aligned naturally with intelligence relationships that gave Moscow insight into Serbian military planning and a channel for occasional influence. The SVR's interest in Yugoslavia was not purely about Serbian sympathy: it was also about the broader strategic question of NATO expansion and European security architecture that the Yugoslav wars would ultimately force the West to address. The operations in Belgrade, Zagreb, and eventually Sarajevo were simultaneously tracking the Yugoslav disintegration and watching the expansion of American military power in Europe — the Bosnia air campaign of 1995, the Kosovo campaign of 1999 — and the erosion of the Soviet-era security architecture that Russia was simultaneously trying to inherit, adapt, and resist. The Balkans in the 1990s were where Soviet-era intelligence relationships met post-Soviet Russian foreign policy anxiety, in a theater that both sides understood to be a preview of the larger European security struggle to come.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Valerie Plame — The Outed CIA Officer
CIA Directorate of Operations · Washington, DC / various NOC postings · outed July 14, 2003

Valerie Plame Wilson was a CIA operations officer who spent nearly two decades working under cover — first as a private citizen under non-official cover, then under State Department official cover — running intelligence operations related to weapons of mass destruction proliferation. On July 14, 2003, syndicated columnist Robert Novak published her identity as a CIA officer in a column discussing her husband Joseph Wilson's public challenge to the Bush administration's use of intelligence about Iraqi uranium purchases from Niger. The leak attributed her CIA affiliation to "two senior administration officials" in what became one of the most significant domestic intelligence controversies of the post-9/11 era. The disclosure of a covert officer's identity is a federal crime under the Intelligence Identities Protection Act. Special Counsel Patrick Fitzgerald's investigation ultimately convicted not the original senior officials responsible for the disclosure but Lewis "Scooter" Libby, Vice President Cheney's chief of staff, of perjury and obstruction of justice for lying to investigators about the leak. Libby's sentence was commuted by President Bush. The original leaker — later identified as Deputy Secretary of State Richard Armitage — was not prosecuted. Plame left the CIA in 2005 and sued several administration officials, unsuccessfully. Her case is significant for two overlapping reasons: it demonstrated the willingness of a presidential administration to use an intelligence officer's identity as an instrument in a domestic political dispute, and it illustrated the operational consequence — the permanent destruction of a career, a cover, and the network of commercial contacts that the cover had enabled over years. She wrote a memoir that became a film. Her cover is permanently burned. The network is gone. The operation is history.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
SVR and China — The Post-Soviet Intelligence Relationship
SVR / MSS (China) · Moscow / Beijing · 1991–present (partnership formalized in 1990s)

The collapse of the Soviet Union and the emergence of the Russian Federation created a significant strategic opportunity: the realignment between Russia and China that developed under Yeltsin and accelerated under Putin transformed a Cold War border tension — the Sino-Soviet split had turned the KGB and Chinese intelligence into adversaries — into a partnership of convenience against perceived Western hegemony, and intelligence sharing was among the institutional expressions of that partnership. The KGB had maintained an extensive presence in China during the brief Sino-Soviet alignment of the 1950s, had watched the relationship collapse into open hostility in the 1960s following the ideological split, and had spent the following two decades running intelligence operations against China as a potential adversary. The SVR's task after 1991 was to reverse this orientation — converting the infrastructure that had been directed at China as an enemy into a cooperative relationship with China as a strategic partner. The convergence was genuine in its underlying structure: both services had immediate intelligence interests in the United States, in NATO, and in the Central Asian states where Soviet withdrawal had created power vacuums; both maintained SIGINT capabilities that could be shared or deconflicted to avoid wasted collection against common targets; and both had strong institutional interests in a Russia-China strategic axis that would require Western intelligence services to divide their attention. The formal cooperation arrangements that emerged from the 1990s partnerships were not publicly disclosed. What was visible was the diplomatic architecture — the Shanghai Cooperation Organization, joint military exercises, energy agreements — beneath which intelligence coordination operated with the institutional logic that had always governed intelligence partnerships: shared adversaries create operational alignment that shared values cannot always sustain.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Brian Regan — The Buried Secrets Spy
U.S. Air Force / National Reconnaissance Office · Washington, DC area / Dulles Airport · arrested August 2001

Brian Regan was a US Air Force master sergeant and later civilian contractor who worked at the National Reconnaissance Office with Top Secret/SCI clearances covering satellite intelligence collection programs, and who from approximately 1999 to 2001 buried tens of thousands of pages of classified documents in parks and forested areas in Virginia and Maryland, intending to retrieve and deliver them to Iraq, China, and Libya in exchange for money and resettlement. He was arrested at Dulles International Airport in August 2001 as he was about to board a flight to Zurich, carrying documents, cash, and handwritten notes addressed to foreign intelligence services. The arrest was routine gate security — in the weeks before September 11, airport security officers were specifically alert for suspicious material — and what they found on Regan was the physical documentation of a spy operation in progress. FBI and intelligence analysts then spent months searching Northern Virginia and Maryland parks for the buried caches, using an NSA-developed system for decoding location coordinates from the handwritten cipher system Regan had invented. The material he had buried was significant: satellite imagery, SIGINT reports, and intelligence assessments that would have provided detailed insight into American collection capabilities to three countries that were subjects of American collection. He was motivated by financial desperation — bankruptcy, significant debts, a financial crisis that had become unbearable on a government salary. He was convicted in 2003 and sentenced to life in prison. His case is notable for the operational eccentricity of his method: burying classified material in parks rather than meeting with foreign intelligence officers, maintaining a self-invented cipher system for coordinates, building toward a single personal delivery trip that September 11 disrupted and Dulles security ended. Patient, elaborate, and ultimately caught by a coincidence of timing that he could not have anticipated.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
SVR Economic Intelligence — The Post-Cold War Espionage Pivot
SVR Directorate ET (Economic Intelligence) · Moscow / Washington / London / Brussels · 1991–present

After the Soviet collapse, the SVR — under pressure to justify its existence and budget in a state whose ideological framework for competing with the West had dissolved — pivoted aggressively toward economic intelligence as a primary collection mission. The shift was both strategic and institutional: strategic because Russian leadership needed economic intelligence to navigate the transition from Soviet planned economy to market integration, to understand Western economic policy toward Russia, and to anticipate sanctions or financial measures before they were imposed; institutional because the SVR needed a mission defensible to a government that could no longer claim Cold War ideological competition as justification for the intelligence apparatus it had inherited. The economic intelligence mission targeted several categories simultaneously: acquisition of Western industrial and commercial technology through intelligence channels (continuing the KGB's Directorate T tradition under a new commercial framing), collection against Western government economic policy discussions that affected Russia, development of sources inside Western financial institutions who could provide advance warning of investment decisions, and penetration of multilateral organizations — the IMF, World Bank, and European Commission — whose policy deliberations directly affected Russian economic conditions. The mission also provided institutional cover: economic attachés and commercial officers gave SVR officers placements in Western capitals that were more defensible to post-Cold War Western governments than the purely political or military intelligence cover of the Soviet era. Western counterintelligence tracked SVR economic collection activities through the 1990s, identifying networks of Russian commercial organizations that served as intelligence fronts. The economic espionage mission proved durable because it was genuinely useful: it provided intelligence that had immediate policy relevance to Moscow while maintaining the institutional presence in Western capitals that other collection missions required. The SVR's economic intelligence directorate became, in the 1990s, the organizational bridge between the KGB's Cold War infrastructure and the Putin-era integrated intelligence model that would follow.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Robert Kim — Navy Intelligence Analyst, Seoul's Asset
ONI / U.S. Office of Naval Intelligence · Arlington, Virginia · arrested September 1996

Robert Chaegon Kim was a naturalized American citizen who had immigrated from South Korea and built a career as a civilian analyst at the Office of Naval Intelligence in Arlington, Virginia. In September 1996 the FBI arrested him at a restaurant in Tysons Corner where he had arranged to meet Brigadier General Kwon Young-hae, his contact at the South Korean Embassy and the recipient of the classified materials he had been providing. He had been passing documents about North Korean military capabilities, submarine operations, and intelligence collection priorities — not to an adversary state in the Cold War sense, but to a treaty ally that operated an independent intelligence service with its own collection priorities and, in some cases, its own channels back to parties the United States would have preferred not to be informed. The distinction mattered legally and operationally: ally espionage sits in a different conceptual category from enemy espionage, raising questions about reciprocal obligations, the nature of the alliance, and the extent to which partners can legitimately be denied information that affects their national security. Kim was convicted in 1997 of conspiracy to commit espionage and sentenced to nine years in federal prison. He served most of his sentence. He told investigators that he had believed he was serving Korea's legitimate security interests and had not fully processed that this constituted a crime against the United States. Brigadier General Kwon was declared persona non grata and returned to South Korea, where he faced no prosecution. The case rarely appears in the canonical Cold War spy literature because it violates the aesthetic of the genre: there is no enemy, no tradecraft drama, no ideological confrontation. There is only an analyst, a general, a restaurant, and the complicated geometry of American alliances.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
SVR Baghdad Station — Intelligence Through the Gulf War
SVR RF / Directorate T; Line PR · Soviet/Russian Embassy, Baghdad · 1991–2003

When the Soviet Union dissolved in December 1991, the KGB's Baghdad residency became an SVR residency without interruption. The physical plant, the personnel, and the collection priorities transferred seamlessly to the successor service. Iraq under Saddam Hussein represented a different strategic relationship for post-Soviet Russia than it had for the Soviet Union — the ideological alignment was gone, the Warsaw Pact framework was gone — but the intelligence value of a Baghdad station remained substantial. The Gulf War of 1991 had demonstrated the full operational capability of American military technology against a Soviet-equipped adversary, and the SVR's Baghdad station had front-row access to the evidence: destroyed T-72s, degraded air defense networks, the performance of Soviet-designed systems against American precision munitions. Russian military planners needed that assessment data. The station also served Russian commercial interests: Russian firms had contracts in Iraq dating from the Soviet period, and the oil-for-food program that the UN administered from 1996 onward created substantial financial flows that Russian companies were positioned to access. SVR collection in Baghdad during the sanctions era provided intelligence useful to both military planning and commercial positioning. The station worked through the UN inspection regime, the sanctions administration, and the diplomatic corps. It also — according to several post-2003 assessments — maintained enough contact with Iraqi intelligence services to develop an understanding of Iraqi capability assessments, including assessments about weapons programs. After the U.S. invasion in 2003, the Russian Embassy in Baghdad closed temporarily and the station's operational continuity was interrupted. It resumed, in a different form, after the establishment of the post-Saddam government. The aesthetic of the Baghdad station — like all SVR operations of the 1990s — is that of an institution managing the transition from superpower to major power while maintaining the institutional forms of the superpower era.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Porter Goss — CIA Officer Turned Overseer
CIA / clandestine service; HPSCI · Florida; Washington, DC · CIA service 1960–1971; DCI 2004–2006

Porter Johnston Goss spent eleven years as a CIA clandestine service officer, operating primarily in Latin America and the Caribbean during the peak years of CIA paramilitary involvement in the region — the period that produced the Bay of Pigs, MONGOOSE, and the various operational programs in Central and South America that the Church Committee later examined. He left the agency in the early 1970s for health reasons, entered Florida local politics, and was elected to Congress from the Sarasota area in 1988. From 1997 to 2004 he chaired the House Permanent Select Committee on Intelligence — the position that made him simultaneously one of the most powerful intelligence overseers in Congress and one of the few members of the oversight apparatus who had sat on the operational side of what he was overseeing. His CIA background shaped his congressional work in ways that cut in both directions: he understood what the agencies were telling the committee in operational and technical terms, which allowed him to ask sharper questions, and he was also culturally sympathetic to operational considerations that might lead a purely civilian overseer to be more skeptical. In August 2004 President Bush nominated him to succeed George Tenet as Director of Central Intelligence; he was confirmed and took office in September. The 2004 intelligence reform legislation created the Director of National Intelligence position above the DCI, which altered his authorities significantly. He resigned in May 2006. The specific circumstances were not officially explained; reporting attributed his departure to friction with other senior intelligence figures and internal personnel disputes. In his HPSCI photographs from the 1990s he looks like what the committee produced in that period: a former intelligence officer who has returned as an overseer and is aware of both roles simultaneously.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Eduard Shevardnadze — The Warning Before the Storm
Soviet MFA / Supreme Soviet · Moscow; worldwide · Foreign Minister 1985–1990

Eduard Amvrosiyevich Shevardnadze served as Soviet Foreign Minister from July 1985, appointed by Gorbachev in the same reformist impulse that brought Gorbachev himself to power. A Georgian Communist Party official with no professional foreign policy background, he nevertheless proved to be one of the more effective diplomatic partners of the late Cold War: his working relationship with James Baker became, in the accounts of both men, a direct personal channel that allowed substantive progress on arms control, German reunification, and the strategic architecture of the post-Cold War order when formal diplomatic channels were insufficient.
