Baker's memoir and Shevardnadze's memoir both describe specific conversations in which they told each other what their governments could actually accept, as distinct from what their governments' official positions were. On December 20, 1990, he appeared before the Fourth Congress of People's Deputies and delivered a resignation speech. He said: "I am resigning. Let this be my protest against the advance of dictatorship." He did not name who would establish it. The congress was surprised. The diplomats in the gallery were surprised. Gorbachev was surprised. The coup attempt came in August 1991 — eight months after his warning. His resignation and the subsequent coup confirmed a pattern: the people who understood the internal dynamics of the Soviet system most clearly said so before it happened, and were not believed. He returned to Georgia after the Soviet collapse, served as Georgian head of state from 1992 to 2003, survived multiple assassination attempts, and died in 2014. In the diplomatic photographs from his Baker years he looks like a man who understands the room he is in and is comfortable there, which is a description that applied to both of them.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Frank Anderson — Near East Division, the Inheritance
CIA / Near East and South Asia Division · Langley, Virginia · Division Chief 1991–1995

Frank Anderson served as chief of the CIA's Near East and South Asia Division from 1991 to 1995, commanding the agency's clandestine human intelligence operations across a region that was simultaneously reorganizing itself after the Cold War's end and producing new threat streams — weapons proliferation, terrorism, state fragmentation, and the management of consequences from the previous decade's covert action programs — that the intelligence community had not yet fully assembled into a unified operational picture. He had run the Afghan operations branch during the final years of the mujahadeen program and the managed drawdown after the Soviet withdrawal. The closure of that program left specific inheritances: Stinger missiles in Afghan faction hands, external intelligence relationships with Pakistan's ISI and with Saudi intelligence that had served the covert action program but whose long-term purposes were now unclear, and a set of armed factions without a war to fight against a common enemy. Anderson understood this inheritance from the inside of the organization that had created it. His division's operational responsibilities from 1991 to 1995 included the Iraqi intelligence landscape after the Gulf War, the Iranian nuclear and ballistic missile programs, the Palestinian-Israeli negotiating process in which CIA played a back-channel facilitation role during the Oslo period, and the continuing deterioration of Afghanistan through civil war. He left the CIA in 1995. In the post-government phase of his career he became a sometimes-public commentator on the longer-term consequences of the programs his division had managed and the choices available to successors. In the limited available photographs from his government years he looks like what the Directorate of Operations produced in the early post-Cold War years: a professional who understands the gap between what was planned and what was inherited.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Yuli Kvitsinsky — The Walk in the Woods
Soviet MFA / INF delegation · Geneva; Bonn · INF negotiator 1981–1983; Ambassador to Germany 1990–1995

Yuli Alexandrovich Kvitsinsky was the Soviet Union's chief negotiator in the Intermediate-Range Nuclear Forces talks in Geneva from 1981 to 1983 — the negotiations over the deployment of American Pershing II missiles and ground-launched cruise missiles in Western Europe, conducted against the backdrop of NATO's dual-track decision of 1979 and the Soviet SS-20 deployments that had precipitated it. On July 16, 1982, he and his American counterpart Paul Nitze took a walk together in the woods above Lake Geneva and conducted, over several hours, an informal conversation about what a compromise agreement might actually look like — bypassing the formal negotiating structure, exploring what each government's real constraints were, and developing a tentative framework that both men believed their capitals might accept. The "walk in the woods" formula called for mutual restraint on intermediate-range missiles in Europe. They were both wrong about their capitals. Washington rejected the formula, primarily because it would have required concessions on the Pershing II's specific accuracy and flight time characteristics that American planners considered unacceptable. Moscow also rejected it. The formal talks resumed and collapsed entirely in November 1983 when NATO began Pershing deployments and the Soviet delegation walked out. Kvitsinsky subsequently served as Soviet and then Russian Ambassador to Germany from 1990 to 1995 — present through German reunification and the first years of the unified German state. His memoir documents the INF negotiations from inside the Soviet delegation. He died in 2010. In the photographs from Geneva he looks exactly like a Soviet diplomat in a forest above a lake who has had a useful conversation and is uncertain whether anyone will be told about it.

UNCLASSIFIED
American
Joseph Fernandez — CIA Costa Rica, the Unwinnable Case
CIA / Costa Rica Station · San José, Costa Rica · Station Chief 1984–1986

Joseph Fernandez served as CIA station chief in Costa Rica from 1984 to 1986, operating under the alias "Tom Castillo," during the period when Oliver North's private Contra resupply network was using Costa Rican territory and airstrips as logistics nodes for flights into Nicaragua. He was directly involved in these operations — working with North, with Richard Secord's network, and with Contra operational elements — at a time when the Boland Amendment had expressly prohibited U.S. government military assistance to the Contras. Independent Counsel Lawrence Walsh indicted him in 1988 on four counts of making false statements to the CIA's Inspector General and obstruction of justice. The prosecution then encountered a structural problem: Fernandez argued that certain classified materials were essential to his defense. The Justice Department, following CIA classification guidance, refused to declassify them. In November 1989, Judge Claude Hilton dismissed all charges — not because Fernandez was innocent, but because the government's classification decisions made a fair trial impossible. The government had, in effect, prosecuted someone it could not try without disclosing information it had decided could not be disclosed. Walsh's final report described this as a significant obstruction of justice by the executive branch and characterized Fernandez's role in the Contra resupply operations as substantial. The case is one of the clearest illustrations in the compendium of a structural property of covert action: the same classification systems that enable these operations to function can, when necessary, function as effective immunity for their participants. Fernandez never faced trial. The information he needed to defend himself remained classified. The charges were dismissed.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Boris Pugo — The Last Interior Minister
Soviet MVD · Moscow · Interior Minister 1990–1991; GKChP member August 19–22, 1991

Boris Karlovich Pugo served as Soviet Interior Minister from 1990 until August 19, 1991, when he was one of the eight members of the State Committee for the State of Emergency — the GKChP — that announced it was assuming power from Mikhail Gorbachev, who they claimed was incapacitated by illness. He had spent his career as a Communist Party official, serving as first secretary of the Latvian Communist Party and then as chief of the Party Control Commission before being appointed Interior Minister. The GKChP controlled, on paper, the institutions required for a successful coup: the Interior Ministry with its internal security troops, the KGB under Kryuchkov, the military under Yazov. They could not make those institutions act decisively. Military commanders refused orders. KGB units were uncertain. Crowds gathered around the Russian White House. The cameras were running. Yeltsin climbed on a tank. Three days after announcing it had taken power, the GKChP had failed. On August 22, 1991, as arrests began, Boris Pugo shot himself at his Moscow apartment. He was 53 years old. His wife was present. He was wounded but survived for several hours before dying. He was the only member of the GKChP to die. The others faced trial; most received suspended sentences or were pardoned. The famous press conference photograph from August 19 — the GKChP members seated at a long table, their hands visibly shaking — includes Pugo at the far end, looking like what the moment required: a man who has made a decision and is already, somewhere in the part of his mind he cannot quite suppress, beginning to understand what comes next.

РАССЕКРЕЧЕНО
Soviet
Anna Chapman — The 2010 Sweep
SVR Directorate S · New York City · arrested June 27, 2010

On June 27, 2010, the FBI arrested ten Russian illegals in coordinated operations across the northeastern United States — the largest sweep of Russian intelligence officers in American history since VENONA. The network, which the FBI had monitored for years under Operation Ghost Stories, had been embedded across suburban New York, New Jersey, Boston, and Washington: couples with children, mortgage holders, some embedded since the mid-1980s, all operating under false identities built from the Directorate S model that Rudolf Abel had made famous in 1957. Anna Chapman was 28 when she was arrested in New York. She had a Russian passport, a Manhattan address, and an internet real estate startup with actual clients. She was not the most operationally significant of the ten — that distinction belonged to officers like the Murphys in New Jersey, who had been embedded for decades. She became by far the most publicly visible: her FBI arrest photographs were striking, her subsequent Russian media career was extensive, and she became, in the Western press, the face of the post-Soviet illegal. The cover at the time of her arrest was not deep: her communications with the Moscow Center had been monitored by the FBI for years. The network was exchanged in July 2010 at Vienna's Schwechat Airport — the same neutral geography, fifty years later, as the exchanges that run throughout this compendium. Chapman and nine others walked in one direction; Sergei Skripal and three others walked in the other. She returned to Russia and became a television celebrity, a model, a government official, and periodically a subject of Western press profiles. She looks, in every post-exchange photograph, exactly like what she became: a woman who was a spy and is entirely comfortable with the fact. The compendium opened with a man who looked like a retired schoolteacher in Brooklyn. It ends with a woman who looks like a television presenter in Moscow. Both worked for the same organization. Both were exactly what they appeared to be, and neither was anything like what they appeared to be. The photographs are the point. They always were.

Further Reading

  • Delta Force — Charles Beckwith (1983)
    Beckwith's own account of founding the unit. Unvarnished.
  • The Master of Disguise — Antonio Mendez (1999)
    Mendez's account of thirty years in CIA's Office of Technical Service. The Argo operation is in here, alongside a career's worth of thinking about how people see and don't see each other.
  • The Quiet Professional — Alan Hoe (1993)
    Biography of Dick Meadows. The best book on what the quiet professional archetype actually looks like up close.
  • The Spy and the Traitor — Ben Macintyre (2018)
    Gordievsky biography. Probably the best single Cold War intelligence narrative written. The diaper-on-the-trunk detail is true.
  • The Main Enemy — Milton Bearden & James Risen (2003)
    CIA vs. KGB from the inside, through the final decade. Bearden's account of the Islamabad years is definitive.
  • Spy Handler — Victor Cherkashin (2005)
    The KGB handler's memoir. Remarkably candid about the Ames and Hanssen recruitments.
  • The Mitrokhin Archive — Christopher Andrew & Vasili Mitrokhin (1999)
    What Mitrokhin buried in his dacha. All of it.
  • Best of Enemies — Gus Russo & Eric Dezenhall (2018)
    The Platt-Vasilenko friendship. The last great spy story of the Cold War, as advertised.
  • My Silent War — Kim Philby (1968)
    Philby's memoir, written in Moscow. Curated for the KGB's preferred narrative and therefore doubly interesting to read for what it doesn't say.
  • 1983: The World at the Brink — Taylor Downing (2018)
    Able Archer 83 and the year the Cold War nearly became hot. The RYAN operation, the exercise, and Gordievsky's role in defusing it.
  • The Farewell Dossier — CIA Studies in Intelligence (1996, declassified)
    Gus Weiss's internal CIA account of the Vetrov operation and the coordinated 1983 expulsions. Available on the CIA FOIA reading room.
  • The Billion Dollar Spy — David Hoffman (2015)
    Definitive account of Polyakov/BOURBON. Uses newly declassified CIA files. The GRU general who asked for nothing.
  • Hunting the Jackal — Billy Waugh & Tim Keown (2004)
    Waugh's memoir. From Son Tay to Khartoum. The morning jogs past Carlos' house are in here.
  • Black Hawk Down — Mark Bowden (1999)
    Mogadishu. The standard.
  • Charlie Wilson's War — George Crile (2003)
    The definitive account of Operation Cyclone — and of Gust Avrakotos, the CIA officer who actually ran it. The bowling shirts are in here.
  • Man Without a Face — Markus Wolf & Anne McElvoy (1997)
    Wolf's own memoir. Revealing about method and motivation, appropriately guarded about sources. The Romeo operations are discussed in general terms.
  • No Room for Error — Col. John T. Carney Jr. & Benjamin F. Schemmer (2002)
    The Combat Control story: Desert One, Grenada, Panama, and beyond. Carney's account of his night alone in the Iranian desert is in Chapter 3.

Photography Notes

Image slots are presently unsourced. The following verified public-domain photographs are recommended for each entry. All linked files are either U.S. government works (17 U.S.C. § 105, no copyright) or are marked public domain on Wikimedia Commons.


All images are placeholders. Verified public-domain sources are noted below. Images marked PD-USGov are works of the U.S. federal government (17 U.S.C. § 105). Save to operators/src/ with the filename indicated.

Abel (abel.jpg): FBI hollow nickel case artifact page — fbi.gov/history/artifacts/rudolph-abel-hollow-nickel-case · PD-USGov

Beckwith (beckwith.jpg): U.S. Army photo — commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Charles_Beckwith.jpg · PD-USGov

Meadows (meadows.jpg): U.S. Army — en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Richard_Meadows_soldier.jpg · verify license before use

Hanssen (hanssen.jpg): FBI official photo — fbi.gov/image-repository/hanssen.jpeg · PD-USGov

Ames (ames.jpg): FBI arrest photo — fbi.gov/image-repository/history/ames-arrest.jpeg · PD-USGov

Gordievsky (gordievsky.jpg): NARA, Reagan meeting, July 21, 1987 — catalog.archives.gov item 75855593 · PD-USGov

160th SOAR, Acid Gambit, Mogadishu, CIA Ground Branch, Delta C Squadron, Task Force Ranger: Search the Defense Visual Information Distribution Service at dvidshub.net — all DVIDS photos are public domain. Also search catalog.archives.gov for "special operations" and "JSOC" within Record Group 330. For Just Cause specifically, NARA holds significant photographic records in RG 319 and RG 554.

Mendez (mendez.jpg): CIA official retirement photo — released via CIA Museum and FOIA, widely reproduced · PD-USGov

Casey (casey.jpg): CIA official portrait, 1981 — cia.gov/about/history/directors/william-j-casey · PD-USGov

Howard (howard.jpg): FBI wanted poster photo, 1985 — released publicly by FBI · PD-USGov

Earnest Will (earnest-will.jpg): NARA/DOD, Operation Prime Chance photography, RG 330, 1987–88 · PD-USGov

Garrison (garrison.jpg): DVIDS or NARA, JSOC Somalia period, 1993 · PD-USGov

Avrakotos (avrakotos.jpg): CIA career portrait released in conjunction with George Crile's research for Charlie Wilson's War; widely reproduced in documentary context · likely PD-USGov, verify before use

Wolf (wolf.jpg): The 1978 Stockholm photograph circulated widely in Western press after Wolf's identity was established; reproduced in German press and BfV publications. Post-1990 press photographs of Wolf (at his trial, at book events) are clearly dated and credited in wire service archives. Wolf's East German Stasi personnel photographs are held at BStU (Bundesbeauftragter für die Unterlagen des Staatssicherheitsdienstes), now the BArch — some have been released to press.

Cohen / Kroger (kroger.jpg): Scotland Yard booking photographs from January 1961 arrest have been released in British press coverage and parliamentary materials. UK National Archives (Kew) holds MI5 files on the Portland Spy Ring (KV 2 series) — some photographs in these files were declassified. Original Soviet photographs of the Cohens may be in SVR historical holdings.

Carney (carney.jpg): USAF official portrait photographs are PD-USGov. DVIDS (dvidshub.net) search for "Combat Control" or "Carney." For Eagle Claw/Desert One imagery: NARA RG 330, also Air Force Historical Research Agency (AFHRA) at Maxwell AFB holds Eagle Claw photographic record.

North (north.jpg): North's 1987 congressional testimony was photographed extensively; all Congressional photographs from committee sessions are in the public domain as records of Congress. USMC official portraits (pre-dismissal) are PD-USGov. FBI/DOJ records from the Iran-Contra investigation released under FOIA include some imagery.

KGB/Soviet entries: Most Soviet intelligence officer photos from this period remain restricted or unverified. The CIA FOIA Reading Room (cia.gov/readingroom) has released some material on major cases. Putin/Dresden: East German Stasi personal identification photographs from the Dresden KGB residency have been reported in German press; originals held at BStU (Federal Commissioner for the Stasi Records). Vetrov: KGB personnel photograph published in French press coverage of the FAREWELL case. Alpha Group 1991: FSB official history releases contain some Alpha Group photographs from this period. Soviet military photographs taken before 1978 are generally public domain in the United States; later photographs are more complex.
